1

Christology

The Person and Work of Jesus Christ

PREFACE

Christology is central to Christian faith and theology. An accurate understanding of Jesus Christ determines the accuracy of our worship, doctrine, preaching, and Christian living. This booklet is prepared as an academic and ministry resource for students of Fusion Church Bible College. The content follows a systematic theological approach and is aligned with Bible college–level syllabus requirements. Scripture is the final authority throughout this course.

COURSE OUTLINE

Christology

Christology is the branch of systematic theology that studies the person, nature, offices, and work of Jesus Christ. The term comes from two Greek words: Christos (Χριστός, meaning “Anointed One” or “Messiah”) and logos (λόγος, meaning “study,” “word,” or “doctrine”). Thus, Christology literally means “the study of Christ.”

In Christian theology, Christology seeks to answer the central question Jesus Himself asked:

1.“Who do people say the Son of Man is?”

2.“Who do you say that I am?” (Matthew 16:15)

Christology is not built on human philosophy or church tradition alone, but on divine revelation through Scripture. Both the Old Testament (through prophecy, types, and promises) and the New Testament (through fulfillment, teaching, and apostolic witness) testify to Christ.

Christology is the systematic, biblical, and theological study of the person and work of Jesus Christ, focusing on His divine and human natures, His redemptive mission, His offices, and His present and future ministry, as revealed in Scripture.

1.Who is Jesus Christ? 2.who do people say the son of man is?

who do you say the son of man is?[This has more significance]

KEY CHRISTOLOGICAL TEXTS

John 1:1-18,Ephesians 1:3-10,Php 2:5-11,Colossians 1:15-22,Colossians 2:9-15,Hebrews 1:1-9.

A.The Deity Of Jesus Christ

1.His Pre-existence

The pre-existence of Christ means that Jesus existed before His birth at Bethlehem. He did not begin His life on earth but entered the world through incarnation, taking on human nature.
John 1:1–2; John 8:58; Colossians 1:16–17; Micah 5:2
Christ is eternal, uncreated, and divine. His incarnation means God became man, not man became God.John 17:5 – “Glorify Me with the glory I had with You before the world was.”

2.His Eternality

The eternality of Jesus Christ means that He has no beginning and no end. Jesus did not come into existence at Bethlehem; rather, He existed eternally before His incarnation. As the eternal Son of God, He shares the same eternal nature as the Father and the Holy Spirit.

Scripture affirms that Jesus existed before time, creation, and history. He is not a created being but the Creator Himself, existing from eternity past and continuing into eternity future.

The Bible declares that Christ is unchanging and everlasting, the same yesterday, today, and forever. His eternality confirms His full deity, showing that He is truly God and worthy of worship, trust, and obedience.

Key Scriptures:

John 1:1–2 – “In the beginning was the Word…”

John 8:58 – “Before Abraham was, I AM”

Colossians 1:16–17 – “He is before all things”

Hebrews 13:8 – “Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever

3.His divine nature[God]

Jesus is fully divine

Jesus Is Fully Divine (Short Note)

Jesus Christ is fully divine, possessing the complete nature and attributes of God. He is not partially God or a lesser deity; He is true God, equal with the Father and the Holy Spirit. The Bible clearly affirms that Jesus shares God’s essence, authority, glory, and power.

Scripture declares that Jesus existed from eternity, created all things, forgives sins, receives worship, and bears the divine names and titles of God. His divine works and claims reveal that He is one with the Father, yet distinct in person.

The full divinity of Christ is essential to salvation, because only God Himself could provide a perfect and sufficient redemption for humanity.

John 1:1 – “The Word was God”, John 10:30 – “I and the Father are one”

Colossians 2:9 – “In Him dwells all the fullness of the Godhead bodily”

Hebrews 1:8 – “Your throne, O God, is forever and ever”,Titus 2:13 – “Our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ”

Jesus exhibited divine attributes which belong only to God

1. Omnipresence of Jesus(Jesus is present everywhere)

Omnipresence means being present at all places at the same time this is an attribute of God alone Jesus demonstrated omnipresence through His words and promises

Matthew 18:20 – “Where two or three are gathered in My name, I am there”,Matthew 28:20 – “I am with you always” ,John 1:48 – Jesus saw Nathanael before meeting him, John 3:13 – Jesus speaks of being in heaven while on earth
Jesus’ presence is not limited by place, proving His divine nature.

2. Omniscience of Jesus(Jesus knows all things)

Omniscience means having complete and perfect knowledge Jesus knew thoughts, hearts, and future events

John 2:24–25 – Jesus knew what was in man,John 16:30 – “Now we know that You know all things”, Mark 2:8 – Jesus knew the thoughts of the scribes,John 4:17–19 – Jesus knew the Samaritan woman’s past,Jesus’ complete knowledge confirms His deity.

3. Omnipotence of Jesus (Jesus is all-powerful)

Omnipotence means having unlimited power Jesus demonstrated power over nature, sickness, demons, and death

Matthew 8:26–27 – Jesus calmed the storm,Luke 4:40 – He healed all who were sick,John 11:43–44 – Jesus raised Lazarus from the dead,Matthew 28:18 – “All authority has been given to Me”Only God has absolute power; Jesus exercises divine authority.

4. Immutability of Jesus (Jesus does not change)

Immutability means being unchanging in nature, character, and purposeJesus remains the same forever Hebrews 13:8 – “Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever”Malachi 3:6 (applied to deity) – “I do not change” Revelation 1:8 – “The Alpha and the Omega”Jesus’ unchanging nature shows His divine identity.

5. Life of Jesus(Jesus as the Source and Giver of Life)

Jesus is not only alive eternally but is the source of life Eternal life comes through HimBible References:John 1:4 – “In Him was life”John 11:25 – “I am the resurrection and the life” John 14:6 – “I am the way, the truth, and the life”John 5:26 – The Son has life in Himself 1 John 5:11–12 – Life is found in the Son
Jesus gives both physical resurrection and eternal life.

4..His names & titles

Jesus Meaning:“The Lord saves”
From the Hebrew Yeshua. It reveals His mission—to save people from sin.
Matthew 1:21

Immanuel Meaning: “God with us”
Declares that God came to live among humanity through Jesus.
Isaiah 7:14, Matthew 1:23

Christ Meaning: “The Anointed One” (Greek for Messiah)
Jesus is the chosen King, Priest, and Prophet sent by God.John 1:41

Yahweh Meaning: “I AM” / The Self-Existing One
God’s personal covenant name in the Old Testament.
Jesus identifies Himself with “I AM”, showing His divinity. Exodus 3:14, John 8:58

Lord Meaning: Master, Ruler, Supreme Authority
Declares Jesus’ authority over all creation and our lives.Philippians 2:9–11

God Meaning: Supreme Divine Being
Jesus is fully God, not merely a prophet or teacher.
John 1:1, John 20:28

Son of God Meaning: Divine Son, same nature as the Father
Affirms Jesus’ deity and unique relationship with God the Father.
Matthew 3:17, John 3:16

Son of Man Meaning: The Human One / The Promised Heavenly Figure
Emphasizes Jesus’ humanity and His role as the suffering Savior and coming Judge. Daniel 7:13–14, Mark 10:45

Son of David Meaning: Heir to King David’s throne
Confirms Jesus as the promised Messianic King.
2 Samuel 7:12–13, Matthew 1:1

Divine Titles

Title Meaning / Significance
Emmanuel Meaning "God with us" (Matthew 1:23), affirming the incarnation and God's presence among His people.
Christ Derived from the Greek Christos, translating the Hebrew word for Messiah of God — the Anointed One.
Yahweh Jesus said He was the same as "I AM" (Exodus 3:14; John 8:58).
Lord New Testament equivalent of the Old Testament Adonai (2 Peter 1:11).
God New Testament equivalent of the Old Testament Elohim (2 Peter 1:1).
Son of God Signifies His divine nature, being one with the Father and the Holy Spirit in the Holy Trinity.
Son of Man Identifies Him as both a real man and a perfect man (Matthew 19:28; Daniel 7:13–14).
Son of David Relates Jesus specifically to the right to sit on the everlasting throne (2 Samuel 7:11–16; Luke 1:26–33).
King of Kings Emphasises His supreme authority and sovereignty over all rulers, kingdoms, and all creation; His eternal dominion is unmatched (Revelation 19:16).
Alpha and Omega Jesus is the beginning and the end, the eternal God who was, is, and always will be.
Lamb of God Highlights Jesus as the ultimate sacrifice for humanity's sins, symbolizing His selfless act of redemption on the cross (John 1:29).

5. His designations

“One and Only Son” (Only-Begotten)

Bible term: Monogenēs (Greek) Meaning: Unique, one-of-a-kind Son
Not created, but eternally from the Father

It speaks about Jesus’ unique relationship and nature, not about origin in time.

John 1:14, John 3:16, John 1:18

Jesus is the only Son who shares the same divine nature as the Father.
Believers become children of God by adoption, but Jesus is Son by nature.

“Firstborn” : Bible term: Prōtotokos (Greek)Meaning: Supreme in rank, authority, and inheritance
Not “first created”In the Bible, firstborn often means pre-eminence, not chronology
(e.g., David was called firstborn though he was the youngest – Psalm 89:27).Jesus is not created.He is the eternal Son, unique in nature, and supreme over all creation.

Colossians 1:15–18, Romans 8:29, Hebrews 1:6

Jesus is above all creation, ruler and heir of everything,
and the first to rise with a glorified resurrection, guaranteeing ours.

6. His pre-incarnate Old Testament appearances

The Angel of the LORD (Angel of Jehovah)

Meaning:
The Angel of the LORD refers to Jesus Christ appearing in the Old Testament before His incarnation (before being born as a man).

The word angel (Hebrew: malʾāk) means “messenger,” not a created angelic being by default.

Why this is understood as Christ (a Christophany )

The Angel of the LORD does things only God can do:

Speaks as God ,Bears God’s name,Accepts worship,Forgives sins,Reveals God’s presence,Yet He is distinct from the Father, showing plurality within the Godhead.

Who Was the Angel of the Lord? Several facts have led many scholars to identify “the angel of the Lord” with Christ: The use of the definite article (“the” angel): appearances of other divine messengers normally do not include the article (Luke 2:9 and Acts 12:7).The angel of the Lord is equated with God and worshiped as God (Exodus 3:1–5).The angel of the Lord never appears after Jesus is born. This should not lead us to conclude that Jesus is an angel or any other kind of created being. The word “angel” is simply “messenger.” These were pre-incarnate appearances of God the Son.

Christ claimed to have existed prior to Abraham.John 8:58 – Jesus said to them, “Most assuredly, I say to you, before Abraham was, I AM.”Jesus here clearly claims to be the “I AM” of Exodus 3:14, i.e., Yahweh, the God of the Israelites. Look at the next few verses to see how the Jews responded to this — they understood that he was equating himself with God, and they wanted to stone him for it.

7. His Prefiguring in the Old Testament (Types & Shadows of Christ)

Christ Prefigured in the Old Testament & Fulfillment in NT

Prophetic Foreshadowing OT Prophecy. Shadow Fulfillment in Christ (NT)

Seed of the woman (Genesis 3:15) Christ destroys Satan

(Galatians 4:4;1 John 3:8)

Born of a virgin (Isaiah 7:14) Virgin birth of Jesus (Matthew 1:23)

Suffering Servant (Isaiah 53) Jesus’ death and resurrection (1Pe 2:24)

King from David’s line (2 Sam 7:12–13). Jesus the eternal King (Luke 1:32–33)

Examples of Prefiguring in the Old Testament

Adam and Christ: The Apostle Paul refers to Adam as a "type of the one who was to come" (Romans 5:14). While Adam's disobedience brought sin and death, Christ's obedience brings righteousness and life, making Jesus the "second Adam".

The Passover Lamb: The sacrifice of a lamb during the Exodus, whose blood protected the Israelites from the angel of death, prefigured Christ as the ultimate "Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world" (John 1:29).

The Bronze Serpent: In the wilderness, Moses lifted up a bronze serpent so that those bitten by venomous snakes would be healed by looking at it (Numbers 21). Jesus explicitly references this, saying the Son of Man must be lifted up in the same way, so that all who believe in him might have eternal life (John 3:14-15).

The Sacrificial System: The entire system of animal sacrifices, the tabernacle, and the priesthood in the Old Testament prefigured Christ's once-for-all sacrifice on the cross and his role as the eternal High Priest who mediates a new covenant (Hebrews 8-10).

Old Testament Type OT Reference How it Prefigures Christ New Testament Fulfillment
Adam Genesis 1–3 Head of humanity Last Adam, giver of life (1 Cor 15:45)
Noah’s Ark Genesis 6–7 Salvation from judgment Christ saves from judgment (1 Pet 3:20–21)
Isaac Genesis 22 Beloved son offered by father Christ offered by the Father (John 3:16)
Joseph Genesis 37–50 Rejected, suffered, exalted Christ rejected then glorified (Phil 2:6–11)
Passover Lamb Exodus 12 Blood saved from death Christ our Passover Lamb (1 Cor 5:7)
Manna Exodus 16 Bread from heaven Bread of Life (John 6:32–35)
Rock in Wilderness Exodus 17:6 Life-giving water Christ the spiritual Rock (1 Cor 10:4)
Tabernacle Exodus 25–40 God dwelling with His people God dwelt among us in Christ (John 1:14)
Sacrificial System Leviticus Temporary covering of sin Once-for-all sacrifice (Heb 9–10)
High Priest Leviticus 16 Mediator for the people Christ our Great High Priest (Heb 4:14–16)
Moses Exodus–Deuteronomy Deliverer & mediator Greater Moses, mediator of new covenant (Heb 3:1–6)
Bronze Serpent Numbers 21:8–9 Look and live Christ lifted on the cross (John 3:14–15)
Kinsman-Redeemer (Boaz) Ruth 4 Redeems at a cost Christ our Redeemer (Eph 1:7)
David 1 Samuel Shepherd-King Good Shepherd & eternal King (John 10:11)

8. His Fulfillment of Old Testament Messianic Prophecies

The Old Testament is not merely history or law; it is a progressive revelation pointing toward a coming Messiah. From Genesis to Malachi, God revealed specific promises concerning the Messiah’s birth, lineage, character, ministry, suffering, death, and glory. Jesus Christ fulfills these prophecies with remarkable precision, demonstrating that He is God’s promised Redeemer.

Prophecies Concerning His Birth

The Messiah was foretold to be born in a very specific way and place.

Virgin Birth
Isaiah 7:14 prophesied that a virgin would conceive and bear a son called Immanuel.
This was fulfilled in Jesus’ birth to Mary through the power of the Holy Spirit (Matthew 1:22–23).

Place of Birth – Bethlehem
Micah 5:2 foretold that the ruler of Israel would come from Bethlehem.
Jesus was born in Bethlehem, though raised in Nazareth (Matthew 2:1–6).

These fulfillments show that Jesus’ birth was divinely orchestrated, not accidental.

Prophecies Concerning His Lineage

The Messiah was promised to come through a specific family line.

Seed of Abraham (Genesis 12:3; 22:18)
Jesus came through Abraham, bringing blessing to all nations (Matthew 1:1; Galatians 3:16).

From the Tribe of Judah (Genesis 49:10)
Jesus belongs to the royal tribe of Judah (Luke 3:33).

Heir to David’s Throne (2 Samuel 7:12–16; Isaiah 9:6–7)
Jesus is the Son of David and rightful King (Luke 1:32–33).

This confirms Jesus as the legitimate Messianic King.

Prophecies Concerning His Nature and Ministry

The Messiah’s character and work were also foretold.

Anointed by the Spirit
Isaiah 61:1–2 speaks of the Spirit-anointed servant.
Jesus publicly applied this prophecy to Himself (Luke 4:17–21).

Ministry of Teaching, Healing, and Compassion
Isaiah 35:5–6 predicted the blind seeing and the lame walking.
Jesus fulfilled this through His miracles (Matthew 11:4–5).

Light to the Gentiles
Isaiah 9:1–2 foretold light shining in Galilee.
Jesus’ ministry began there (Matthew 4:13–16).

Jesus fulfilled these prophecies not only in word but in power and action.

Prophecies Concerning His Rejection

The Old Testament also foretold that the Messiah would be rejected.

Rejected by His Own People
Isaiah 53:3 says He would be despised and rejected.
Jesus was rejected by religious leaders and many in Israel (John 1:11).

Betrayed by a Close Friend
Psalm 41:9 foretells betrayal by a companion.
Judas fulfilled this prophecy (Matthew 26:47–50).

This reveals that rejection was part of God’s redemptive plan, not a failure.

Prophecies Concerning His Suffering and Death

The suffering of the Messiah is described in striking detail.

The Suffering Servant
Isaiah 53 vividly describes His substitutionary suffering for sin.
Jesus fulfilled this through His crucifixion (1 Peter 2:24).

Pierced Hands and Feet
Psalm 22:16–18 describes crucifixion centuries before it was practiced.
This was fulfilled at the cross (John 19:23–24).

Silent Before His Accusers
Isaiah 53:7 foretold His silence.
Jesus remained silent before Pilate and Herod (Matthew 27:12–14).

These fulfillments confirm that Jesus died as the sin-bearing Lamb.

Prophecies Concerning His Resurrection and Exaltation

The Messiah’s victory over death was also foretold.

Resurrection
Psalm 16:10 declares that God’s Holy One would not see decay.
Peter applies this directly to Jesus’ resurrection (Acts 2:24–31).

Ascension and Glory
Psalm 110:1 speaks of the Messiah seated at God’s right hand.
Jesus fulfills this through His ascension and reign (Hebrews 1:3).

The resurrection proves that Jesus is Lord, not merely a martyr.

Significance of Fulfilled Prophecy

The fulfillment of Messianic prophecy shows that:

Jesus is the promised Messiah

Scripture is divinely inspired and trustworthy

God is faithful to His promises

Salvation was planned from eternity

Jesus Himself said, “These are the Scriptures that testify about Me” (John 5:39).

Prophecies of Jesus’ Future Work

Second Coming:
Jesus Christ will return personally, visibly, and gloriously to complete God’s redemptive plan (Daniel 7:13–14; Matthew 24:30; Acts 1:11).

Righteous Judge:
Christ will judge the living and the dead with perfect justice and righteousness (Isaiah 11:3–4; John 5:22; Revelation 20:11–15).

Eternal King:
Jesus will establish an everlasting kingdom marked by peace, justice, and righteousness (Isaiah 9:6–7; Daniel 2:44; Revelation 11:15).

Restoration of Israel and Nations:
Israel will recognize Jesus as Messiah, and all nations will experience blessing under His reign (Zechariah 12:10; Romans 11:26; Isaiah 2:2–4).

Final Victory Over Evil:
Christ will defeat Satan, sin, and death forever, bringing complete victory (Genesis 3:15; Isaiah 25:8; Revelation 20:1–10).

New Heavens and New Earth:
Jesus will usher in the eternal state, where God dwells with His people forever (Isaiah 65:17; 2 Peter 3:13; Revelation 21–22)

Messianic Prophecies Fulfilled in Christ

Messianic Prophecy Prophecy OT Reference Fulfillment in Jesus (NT)
Seed of the woman Messiah will defeat Satan Genesis 3:15 Galatians 4:4; Hebrews 2:14
Born of a virgin Virgin will conceive Isaiah 7:14 Matthew 1:22–23
Born in Bethlehem Ruler from Bethlehem Micah 5:2 Matthew 2:1–6
Called Immanuel God with us Isaiah 7:14 Matthew 1:23
Descendant of Abraham Blessing to all nations Genesis 12:3 Matthew 1:1; Galatians 3:16
From tribe of Judah King from Judah Genesis 49:10 Hebrews 7:14
Son of David Everlasting kingdom 2 Samuel 7:12–13 Luke 1:32–33
Anointed by the Spirit Spirit upon Messiah Isaiah 61:1 Luke 4:18–21
Ministry of healing Blind see, lame walk Isaiah 35:5–6 Matthew 11:4–5
Light to the Gentiles Salvation for all nations Isaiah 42:6; 49:6 Luke 2:30–32; Acts 13:47
Triumphal entry King riding on a donkey Zechariah 9:9 Matthew 21:4–5
Rejected by His own Despised and rejected Isaiah 53:3 John 1:11
Betrayed for 30 pieces Price of betrayal Zechariah 11:12–13 Matthew 26:14–15; 27:9–10

Ascension & exaltation Seated at God’s right hand Psalm 110:1 Hebrews 1:3
Eternal King Eternal dominion Daniel 7:13–14 Matthew 28:18; Revelation 11:15

B. The Humanity of Christ

The humanity of Christ is a central doctrine of Christian theology. The Bible clearly teaches that Jesus Christ, while remaining fully divine, became fully human. His humanity was real, complete, and essential for the fulfillment of God’s plan of salvation. By becoming human, Christ was able to identify with mankind, represent humanity before God, and accomplish redemption.

The incarnation refers to the act by which the eternal Son of God took upon Himself human flesh. “The Word became flesh and dwelt among us” (John 1:14). In the incarnation, Jesus did not stop being God; rather, He added a human nature to His divine nature. Born at a specific time in history, He entered the human family and lived among people, experiencing life as a man while remaining the eternal Son of God (Galatians 4:4).

John1:1-3,Ps 2:4-12.

Why Was It Necessary for Jesus to Become Man?

It was necessary for Jesus Christ to become man in order to accomplish God’s plan of salvation. Only by taking on true humanity could He fully represent mankind, obey God’s law, and offer Himself as a perfect sacrifice for sin.

1. To Reveal God to Humanity
As a man, Jesus made the invisible God known in a way humans could understand. In His life, words, and actions, God was revealed personally and perfectly (John 1:18; Hebrews 1:1–3).

2. To Identify with Humanity
By becoming human, Jesus shared in human experiences such as hunger, pain, sorrow, and temptation. This enables Him to sympathize with human weakness (Hebrews 2:17–18; 4:15).

3. To Fulfill the Law Perfectly
Only a true man could live in complete obedience to God’s law on behalf of humanity. Jesus obeyed where Adam and all others failed (Matthew 5:17; Romans 5:18–19).

4. To Die as a Substitute for Sin
God cannot die, but man can. By becoming man, Jesus could die in the place of sinners, offering a substitutionary sacrifice acceptable to God (Hebrews 2:14; 1 Peter 2:24).

Virgin birth

Virgin birthClosely connected to the incarnation is the virgin birth. Jesus was conceived by the Holy Spirit and born of the virgin Mary. This miraculous birth fulfilled Old Testament prophecy (Isaiah 7:14) and ensured that Christ was free from inherited sin. Though born of a woman, His origin was divine, affirming both His true humanity and His sinless nature (Matthew 1:18–25; Luke 1:34–35).

Why Did God Choose the Virgin Birth to Make Jesus Man?

God chose the virgin birth as the means for Jesus Christ to become man in order to preserve both His full humanity and full deity, while accomplishing salvation according to God’s perfect plan.

1. To Unite Full Deity and Full Humanity
Through the virgin birth, Jesus was truly human (born of a woman) and truly divine (conceived by the Holy Spirit). This ensured He was not merely a man nor less than God (Luke 1:35; Galatians 4:4).

2. To Prevent the Transmission of Sin
Humanity inherits a sinful nature from Adam. By being conceived by the Holy Spirit and not through a human father, Jesus was free from original sin and remained sinless (Romans 5:12; Hebrews 4:15).

3. To Fulfill Old Testament Prophecy
The virgin birth fulfilled God’s promise in Isaiah 7:14, confirming that Jesus is the promised Messiah and that salvation is God’s work, not human effort.

4. To Show Salvation Is from God Alone
The virgin birth highlights divine initiative. Human effort did not produce the Savior—God did. This emphasizes grace, not works (Luke 1:37; Ephesians 2:8).

5. To Establish Jesus as the Second Adam
Like Adam, Jesus entered the world by a direct act of God. Unlike Adam, He obeyed perfectly and became the head of a new redeemed humanity (1 Corinthians 15:45).

God chose the virgin birth so that Jesus could be sinless, divine, human, prophetic, and redemptive—the only Savior capable of reconciling humanity to God.

Accounts of the Virgin Birth (Biblical Records)

The virgin birth of Jesus Christ is recorded clearly in the New Testament through two primary historical accounts, each emphasizing different aspects of the event.

1. Gospel of Matthew (Matthew 1:18–25)

Written mainly from Joseph’s perspective.

Mary is found to be with child through the Holy Spirit before marriage.

An angel appears to Joseph in a dream, explaining the divine origin of the child.

Confirms fulfillment of Isaiah 7:14 (“A virgin shall conceive”).

Emphasizes Jesus as Messiah and King in David’s line.

Key emphasis: Fulfillment of prophecy and Jesus’ legal right to David’s throne.

2. Gospel of Luke (Luke 1:26–38; 2:1–7)

Written mainly from Mary’s perspective.

Angel Gabriel announces to Mary that she will conceive by the Holy Spirit.

Mary’s question (“How can this be?”) highlights her virginity.

Stresses God’s power and grace in the miracle.

Key emphasis: Jesus’ true humanity and divine conception.

Supporting References

Isaiah 7:14 – Prophetic promise of a virgin-born child.

Galatians 4:4 – “Born of a woman,” affirming real humanity.

Luke 1:35 – The Holy Spirit’s role in conception, ensuring holiness.

Summary

The virgin birth is attested by two independent Gospel witnesses, fulfills Old Testament prophecy, and safeguards the truth that Jesus is fully God and fully man, born without sin by God’s direct action.

Kenosis

Kenosis, The self-emptying of Christ, known as the kenosis, describes His voluntary humility. According to Philippians 2:6–8, Christ did not empty Himself of His deity but laid aside the independent exercise of His divine privileges. He chose to live as a servant, fully dependent upon the Father and empowered by the Holy Spirit, demonstrating perfect obedience even unto death.

1. Eternal Deity → Chosen Humility

Philippians 2:6

“Being in very nature God…”

John 1:1 – “The Word was God.”

Colossians 1:15–17 – He existed before all things.

Hebrews 1:3 – He is the radiance of God’s glory.

Kenosis begins with Christ’s full deity. Only one who truly possessed divine equality could choose humility. His self-emptying was not from weakness but from sovereign love.

2. Not Clinging to Divine Privilege voluntarily gave up the right to use some divine prerogatives and His divine position for a time

Philippians 2:6

“Did not consider equality with God something to be used to his own advantage…”

Matthew 26:53 – Jesus could call twelve legions of angels but chose not to.

John 18:36 – “My kingdom is not of this world.”

John 5:19 – The Son does only what He sees the Father doing.


Christ willingly restrained the independent exercise of His divine rights. He did not stop being God; He chose not to act for self-exaltation.

3. Self-Emptying by Taking Humanity

Philippians 2:7

“He made himself nothing, taking the form of a servant…”John 1:14 – “The Word became flesh.”

2 Corinthians 8:9 – Though rich, He became poor.

Romans 8:3 – Sent in the likeness of sinful flesh.

The “emptying” happened by addition, not subtraction. Christ emptied Himself by taking human nature and the role of a servant, not by losing deity.

4. True Human Dependence

Philippians 2:7

“Being made in human likeness…”

Luke 2:52 – He grew in wisdom and stature.

Matthew 4:2 – He experienced hunger.

John 5:30 – “I can do nothing by myself.”

Kenosis emphasizes real humanity. Jesus lived within human limitations, fully dependent on the Father and empowered by the Holy Spirit.

5. Obedience unto Death

Philippians 2:8

“He humbled himself by becoming obedient to death—even death on a cross.”

Isaiah 53:5–7 – The suffering servant who submits silently.

Hebrews 5:8 – He learned obedience through suffering.

Matthew 26:39 – “Not my will, but yours be done.”

John 10:18 – He laid down His life voluntarily.

The climax of kenosis is the cross. Christ’s humility reached its deepest point in obedient, sacrificial death for sinners.

Isaiah 9:6 — The Servant with Divine Authority

“For unto us a child is born, unto us a son is given…”

Although this verse highlights Christ’s divine titles—Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace—it also emphasizes that He is given. The Son is God’s gift to humanity. His coming as a child points to humility and willingness to enter human history, laying the foundation for His servant role.

Servant emphasis: The divine Son is sent and given for others.

Hebrews 1:1–3 — The Servant Who Reveals and Redeems

“…in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son…”

Jesus is the final and perfect revelation of God. Though He is the radiance of God’s glory and sustainer of all things, He made purification for sins. This act required humble obedience and self-sacrifice.

Servant emphasis: The exalted Son serves by accomplishing redemption through sacrifice.

Hebrews 12:2 — The Servant Who Endured the Cross

“…who for the joy set before him endured the cross, despising the shame…”

Jesus willingly endured suffering and shame for the sake of God’s redemptive purpose. His endurance reveals the heart of a servant—obedience despite suffering, focused on fulfilling the Father’s will.

Servant emphasis: Faithful obedience through suffering unto victory.

Summary

Together, these passages show that Jesus’ servanthood does not contradict His deity. Rather, His greatness is revealed through humble service—being given by the Father, redeeming through sacrifice, and enduring the cross for God’s glory and human salvation

Other Important Considerations about Kenosis

Kenosis is a rich and carefully balanced doctrine. Beyond its basic meaning (self-emptying), Scripture and theology highlight several important considerations that guard against misunderstanding.

1. Kenosis Does NOT Mean Loss of Deity

Jesus did not cease to be God at any point. His divine nature remained unchanged.

John 1:1, 14 – The Word was God and became flesh

Colossians 2:9 – All the fullness of deity dwells in Him bodily

Kenosis is functional humility, not ontological reduction.

2. Kenosis Is Voluntary, Not Forced

Christ’s self-emptying was a free, loving choice.

John 10:17–18 – “I lay down my life… no one takes it from me”

Philippians 2:7 – “He made himself nothing”

True servanthood flows from willingness, not compulsion.

3. Kenosis Involves Limitation, Not Weakness

Jesus accepted real human limitations (hunger, fatigue, learning), yet without sin.

Luke 2:52 – Growth in wisdom

Matthew 4:2 – Hunger in the wilderness

Limitation was embraced for mission, not due to lack of power.

4. Kenosis Explains Christ’s Dependence on the Father

Jesus lived in constant dependence on the Father and the Holy Spirit.

John 5:30 – “I can do nothing by myself”

Acts 10:38 – Anointed with the Holy Spirit and power

This models perfect obedience and trust.

5. Kenosis Does Not Eliminate Divine Authority

Even in humility, Jesus possessed divine authority.

Matthew 28:18 – “All authority… has been given to me”

Mark 2:10 – Authority to forgive sins

Humility and authority coexist in Christ.

6. Kenosis Has a Redemptive Purpose

The goal of kenosis is salvation, not merely example.

2 Corinthians 8:9 – He became poor so we might become rich

Hebrews 2:14–17 – He became like us to redeem us

His humility was mission-driven.

7. Kenosis Is a Pattern for Christian Living

Paul presents kenosis as a model for believers.

Philippians 2:5 – “Have this mind among yourselves”

Mark 10:45 – The Son of Man came to serve

Christian maturity is shaped by Christlike humility

Jesus’ humanity was genuine and complete. He possessed a physical body, a human mind, emotions, and will. He grew in wisdom and stature (Luke 2:52), experienced hunger and thirst (John 4:6; Matthew 4:2), felt compassion and sorrow (Matthew 9:36; John 11:35), and endured pain and suffering. These experiences confirm that He was truly human and not merely appearing as a man.

Jesus Christ was truly and fully human. Scripture affirms His humanity in the following five clear ways:

Real Human Birth
Jesus was born of a woman at a specific time in history, entering the human family (Luke 2:7; Galatians 4:4).

True Human Body and Nature
He possessed a physical body, human mind, emotions, and will (John 4:6; Luke 2:52; John 11:35).

Experienced Human Limitations
Jesus experienced hunger, thirst, fatigue, pain, and suffering, just as other humans do (Matthew 4:2; John 19:28).

Subject to Temptation Yet Sinless
He was genuinely tempted but never sinned, proving perfect obedience (Matthew 4:1–11; Hebrews 4:15).

Died a Real Human Death
Jesus truly suffered and died on the cross, making atonement for humanity (Mark 15:37; Hebrews 2:14).

Hypostatic union

Hypostatic union. The union of Christ’s divine and human natures is known as the hypostatic union. In one person, Jesus Christ possesses two distinct natures—divine and human—without confusion, change, division, or separation. Both natures operate together in perfect unity, enabling Christ to act as both God and man (John 1:1,14; Colossians 2:9).

Orthodox Statements on the Person of Christ

Orthodox Christology affirms biblical truth as defined and guarded by the historic church. The Person of Christ refers to who Jesus is, not merely what He does.

Jesus Christ is one divine Person who possesses two complete natures—divine and human.

Core Orthodox Statements

One Person, Not Two
Jesus Christ is one Person, not two separate persons acting together. The subject of all His actions is the eternal Son of God.

Two Natures
Christ possesses two complete natures:

Fully divine , Fully human

Without Confusion or Change
The divine and human natures are not mixed or altered. Each nature retains its own properties.

Without Division or Separation
The two natures are united in one Person and cannot be separated.

The Eternal Son Became Man
The second Person of the Trinity assumed human nature at the incarnation; He did not become a new person.

One Willful Subject
All actions—miracles, suffering, obedience, death—are performed by the one Person of Christ according to either nature.

True God and True Man Forever
After the resurrection and ascension, Christ remains both God and man eternally.

Dissents from orthodoxy are teachings that depart from the biblical and historic Christian understanding of the Person of Jesus Christ—one Person in two natures, fully God and fully man.

Major Christological Dissents

Denial of Deity
Jesus is viewed as only a man, prophet, or exalted teacher.
Rejects His eternal Godhood.

Denial of Humanity
Jesus only appeared human and did not truly suffer.
Rejects real incarnation and redemption.

Christ as a Created Being
Jesus is considered the highest creature, not eternal God.
Denies equality with the Father.

Division of Christ’s Person
Separates Jesus into two persons—divine and human.
Destroys the unity of Christ.

Confusion of Natures
Blends divine and human natures into one mixed nature.
Eliminates true humanity.

Partial Humanity
Christ lacked a human mind or will.
Makes His humanity incomplete.

Adopted Sonship
Jesus became God’s Son at baptism or resurrection.
Denies eternal Sonship.

FOUR CHURCH COUNCILS

Council Error Addressed Decision
(1) Nicea 325 Arius claimed that Christ was not eternal but a created being – divine but not God (denied full deity). Not true God. Christ is the same essence and being as the Father but distinct in Person.
(2) Constantinople 381 Apollinarius claimed that Christ had a human body and soul, but a divine spirit. Not true man. Affirmed Nicea and declared Christ as fully human.
(3) Ephesus 431 Nestorius denied the full deity and union and stated that God could not become flesh; the divinity of Christ was only in appearance – he was a “god-bearer,” not true God-man. Declared the full union of Christ’s human and divine natures in one person.
(4) Chalcedon 451 Eutyches claimed that the humanity of Christ was swallowed up in His divinity; Christ was only a phantom. Not true man. Orthodox position: fully God and fully man inseparably united, but unconfused, in one person forever (the hypostatic union).

Council of Nicea (AD 325) – Important Details (Short)

Convened to address the Arian controversy concerning the nature of Christ

Arius taught that Jesus Christ was a created being and not eternal

The council affirmed that Jesus Christ is fully God

Declared Christ to be of the same essence (homoousios) as the Father

Upheld that Christ is eternal, uncreated, and true God

Formulated the Nicene Creed to safeguard orthodox belief

Who wrote the Nicene Creed and why?

Who wrote it?
The Nicene Creed was formulated by the bishops of the early Church who gathered at the Council of Nicaea (AD 325).

The council was convened by Constantine the Great

Around 300 bishops participated

The creed reflects the collective teaching of the Church, strongly influenced by leaders like Athanasius of Alexandria

Why was it written?
The creed was written to defend the biblical truth about Jesus Christ against false teaching.

Main reasons:

To refute Arianism, which taught that Jesus was a created being

To affirm that Jesus Christ is fully God, eternal and uncreated

To clearly state that Christ is of the same essence (homoousios) as the Father

To preserve unity and orthodoxy in the Church

To provide a clear doctrinal standard for teaching and worship

The Nicene Creed (AD 325)

We believe in one God, the Father Almighty,
Maker of all things visible and invisible.

And in one Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of God,
Begotten of the Father, the only-begotten;
That is, of the essence of the Father,
God of God, Light of Light, very God of very God,
Begotten, not made, being of one substance (homoousios) with the Father;
By whom all things were made;

Who for us men and for our salvation came down from heaven,
And was incarnate, and was made man;
He suffered, and the third day He rose again,
And ascended into heaven;
And He shall come again to judge the living and the dead.

And in the Holy Spirit.

Jesus’ baptism

Jesus’ baptism marked the beginning of His public ministry. Although He was sinless, He was baptized by John to identify with sinful humanity and to fulfill all righteousness (Matthew 3:13–17). His baptism also revealed the Trinity, with the Father’s voice from heaven and the Spirit descending upon Him.

The temptation: of Christ further demonstrates His true humanity. Jesus was tempted by Satan in the wilderness in every major area of human weakness, yet He remained without sin (Matthew 4:1–11). His victory over temptation proves His moral perfection and enables Him to sympathize with believers in their struggles (Hebrews 4:15).

The Temptation of Jesus

The temptation of Jesus is a crucial event that reveals both His true humanity and His perfect obedience. Immediately after His baptism, Jesus was led by the Holy Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil. This event shows that Jesus was tested as a real man, yet remained completely sinless.

A. The Setting of the Temptation

After His baptism, where the Father affirmed Him and the Spirit descended upon Him, Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness (Matthew 4:1; Luke 4:1).

The wilderness represents isolation, testing, and dependence on God.Jesus fasted for forty days and nights, emphasizing His physical weakness.The temptation did not come when Jesus was strong, but when He was humanly vulnerable.This shows that temptation itself is not sin; yielding to it is.

B. The Tempter

The Bible identifies the tempter as Satan, the adversary and deceiver (Matthew 4:3).

Satan tempts to destroy, deceive, and derail God’s purposes.He attacks Jesus at the very beginning of His public ministry.The temptations are designed to challenge Jesus’ identity, trust in God, and obedience.Satan often tempts us at moments of spiritual clarity or exhaustion.

C. The First Temptation – Stones to Bread

(Appeal to Physical Need)

Satan said, “If You are the Son of God, command these stones to become bread” (Matthew 4:3).

Jesus was hungry after fasting.The temptation was not to eat, but to use divine power independently of the Father.Satan questioned Jesus’ identity: “If You are the Son of God…”

Jesus responded with Scripture:
“Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceeds from the mouth of God” (Deuteronomy 8:3).

D. The Second Temptation – Leap from the Temple

(Appeal to Pride and Presumption)

Satan took Jesus to the pinnacle of the temple and urged Him to throw Himself down, quoting Scripture (Psalm 91:11–12).

Satan misused Scripture to justify reckless behavior.This temptation encouraged Jesus to force God’s hand and prove His Sonship publicly.It was a call to presumption, not faith.

Jesus replied:
“You shall not test the Lord your God” (Deuteronomy 6:16).

Lesson: True faith trusts God without demanding signs or proofs.

E. The Third Temptation – Kingdoms of the World

(Appeal to Power and Shortcut to Glory)

Satan offered Jesus all the kingdoms of the world in exchange for worship (Matthew 4:8–9).

This was an offer of glory without the cross.Satan offered what did not truly belong to him.The temptation challenged Jesus’ loyalty and worship.

Jesus firmly responded:
“You shall worship the Lord your God, and Him only you shall serve” (Deuteronomy 6:13).

Lesson: God’s kingdom cannot be gained by compromising worship or obedience.

F. Jesus’ Victory Over Temptation

Jesus resisted every temptation using Scripture.He did not argue, negotiate, or rely on miracles.His victory demonstrates His sinless nature (Hebrews 4:15).Angels ministered to Him after the temptation (Matthew 4:11).

Jesus succeeded where Adam and Israel failed, proving Himself to be the obedient Son.

G. Theological Significance

The temptation of Jesus shows that:

Jesus is fully human and understands our struggles.Jesus is fully divine and perfectly obedient.Jesus is qualified to be our High Priest (Hebrews 2:18).Victory over temptation comes through dependence on God’s Word.

Sinless:Throughout His earthly life, Jesus lived in complete obedience to the Father and remained entirely sinless. Scripture affirms that He “knew no sin” and committed no wrongdoing (2 Corinthians 5:21; 1 Peter 2:22). His sinless life qualified Him to be the perfect sacrifice for human sin.

10. His earthly ministry and miracles

While the primary purpose of Jesus’ earthly ministry (see the Life of Christ chart at Matthew 26, NIV Study Bible) was to carry out God’s plan of redemption, His earthly ministry included a number of significant activities:

- preaching the Gospel and the need for repentance. Mk. 1:14–15

- teaching God’s principles for living life well. Mt. 5:1–11

- healing disease and sickness. Mt. 4:23

casting out demons. Mk. 1:21–28

1. Confronting the false teaching of His day – Mt. 5:17-20, 27-28 (see chart on the main teachings of the Jewish Sects at Matthew 23).

2. Training His disciples – Mk. 4:33-34; Mk. 6:7-12; Lk. 4:25-35 (see chart on the Parables at Luke 15).

3. Meeting the physical needs of people – Mk. 6:30-44.

4. Correcting the false practice of religion – Mk. 7:5-13.

5. Demonstrating servant leadership – Jn. 13:1-17.

6. Previewing the reality of heaven and hell – Lk. 16:19-31; Mt. 25:40-46.

7. Predicting the future, including His second coming – Mt. 24–25.

8. Proving He was the Son of God, the promised Old Testament Messiah

- by His words: Lk. 4:14-21.

- by His works: Mt. 9:1-8.

9. Fulfilling Old Testament prophecy – Mt. 8:16-17.

10. Causing men to place their faith in Him – Jn. 2:11; Jn. 20:30-31.

11. Glorifying the Father – Jn. 8:50; Jn. 17:4.

1. Four great periods of miracles:

- (1) the time of the Exodus (Moses).

- (2) the time of the prophets Elijah and Elisha (1 and 2 Kings).

- (3) the time of Christ.

- (4) the time of Acts and the Apostles (Peter and Paul).

2. General purposes of the miracles in each period:

- make God known to men and glorify Him.

- demonstrate the supremacy of God’s power.

- authenticate God’s Word.

- confirm God’s messenger.

3. Specific purposes of Jesus’ miracles:

- authenticated Jesus as Messiah (Jn. 20:30–31).

- glorified God and made Him known (Jn. 11:38–44).

- proved that Jesus was God (Jn. 9:30–33).

- caused men to believe in Christ (Jn. 11:45).

His earthly ministry:During His earthly ministry, Jesus taught with authority, preached the kingdom of God, healed the sick, cast out demons, and performed many miracles. These works demonstrated God’s compassion and confirmed Jesus’ identity as the Messiah. His miracles were signs of divine power operating through His human life (Acts 2:22).

His mission of salvation:The ultimate purpose of Christ’s humanity was His mission of salvation. By becoming human, He could act as the perfect mediator between God and mankind. He offered His life as a ransom for many, died on the cross in the place of sinners, and rose again as a glorified man (Mark 10:45; Hebrews 2:17; 1 Timothy 2:5). Through His humanity, salvation was accomplished for all who believe.

In conclusion, the humanity of Christ is not a secondary doctrine but a foundational truth of the Christian faith. Jesus Christ is fully human, yet without sin, enabling Him to sympathize with humanity and provide complete and eternal salvation.

His Offices: Prophet, Priest, Judge, and King

— written for Bible teaching, sermon delivery, or study notes, with smooth flow and key Scriptures.

His Offices

Jesus Christ fulfills the divinely appointed offices through which God relates to humanity. In the Old Testament, prophets, priests, judges, and kings were anointed to serve God’s purposes. Jesus perfectly and finally fulfills all these offices, revealing Him as the complete and eternal Mediator between God and mankind.

1. Christ as Prophet

A prophet is one who speaks God’s Word to the people.

Old Testament Background

God promised a prophet like Moses (Deuteronomy 18:15–19).

Prophets revealed God’s will, called people to repentance, and foretold future events.

Fulfillment in Christ

Jesus is the ultimate Prophet who reveals God perfectly (Hebrews 1:1–2).

He did not merely speak God’s Word—He is the Word (John 1:1, 14).

Jesus taught with authority, not as the scribes (Matthew 7:28–29).

He foretold future events such as His death, resurrection, and the destruction of Jerusalem (Matthew 24; Mark 8:31).

Significance

Jesus as Prophet reveals God’s truth clearly and completely, leaving no greater revelation to come.

2. Christ as Priest

A priest is one who represents the people before God and offers sacrifice.

Old Testament Background

Priests came from the tribe of Levi.

They offered daily sacrifices for sin (Leviticus 16).

The high priest entered the Most Holy Place once a year.

Fulfillment in Christ

Jesus is our Great High Priest (Hebrews 4:14).

He offered not an animal sacrifice, but Himself (Hebrews 9:12).

His sacrifice was once for all (Hebrews 10:10).

He continually intercedes for believers (Romans 8:34; Hebrews 7:25).

Significance

Jesus as Priest removes the barrier of sin and gives believers direct access to God.

3. Christ as Judge

A judge is one who administers justice and renders final decisions.

Old Testament Background

Judges in Israel were raised by God to deliver and govern the people.

God Himself is the ultimate Judge (Psalm 9:7–8).

Fulfillment in Christ

God has appointed Jesus as the Judge of all humanity (Acts 17:31).

Jesus will judge the living and the dead (2 Timothy 4:1).

His judgments are righteous, fair, and based on truth (John 5:22, 27).

Significance

Jesus as Judge assures believers of justice and warns the world of accountability before God.

4. Christ as King

A king is one who rules with authority and sovereignty.

Old Testament Background

God promised an everlasting King from David’s line (2 Samuel 7:12–16).

The Messiah was foretold as a righteous and eternal ruler (Isaiah 9:6–7).

Fulfillment in Christ

Jesus is the King of Kings and Lord of Lords (Revelation 19:16).

His kingdom is both present and future (Luke 17:20–21).

He reigns now from heaven and will reign visibly at His return (Matthew 25:31).

Significance

Jesus as King rules over hearts today and will establish perfect justice and peace forever.

Summary of His Offices

Office Role Fulfillment in Christ
Prophet Reveals God to people Final and perfect revelation
Priest Represents people to God Once-for-all sacrifice
Judge Executes justice Righteous and final judgment
King Rules God’s people Eternal and universal reign

Conclusion

Jesus Christ uniquely fulfills all four offices—Prophet, Priest, Judge, and King. He speaks God’s truth, provides atonement, executes justice, and reigns eternally. In Him, God’s plan of redemption, righteousness, and restoration is perfectly accomplished.

13. His Sacrificial Substitutionary Death

The death of Jesus Christ was not an accident or merely the result of human injustice. It was a divinely planned, sacrificial, and substitutionary act through which God accomplished redemption. Jesus died in the place of sinners, bearing the penalty of sin so that humanity might be reconciled to God.His death was voluntary, sacrificial, substitutionary, atoning, sufficient for all.

Meaning of Substitution

Substitution means one person dying in place of another.

The penalty for sin is death (Romans 6:23).

Jesus took upon Himself what sinners deserved (2 Corinthians 5:21).

Old Testament Background

The sacrificial system pointed to a substitute bearing guilt (Leviticus 16).

The Passover lamb died in place of the firstborn (Exodus 12).

Isaiah 53 portrays the Messiah bearing the sins of many.

Fulfillment in Christ

Jesus is the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world (John 1:29).

He willingly laid down His life (John 10:17–18).

His death satisfied God’s justice and expressed God’s love (Romans 3:25–26; 5:8).

Significance

Sin is forgiven.John 6:38-40,10:18,17:4

God’s wrath is satisfied.Heb 9:13-14,Col 2:13-15, 1 Jn 2:2,Eph 1:7-10

Christ demonstrated God’s undeserved love.Jn 3:16,1 Jn 4:9-10, Rom5:8

Salvation is available through faith alone to all.Jn 1:12,3:14-16,3:36,6:40,Ro 4:22-25.

14. His Bodily Resurrection

The resurrection of Jesus Christ is the cornerstone of Christian faith. It was not a spiritual illusion or symbolic event, but a real, bodily resurrection in history. God raised Jesus from the dead, declaring Him victorious over sin and death.

Nature of the Resurrection

Jesus rose with a physical, glorified body (Luke 24:39).

The tomb was empty (Matthew 28:6).

He was seen and touched by many witnesses (1 Corinthians 15:3–8).

Importance of the Resurrection

Confirms Jesus’ identity as the Son of God (Romans 1:4).

Validates His sacrificial death (1 Corinthians 15:17).

Defeats death and the grave (1 Corinthians 15:54–57).

Guarantees the future resurrection of believers (1 Corinthians 15:20–23).

Significance

Without the resurrection, faith is meaningless. With it, hope is secure, sin is defeated, and eternal life is assured.

> The reality of Christ’s bodily resurrection is one of the best attested historical events of all time.

The following facts offer substantial un refuted evidence:

The empty tomb.

The angel's announcement.

The absent body.

The linen wrappings left behind.

The testimony of the women.

The testing of the 2 disciples on the road to Emmaus.

The appearance to Peter.

The 2 appearances to the disciples.

The changes in and reality of Christ's glorified body (flesh and bones and yet spiritual).

The dramatic change in the boldness and witness of the lives of the apostles.

The miraculous display of power in Acts.

The change in Christian worship from the Sabbath to Sunday, the day of the resurrection.

The existence of the Church today.

Admittedly all of the evidence is biblical, so we rest once again on the reliability of Scripture. Since Scripture is true, so is the resurrection.

False views of the resurrection

There are many false humanistic views of the resurrection but they all have to ignore or deny the truth of the Bible. Some of these false views are as follows:

- That Christ did not really die but only lost consciousness on the cross.

- That the disciples stole His body and hid Christ's body and lied about His appearances.

- That only Christ's spirit was made alive, not His body.

> All of these and other similar false views directly contradict the teaching of Scripture and therefore must be rejected.

The reality of the bodily resurrection of Christ can only be spiritually discerned as a person chooses to believe by faith in the truth of the Word.

The significance of the bodily resurrection is the essence of Christianity

The bodily resurrection of Jesus

- validates Old Testament and New Testament prophecy.

- Lk. 24:19–27 - Lk. 24:44–45 - Ps. 16:10 - Ac. 2:24–35

- demonstrates the deity of Christ and God's approval of His redemptive work.

- Ro. 1:4 - Eph. 1:18–21

- comprises the central message of the Gospel.

- 1 Co. 15:2–4 - Ro. 10:9–10- Ac. 2:24 - Ac. 4:8–11 - Ac. 17:31–32

- assures us that

- Christ is alive.

- preaching the Gospel is not wasted effort.

- our faith is not in vain or worthless.

- we are not giving false testimony.

- we are not still dead in our sins.

- sin and death have been defeated.

- we have hope of eternal life.

- we will also be bodily resurrected and have a glorified body like Christ’s.

- we will not be disembodied spirits for eternity.

1 Co. 15- enables believers to live in the resurrection power of Jesus and not sin.

Ro. 6:4-12 Ro. 8:9-12,Php. 3:10-11

The resurrection body of Jesus Christ

The glorified resurrected body of Jesus had the following characteristics:

- was physical (real) and yet spiritual.

- was able to talk and be heard.

- was recognizable.

- was able to eat.

- was non‑material in some ways.

- was not limited by time, space or material objects.

Lk. 24:13-43,Jn. 20:10‑21:25

Based on 1 Co. 15:35‑55, the glorified resurrection body of believers will be like unto Christ’s.

Without the bodily resurrection of Christ there is

- no forgiveness of sins.

- no freedom from the curse of death.

- no life after death.

By the resurrection Christ’s humanity was glorified and His deity reinstated to His former exalted position.

15. His Ascension

Having finished His earthly mission, Jesus ascended into heaven after 40 days of post-resurrection appearances and ministry. Christ returned to heaven in His glorified body and is now seated at the right hand of God. From every indication of Scripture Christ will retain His glorified humanity forever. (See Rev. 5:6) Christ ascended into heaven as the glorified God/man. Christ will return in the same way. The incarnation was Jesus’ descent from heaven. The ascension is His return to heaven.

Key texts on the ascension:

- Mk. 16:19,- Lk. 24:45–53- Jn. 6:62- Jn. 20:17- Ac. 1:1–11- Eph. 4:7–13- 1 Pe. 3:21–22

Based on these and other passages the Ascension of Jesus Christ is significant because it

- completes the resurrection.Ac. 2:32–33

- commences His re-exaltation and enthronement.Php. 2:5–11,Jn. 17:5

confirms God’s approval of His redemptive work.Heb. 1:3, Heb. 12:2

- activates the intercessory ministry of Christ.Ro. 8:34

- fulfills the earthly mission of Christ and ends His self‑limitation (Kenosis).

Php. 2:9–11

- allows the Holy Spirit to come into the world to indue believers and convict sinners.Jn. 16:7‑11

- permits access by believers to God.Heb. 4:14‑16,Ro. 5:1‑2

- inaugurates the Church Age and looks forward to Christ’s second coming.Ac. 1:7‑11

C. The present ministry of Christ

Christ is now in heaven seated at God’s right hand having resumed His pre‑incarnation position of pre‑eminence and sovereignty.

1. Christ’s ministry in heaven generally

- Christ is exercising authority over and sustaining all of the created order in accordance with the Father’s plan.

Mt. 28:18, Eph. 1:19‑22,Col. 1:17‑18,1 Pe. 3:21‑22

- interceding for individual saints and the Church before the Father.

- 1 Jn. 2:1 - Ro. 8:33‑34

- securely protecting the salvation of all saints.

- Heb. 7:25 - Jn. 10:27‑30- Ro. 8:38‑39- Jude 24

- preparing a place for believers in heaven.- Jn. 14:1‑3

2. Christ’s ministry to the Church.

Christ is

- acting as head and guardian. - Eph. 1:19‑23 - Heb. 13:20

- being the chief cornerstone and foundation. - 1 Co. 3:11‑15 - Eph. 2:20- 1 Pe. 2:4‑8

- cleansing the Body of Christ and preparing her to be His bride. - Eph. 5:25‑27

- giving spiritually gifted men to the Church to equip and build up the Church to maturity in Him. - Eph. 4:7‑16

3.Christ’s ministry to individual believers

“Christ’s ministry to individual believers”, listing twelve aspects of Christ’s work in believers with supporting Scripture references:

1. Christ is indwelling and empowering – Romans 8:9–12; Philippians 4:13.

2. making them dead to sin but alive to God – Romans 6:11–12.

3. sympathizing with them in their weakness – Hebrews 4:15.

4. extending mercy and grace to help in time of need – Hebrews 4:16.

5. enabling believers to bear fruit as they abide in Him and His Word John15:1–17.

6. empowering them to resist temptation – Hebrews 2:17–18.

7. witnessing through them – Acts 1:8.

8. being their wisdom, righteousness and holiness – 1 Corinthians 1:30.

9. manifesting His life and glory through them – 2 Corinthians 4:5–7; Galatians 2:20; Colossians 3:4.

10. fellowshipping with them – 1 John 1:3.

11. equipping them to do His will and please God – Hebrews 13:21.

12. conforming them to His image – 2 Corinthians 3:18; Romans 8:28–29; Philippians 3:20–21.

D.The future ministry of Christ

1. Return to rapture His Church – Jn 14:1-3 & 1 Th. 4:13-18.

2. Resurrect dead believers and transform living believers – Php. 3:20-21.

3. Oversee the pouring out of judgment on sin during the tribulation – Rev. 5:1-10.

4. Conclude His marriage to His bride the Church in heaven – Eph. 5:25-27 & Rev. 19:6-9.

5. Conduct the “bema seat” evaluation of believers in heaven

Ro. 14:9-12, 2 Co. 5:9-10, 1 Co. 3:6-15, & 1 Co. 4:1-5.

6. Return in His second coming to judge sin and defeat God’s enemies at Armageddon

Rev. 19:11-21.

7. Put everything in subjection to Himself, and hand the kingdom over to God the Father

Rev. 11:15 & 1 Co. 15:24-28.

2

Pneumatology

The Holy Spirit — Nature, Person, and Ministry

Introduction

Many studies of the Holy Spirit focus on His power, work, and gifts to the exclusion of His deity and personhood. And simply equating the Holy Spirit with “power” usually leads to erroneous practice in relationship to doctrine. Our emphasis will be on what the Bible teaches about the nature, person, and ministry of the Holy Spirit – the 3rd Person of the Trinity.

A. The Nature and Person of the Holy Spirit

1. His Deity

The Holy Spirit is in essential nature as equally God as is the Father and the Son, and relates to them as a person.

Matthew 28:19 — The “name” of the Holy Spirit is on the same level as the Father and Son.

John 14:16–17 — The Holy Spirit is referred to as “another Counselor” of the same kind as Jesus.

2 Corinthians 13:14 — The grace of Christ, love of God, and “fellowship” of the Holy Spirit are equated.

Hebrews 10:15–17 — When God speaks, the Holy Spirit is said to speak.

Acts 5:3–4 — Since only God is “Holy,” for the Spirit to be called the “Holy Spirit” identifies Him as deity.

Romans 8:11 — Omnipotence – a characteristic that belongs only to God.

2. His Characteristics (Attributes)

The characteristics that belong only to God are attributed to the Holy Spirit:

Attribute Reference
Eternity Hebrews 9:14
Life John 6:63
Love Romans 5:5
Truth John 14:16–17
Omniscience 1 Corinthians 2:10–11
Omnipresence Psalm 139:7–12

Symbols of the Holy Spirit

Symbol Reference
Wind John 3:8
Oil 1 Samuel 16:13; Acts 10:38; 1 John 2:20, 27
Living Water John 4:14; John 7:38–39
Fire Matthew 3:11–12
Deposit (Guarantee) Ephesians 1:13–14
Seal (Security) 2 Corinthians 1:22
Clothing Luke 24:49
Dove John 1:32–34
Power Acts 1:8

Meaning of “Clothed” The Greek word endys■sthe means:

It is not partial covering — it implies being fully wrapped. This is not self-generated strength. This is not education or talent. This is divine empowerment.

  • To put on garments
  • To be dressed
  • To be enveloped or covered

3. His Titles and Symbols

The titles given to the Holy Spirit in Scripture denote His nature as God:

  • Spirit of Holiness – Romans 1:4
  • Spirit of the Lord – Luke 4:18
  • Spirit of Christ – Romans 8:9
  • Spirit of Jesus – Acts 16:7
  • Spirit of Your Father – Matthew 10:20
  • Spirit of the Living God – 2 Corinthians 3:3

4. His Procession

The Holy Spirit eternally proceeds from the Father and the Son (Trinitarian doctrine). Scripture References: John 14:15–17; John 15:26; John 16:7; Galatians 4:4–6 The Spirit was “sent forth” on Pentecost to:

  • Empower the work of the Church
  • Baptize believers into the Body of Christ and enable believers to witness and produce spiritual fruit
  • Convict the world of sin

Related Verses: Luke 24:48–49; John 7:39–40; Acts 1:1–5; John 16:7–11

5. His Personhood

While the Holy Spirit is identified with the “power” of God, He is not an impersonal force but a person – the 3rd Person of the Trinity.

Meaning of the Term “Holy Spirit” – The Paraclete “Holy Spirit” is referred to as the “Paraclete” in the New Testament, which means:

And can be literally translated as “One who comes alongside to aid or help.” The Holy Spirit is said to be the consolation (Paraclete) of Israel, related to salvation which would come through the person of a Redeemer. So even “Paraclete” points to personhood (Luke 2:22–32).

  • Counselor – John 14:16
  • Comforter – John 14:26; John 15:26
  • Consoler – John 16:7
  • Advocate – 1 John 2:1

The Holy Spirit Has…

  • Emotions – Ephesians 4:30
  • A Mind – Romans 8:27
  • A Will – 1 Corinthians 12:11

The Holy Spirit as a Person

  • Speaks – John 15:26
  • Prays – Romans 8:26–27
  • Guides – John 16:13
  • Teaches – 1 Corinthians 2:13
  • Leads – Romans 8:14

The Holy Spirit Can Be…

  • Resisted – Acts 7:51
  • Lied To – Acts 5:3
  • Grieved – Ephesians 4:30
  • Insulted – Hebrews 10:29
  • Quenched – 1 Thessalonians 5:19

Masculine Personal Pronoun “He” The Holy Spirit is referred to in Scripture by use of the masculine personal pronoun “He,” even though “spirit” is neuter (John 16:13–14), and in John 14:16, Jesus refers to the Holy Spirit as “another Counselor,” which means “of the same kind” as Jesus. It is a great disservice to the Holy Spirit to deny His personhood and refer to Him as a force! Such a concept is not biblical.

✎ Assignment: Why do we usually think of the Holy Spirit as simply “power”?

B. The Past Ministry and Work of the Holy Spirit

1. In the Old Testament

The Holy Spirit in Creation, Sustaining and Preserving Life

Numbers 11:24–30 — Moses gathered seventy elders around the Tabernacle. The Spirit of the Lord came upon them, and they began to prophesy (v. 25). Two men, Eldad and Medad, remained in the camp but also received the Spirit and prophesied (v. 26). Joshua wanted Moses to stop them. Moses responded with humility:

Numbers 11:29 — “Are you zealous for my sake? Oh, that all the Lord’s people were prophets and that the Lord would put His Spirit upon them!”

Anointing and Empowering for Service The Spirit of the Lord empowered Gideon to lead Israel against the Midianites.

Judges 6:33–34 — “Then all the Midianites and Amalekites, the people of the East, gathered together; and they crossed over and encamped in the Valley of Jezreel. But the Spirit of the Lord came upon Gideon; then he blew the trumpet, and the Abiezrites gathered behind him.”

Permanent Indwelling of the Spirit – Old Testament vs. New Testament The Bible shows that the work of the Holy Spirit in the Old Testament appears different from that in the New Testament.

Old Testament New Testament
The Spirit came upon people temporarily The Spirit dwells permanently in believers
Usually for special tasks or leadership Believers are empowered, filled, and guided daily by the Spirit

Given to selected individuals (prophets, kings, judges) Given to all believers

Could depart from a person — Remains permanently in believers

Mainly for specific tasks or leadership — For daily guidance, holiness, and power

John 14:17 — “He lives with you and will be in you.”

1 Corinthians 6:19 — “Or do you not know that your body is the temple of the Holy Spirit who is in you, whom you have from God, and you are not your own?”

Acts 1:8 — “But you shall receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you; and you shall be witnesses to Me in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth.”

Judges 15:14–15 – The Spirit Coming Upon People (Old Testament)

When Samson came to Lehi, the Philistines came shouting against him. The Spirit of the Lord came powerfully upon Samson, and the ropes on his arms became like burned flax and fell from his hands. Samson then found the jawbone of a donkey, picked it up, and struck down a thousand Philistines. In the Old Testament, the Holy Spirit certainly worked among God’s people, but His ministry was different from the New Testament. Several ministries of the Spirit that believers experience today were not normally present in the Old Testament:

1. Permanent Indwelling

In the Old Testament, the Holy Spirit did not permanently live inside believers. The Spirit came upon people temporarily for a specific task, and could leave a person. Example: Saul – the Spirit came upon him but later departed (1 Samuel 10:10; 1 Samuel 16:14) Contrast with the New Testament: Believers now have the Spirit living in them permanently (John 14:17 – “He dwells with you and will be in you.”)

2. Regular On-Going Empowerment

In the Old Testament, the Spirit empowered individuals occasionally, not continuously. The Spirit came upon judges, prophets, and kings when needed.

But this empowerment was not the regular experience of every believer.

  • Samson received strength when the Spirit came upon him (Judges 15:14–15)
  • David received empowerment for leadership (1 Samuel 16:13)

3. Sealing

The Holy Spirit did not seal believers in the Old Testament. In the New Testament, sealing means the Spirit marks believers as belonging to God and guarantees salvation. Example: Ephesians 1:13 – believers are sealed with the Holy Spirit. This ministry began after Christ’s resurrection and Pentecost.

4. Baptizing into the Body of Christ

The Holy Spirit did not baptize believers into the body of Christ in the Old Testament. Spirit baptism began in the New Testament church.

1 Corinthians 12:13 — “For by one Spirit we were all baptized into one body.” This happens when a person believes in Christ. The Holy Spirit was the same in the Old and New Testament, but His role and the way in which He revealed Himself to man was different than in the New Testament.

✎ Assignment: Why did the Holy Spirit function differently in the Old Testament?

The Spirit in Christ’s Sacrifice and Resurrection

Hebrews 9:14 — “Christ offered Himself through the eternal Spirit.”

Romans 8:11 — “The Spirit of Him who raised Jesus from the dead lives in you.”

1 Corinthians 3:16 — “Do you not know that you are God’s temple and that God’s Spirit dwells in you?” But the Spirit did selectively:

Ministry Reference
Convict of sin Genesis 6:3
Teach and instruct Nehemiah 9:20
Admonish Nehemiah 9:30
Lead Psalm 143:10
Was rebelled against Psalm 106:33
Was not depended on Isaiah 30:1

✎ Assignment: Why did the Holy Spirit function differently in the OT?

Revelation and Inspiration One of the major aspects of the Holy Spirit’s work in the Old Testament had to do with enabling men to speak for God. The Holy Spirit was the means which God used to speak through men (2 Samuel 23:2;

Micah 3:8). —

a. The Spirit Spoke Through David

2 Samuel 23:2 — “The Spirit of the Lord spoke by me, and His word was on my tongue.” The Holy Spirit inspired David to speak and write God’s word.

b. The Spirit Empowered the Prophet Micah

Micah 3:8 — “But truly I am full of power by the Spirit of the Lord, and of justice and might, to declare to Jacob his transgression and to Israel his sin.” The Holy Spirit gave prophets power, courage, and authority to proclaim God’s message.

c. Characteristics of Spirit-Inspired Revelation When the Holy Spirit inspired Old Testament prophets:

  • The message came from God, not from human ideas
  • The prophets spoke with divine authority
  • The message often included correction, warning, or hope
  • The revelation pointed toward God’s redemptive plan, including the coming Messiah

d. Connection to the Doctrine of Inspiration The Old Testament examples support the biblical doctrine that Scripture is inspired by God through the Holy Spirit, confirmed by a later New Testament explanation:

2 Peter 1:21 — “For prophecy never came by the will of man, but holy men of God spoke as they were moved by the Holy Spirit.”

Aspect Reference Description

Speaking through God’s servants 2 Samuel 23:2 — The Spirit spoke through David

Empowering prophets Micah 3:8 The Spirit gave power to proclaim God’s message

Revelation of God’s truth Various prophets The Spirit revealed divine truth

Inspiration of Scripture 2 Peter 1:21 Prophets spoke as moved by the Spirit

e. The Spirit Spoke Through David (Recognized by Jesus) Jesus Himself affirmed that the words of David in the Psalms were spoken by the Holy Spirit.

Matthew 22:43–44 — “He said to them, ‘How then does David in the Spirit call Him “Lord,” saying: “The Lord said to my Lord, Sit at My right hand, Till I make Your enemies Your footstool”?’” referring to Psalm 110:1, a prophetic Psalm about the Messiah. This confirms that Old Testament Scripture was inspired by the Holy Spirit.

f. The Spirit Spoke Through the Scriptures The early church also recognized that the Holy Spirit spoke through the Old Testament Scriptures.

Acts 1:16 — “Men and brethren, this Scripture had to be fulfilled, which the Holy Spirit spoke before by the mouth of David concerning Judas, who became a guide to those who arrested Jesus.”

g. God Spoke Through the Prophets

Hebrews 1:1–2 — “God, who at various times and in various ways spoke in time past to the fathers by the prophets, has in these last days spoken to us by His Son, whom He has appointed heir of all things, through whom also He made the worlds.”

h. The Spirit Convicted People of Sin Through Prophetic Messages Their Spirit-inspired messages were meant to bring spiritual awakening and repentance. Through the prophets, the Spirit would expose sin, warn of judgment, call people back to God, and reveal God’s covenant faithfulness. Examples include the ministries of Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, Micah, and Amos.

i. The Role of the Holy Spirit in Prophetic Revelation

  • The Holy Spirit was the source of prophetic revelation
  • God spoke through human messengers
  • Scripture was inspired by the Spirit
  • Prophetic messages often brought conviction of sin
  • These revelations pointed forward to Christ

j. God Spoke Through Human Messengers

God often introduced prophetic messages with phrases such as “Thus says the Lord,” “The word of the Lord came to me,” and “Hear the word of the Lord.”

Jeremiah 1:9 — “Then the Lord put forth His hand and touched my mouth, and the Lord said to me: ‘Behold, I have put My words in your mouth.’”

k. Scripture Was Inspired by the Holy Spirit

2 Timothy 3:16 — “All Scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness.”

Prophetic Messages Often Brought Conviction of Sin The prophets frequently confronted idolatry, injustice, disobedience to God’s law, and moral corruption (Micah 3:8). This shows that the Holy Spirit empowered prophets not only to reveal truth but also to expose sin and call people to repentance.

l. Prophetic Revelation Pointed Forward to Christ Another important aspect of prophetic revelation is that it prepared the way for the coming of Jesus Christ – the coming Messiah, His suffering and death, His kingdom and reign, and the salvation He would bring.

Isaiah 53:5 — “But He was wounded for our transgressions, He was bruised for our iniquities; the chastisement for our peace was upon Him, and by His stripes we are healed.”

Luke 24:27 — “And beginning at Moses and all the Prophets, He expounded to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning Himself.” It is also quite clear that the Holy Spirit was the primary agent with regard to the inspiration of Scripture (2 Timothy 3:16), teaching, guiding, directing, and influencing the human authors of Scripture (2 Peter 1:20–21). The Holy Spirit also enabled the New Testament writers to remember what Christ taught.

2. In the Life of Christ

Romans 1:4 and 8:11, along with 1 Peter 3:18, seem to indicate that the Spirit was personally involved in Christ’s birth and resurrection. Note: Jesus as a man is a meaningful example for believers to follow, because He demonstrated over and over again His dependence on the Holy Spirit to perform ministry and live a life pleasing to God.

✎ Assignment: Since Jesus was God, why did He so often depend on the Spirit for power and enablement?

3. In Acts and New Testament Times

Here we will deal briefly with the biblical evidence of the work of the Holy Spirit at and after He was poured out at Pentecost. The work of the Holy Spirit is so prominent in the book of Acts that the most accurate title for the book might be “The Acts of the Holy Spirit through the Apostles.” The following affirmations of the work of the Holy Spirit in Acts can be confidently made:

2:28–29 and the promise of Christ in Luke 24:49, John 16:7, and Acts 1:4–5

unbelievers (see also 1 Corinthians 14:22) Tongues (“glossa”) in Acts clearly refers to foreign languages which were unknown to the speakers but known to the hearers. 1 Corinthians 14:21–22 makes it clear that tongues validated God’s supernatural work here. In fact, the word “dialektos” (“native languages”) in Acts 2:6, 8 is not “glossa” but a distinct term for known tongues. The “tongues” gave credibility to the word that was then preached through Peter in Acts 2 and validated his apostolic ministry. Note: in Acts 10 “tongues” validated salvation to the Gentiles, and in Acts 19 tongues validated Paul’s Apostolic ministry.

  • The filling of the apostles with the Holy Spirit was primarily for empowerment for witnessing (Acts 1:8)
  • The baptism and filling of the Spirit on the day of Pentecost in Acts 2 was in direct fulfillment of Joel
  • Being filled with the Spirit and speaking in tongues in Acts 2:11 was a sign of authentication from God to

Note on Tongues It is evident that tongues in Acts were foreign languages, not ecstatic utterances or babblings. When Paul dealt with tongues in 1 Corinthians 12–14, the basic word “glossa” is used. The point Paul makes is that except the speaker gives an interpretation, no one will understand the foreign language being spoken which communicated God’s Word. With the Bible now having completed God’s revelation, there seems to be no real reason for the gift of tongues being exercised in the Church. The private use of tongues for personal worship and edification is left to the individual. The Holy Spirit “baptized” believers into the Body of Christ (1 Corinthians 12:13). By this one baptism with the Holy Spirit, to receive the gift of salvation from God, a believer is:

Note carefully that “were all baptized” is past tense and refers to all believers, whether or not they speak in tongues or use other sign gifts (1 Corinthians 12:27–30). Scripture simply does not teach a second baptism of the Spirit (or second work of grace) after salvation. Further ministries of the Holy Spirit in Acts:

  • Identified with the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ (Romans 6:1–4)
  • Placed into the Body of Christ as a member of His Church (1 Corinthians 12:13)

of God, to glorify God, and to cause men to believe in God (Acts 3:1–9; Acts 5:12–16)

12:1–11)

Note: Spiritual gifts will be dealt with in the course on Church ministry.

  • Enabled bold witnessing for Christ (Acts 1:8; Acts 4:31)
  • Performed miracles through the Apostles – to authenticate the man and his message, to display the power
  • Set apart and empowered men for missionary service (Acts 13:1–5)
  • Filled (directed, influenced, and guided) people for effective service (Acts 6:1–4; Acts 11:22–24)
  • Determined, distributed, and developed spiritual gifts to build up the Body of Christ (1 Corinthians
  • Spoke through prophets (Acts 11:27–28)
  • Purified the Church from sin (Acts 5:1–11)
  • Instructed and led in ministry (Acts 16:6)

Giving of the Holy Spirit in Acts

Event Phenomena Sequence Witnesses Significance

Ch. 2 Tongues and 1. Belief Apostles H.S. sovereignly given to Jewish Pentecost – preaching 2. Water baptism Jews; fulfills O.T. prophecy Holy Spirit given to 3. H.S. after (Joel 2) and Jesus’ promise Jews salvation (Luke 24)

Ch. 8 Laying on of hands 1. Belief Peter & John Authenticates Peter’s Holy Spirit given 2. Water baptism apostolic ministry; through Peter to 3. H.S. after preserves “one Church” in Samaritans salvation Samaria

Ch. 10 Tongues and 1. Belief 7 witnesses H.S. sovereignly given to Gentile Pentecost – exalting God 2. Holy Spirit Gentiles; spiritual blessing Holy Spirit given to 3. Water baptism to all (Genesis 12:3) Gentiles

Ch. 19 Tongues and 1. Belief 12 men Authenticates Paul’s Holy Spirit given prophesying; 2. Water baptism apostolic ministry

through Paul to — laying on of hands 3. H.S. after

Gentiles — salvation

In summary, especially in Acts, the Holy Spirit:

And what was true of the work of the Holy Spirit in Acts was true throughout the New Testament period of 37 A.D. – 100 A.D. Most of the work and ministry of the Spirit as seen in Acts and New Testament times is still present today.

  • Founded the Church
  • Developed the Church
  • Expanded the Church
  • Empowered the Church

✎ Assignment: Why was the miraculous demonstration of the Spirit so necessary in Acts?

C. The Present Ministry and Work of the Holy Spirit

1. To the Lost World Generally

Convicting of Sin (Declaring Guilty)

  • Of sin
  • Of righteousness
  • Of judgment

John 16:8–11 — The ministry of the Holy Spirit is to continue on earth the work which Christ inaugurated of convicting unbelievers of their lostness and need of salvation.

Glorifying Christ

John 16:13–14 — The Holy Spirit will not bring glory to Himself but to Christ.

Restraining Evil

2 Thessalonians 2:6–7 — The Holy Spirit, through the Church, holds back the forces of evil and sin in the world until God determines to let it loose in the Great Tribulation.

2. To the Church Corporately

The Holy Spirit's ministry to the Church includes:

  • Empowering the Church for witnessing and work – Acts 1:8; Luke 24:46–49; 1 Corinthians 2:1–5
  • Making Gospel preaching effective – 1 Peter 1:12
  • Baptizing believers into the Body of Christ – 1 Corinthians 12:13
  • Distributing spiritual gifts within the Body – 1 Corinthians 12:1–11
  • Enabling spiritual growth in the Body – Colossians 1:28–29
  • Unifying the Body of Christ – Ephesians 4:3–4
  • Fostering fellowship among believers – Philippians 2:1

3. To Believers Individually

Identifies Believers with Christ Romans 6:3; Colossians 2:12

Regenerates (Saves) The Holy Spirit is God’s means to effect the new birth in the believer – he is born again, or “from above.” John 3:3–8; 1 Corinthians 6:11
