Cleanses and Purifies from Sin Romans 2:29; Romans 8:13–14

Sanctifies (Sets Apart and Makes Holy)

Romans 15:16; 2 Thessalonians 2:13; 1 Peter 1:2

Indwells Fully and Permanently John 14:17; 1 Corinthians 6:19; 1 Corinthians 3:16; Romans 8:9

Seals and Guarantees Salvation All believers are protected by the Spirit, who was given as a guarantee of our ultimate redemption. 2 Corinthians 1:21–22; Ephesians 1:13–14; Ephesians 4:30

Assures Believers of Salvation The Spirit internally testifies that we are children of God.

Romans 8:16 —

Fills Believers for Service The Spirit directs and controls believers who yield to Him.

Ephesians 5:18 —

Produces Christlike Fruit Galatians 5:16, 22–25; Galatians 6:7–8

Illuminates the Mind to Understand the Things of God 1 Corinthians 2:10–15; 1 John 2:20, 27

Prays for Believers The Holy Spirit helps believers in their weakness. Romans 8:26–27; Ephesians 6:18

Discerns False Spirits and Doctrine

1 John 4:1–4 —

Leads and Guides Believers

Romans 8:14 —

Teaches Believers the Truth

John 16:12–15 —

Note on Spirit Baptism, Indwelling, and Filling Scripture teaches some key concepts and distinctions about the work of the Holy Spirit in these three areas:

Spirit Baptism (1 Corinthians 12:13)

There is no strong scriptural support for a “second baptism,” so-called “second blessing,” or “deeper work of the Spirit.”

  • A one-time event which occurs at salvation for all believers
  • Places believers into the Body of Christ
  • May or may not be accompanied by signs

Indwelling (John 14:17; 1 Corinthians 6:19) All believers, from the moment of salvation, are permanently and fully indwelt by the Holy Spirit who lives in them.

Filling (Ephesians 5:18)

guide, influence, enable, and empower the believer in Christ-likeness and obedience

A believer can never get more of the Holy Spirit than he receives at salvation, but he can allow the Holy Spirit to get more of him.

  • Occurs whenever the believer yields control of his life to God and allows the indwelling Spirit to direct,
  • Can be temporary or consistent
  • Can occur many times

✎ Assignment: How significant is the ministry of the Holy Spirit to the Church and to believers?

D. The Future Ministry and Work of the Holy Spirit

1. During the Great Tribulation

The Bible indicates that the Holy Spirit will continue to work even during the Great Tribulation. Although His restraining ministry through the Church may change, His role in convicting people of sin, bringing them to repentance, empowering God’s servants, and enabling prophetic witness will still be evident. Many will come to faith in God during this difficult period because of the Spirit’s work.

a. Convicting People to Repentance During the Tribulation, the Holy Spirit will continue to convict hearts and lead people to repentance. God will pour out His Spirit, causing many to recognize their sin and turn to Him in humility.

Zechariah 12:10 — “I will pour on the house of David and on the inhabitants of Jerusalem the Spirit of grace and supplication; then they will look on Me whom they pierced.”

b. Outpouring of the Spirit on Many People The prophecy of the Spirit being poured out on all flesh will have continuing significance, especially in the last days. People from different groups will experience visions, dreams, and prophetic messages as the Spirit reveals God’s truth.

Joel 2:28–32 — “I will pour out My Spirit on all flesh; your sons and your daughters shall prophesy… And it shall come to pass that whoever calls on the name of the Lord shall be saved.”

c. Empowering God’s Servants for Ministry The Holy Spirit will empower God’s servants to accomplish His work even in a time of intense persecution. Their strength and success will come not through human ability but through the Spirit’s power.

Zechariah 4:6 — “Not by might nor by power, but by My Spirit, says the Lord of hosts.”

d. Empowering Prophetic Witness During the Tribulation, God will raise up powerful witnesses who will boldly proclaim His message. The Holy Spirit will empower them to perform miracles, testify to the truth, and stand firm even in the face of opposition.

Revelation 11:1–12 — This passage describes the two witnesses who prophesy with great authority and power, demonstrating the Spirit’s enabling presence in their ministry.

Summary During the Great Tribulation, the Holy Spirit will continue to work by:

  • Convicting people of sin and leading them to repentance
  • Being poured out on people in prophetic revelation
  • Empowering God’s servants for ministry
  • Strengthening prophetic witnesses to proclaim God’s truth

Tribulation vs. The Great Tribulation

1. Tribulation

Tribulation means a time of trouble, suffering, persecution, or distress. In the Bible, it can refer to the difficulties believers experience in this present world. Jesus taught that His followers would face tribulation because they live in a fallen world and follow Him.

John 16:33 — “In the world you will have tribulation; but be of good cheer, I have overcome the world.” Meaning in general biblical usage:

So, tribulation can happen at any time in history, including today.

  • Trials and hardships believers face
  • Persecution for faith
  • Difficult circumstances that test faith
  • Suffering in the present age

2. The Great Tribulation

The Great Tribulation refers to a specific future period of unprecedented suffering and judgment before the Second Coming of Jesus Christ. It is described in biblical prophecy as the most intense time of trouble the world has ever experienced.

Matthew 24:21 — “For then there will be great tribulation, such as has not been since the beginning of the world until this time, no, nor ever shall be.” Characteristics of the Great Tribulation:

Important passages describing it include Daniel 12:1; Matthew 24:15–31; and Revelation 6–18.

  • A future prophetic period
  • Severe persecution and global turmoil
  • God’s judgments on the earth
  • Rise of evil powers and deception
  • Many people turning to God during this time
  • Occurs before Christ’s return

Simple Comparison

Tribulation Great Tribulation
General suffering or trials believers face A specific future prophetic period
Happens throughout history Happens near the end of the age
Personal or local persecution Global and unprecedented distress
Mentioned in many New Testament teachings Strongly described in Matthew 24 and Revelation

2. During the Millennial Kingdom

The Millennial Kingdom refers to the future reign of Christ on earth for a thousand years. During this time, the Holy Spirit will actively work in the world by resting upon Christ the King and by transforming the hearts of His people. The Spirit will bring righteousness, peace, spiritual renewal, and obedience to God among the nations.

a. The Spirit Resting on the Messiah (Christ) During the Millennial reign, the Spirit of the Lord will fully rest upon the Messiah, giving Him wisdom, understanding, counsel, might, knowledge, and the fear of the Lord. Under His Spirit-filled rule, justice and righteousness will prevail throughout the earth.

Isaiah 11:1–5 — “There shall come forth a Rod from the stem of Jesse, and a Branch shall grow out of his roots. The Spirit of the Lord shall rest upon Him, the Spirit of wisdom and understanding, the Spirit of counsel and might, the Spirit of knowledge and of the fear of the Lord.” This chapter also describes a time of universal peace, when creation itself experiences harmony under Christ’s rule (Isaiah 11:6–9).

b. The Spirit Bringing Spiritual Renewal to God’s People In the Millennial Kingdom, God will restore His people and give them new hearts. The Holy Spirit will dwell within them, enabling them to obey God and live according to His commandments.

Ezekiel 36:26–27 — “I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit within you… I will put My Spirit within you and cause you to walk in My statutes, and you will keep My judgments and do them.” This passage also speaks of:

  • Restoration of Israel to their land
  • Cleansing from sin
  • Spiritual renewal and obedience
  • God’s blessing and prosperity

Summary During the Millennial Kingdom, the ministry of the Holy Spirit will include:

These prophecies show that the Spirit of God will play a central role in establishing righteousness, peace, and spiritual transformation during Christ’s thousand-year reign.

  • Resting upon Christ the King in His righteous rule
  • Bringing wisdom, justice, and peace to the earth
  • Renewing the hearts of believers
  • Enabling obedience and holy living
  • Restoring God’s people spiritually and physically
3

Hamartiology

The Doctrine of Sin

PREFACE

The doctrine of sin, or Hamartiology, occupies a central place in Christian theology. Any meaningful understanding of salvation, grace, and the work of Christ must begin with a clear and biblical understanding of sin. It is only when the seriousness, depth, and universality of sin are rightly grasped that the beauty and necessity of the gospel can be fully appreciated. This book has been prepared with that foundational conviction.

Purpose of the Book

The primary purpose of this textbook is to provide a clear, systematic, and biblically grounded study of the doctrine of sin. It seeks to guide students through the essential aspects of Hamartiology, including the origin, nature, extent, and consequences of sin, as well as God’s redemptive provision through Jesus Christ.

This work is designed not merely to inform the mind but to shape theological understanding in a way that is faithful to Scripture and applicable to life and ministry. By carefully examining what the Bible teaches about sin, this book aims to equip students to think theologically, teach accurately, and minister effectively.

Target Audience

This book is specifically written for Fusion Bible college students who are being trained for ministry, teaching, and leadership within the church. It assumes a commitment to the authority of Scripture and seeks to present doctrine in a manner that is both academically responsible and spiritually edifying.

At the same time, the content has been structured in a way that is accessible to all serious students of the Word of God. Even those who may not have attended formal classes should be able to follow the material and gain a clear understanding of each concept through its step-by-step development.

Approach: Biblical and Systematic

The approach of this book is both biblical and systematic. Each doctrine is grounded in the teaching of Scripture, with careful attention given to key passages that form the basis of theological understanding. At the same time, the material is organized systematically, allowing students to see how various aspects of the doctrine of sin relate to one another in a coherent framework.

Care has been taken to present truth with clarity and precision, while avoiding unnecessary complexity. Definitions, explanations, and illustrations are provided to help students grasp both the depth and the practical implications of each concept. The goal is not only to present doctrine but to make it understandable and teachable.

Encouragement to Students

As you study this subject, it is important to approach it with both humility and reverence. The doctrine of sin is not merely an abstract theological topic—it is a reality that affects every human life. A proper understanding of sin should not lead to despair, but to a deeper appreciation of God’s grace in Christ.

Let this study lead you to:

A greater awareness of your dependence on God

A deeper gratitude for the salvation provided through Jesus Christ

A renewed commitment to holiness and obedience

A burden to faithfully communicate the truth to others

May this book serve not only as an academic resource but also as a tool for spiritual growth and ministry. As you engage with these truths, may the Holy Spirit guide you into deeper understanding and transform your life for the glory of God.

Soli Deo Gloria
For the Glory of God Alone

CHAPTER 1 — INTRODUCTION TO HAMARTIOLOGY

1.1 Meaning of Hamartiology

Hamartiology is derived from the Greek word “hamartia”, meaning sin. It is the branch of systematic theology that studies:

The origin of sin
Examines how sin first entered creation through the rebellion of Satan and subsequently into humanity through Adam’s disobedience, affirming that God is not the author of sin (Ezekiel 28:15–16; Romans 5:12).

The nature of sin
Defines sin as a moral corruption of human nature, involving thoughts, attitudes, and actions that fall short of or oppose God’s holy character and law (Romans 3:23; 1 John 3:4).

The consequences of sin
Explains that sin results in spiritual death, separation from God, physical death, and eternal judgment apart from Christ (Romans 6:23; Isaiah 59:2).

The solution to sin
Presents God’s redemptive provision through Jesus Christ, whose atoning death and resurrection provide forgiveness, justification, and restoration to all who believe (2 Corinthians 5:21; Romans 5:8–11).

1.2 Importance of Studying Sin

Understanding sin is foundational because:

It explains the human condition
It reveals that humanity is universally fallen in Adam, possessing a sinful nature that affects every aspect of life, rendering all people guilty before God and unable to attain righteousness on their own (Romans 3:9–12, 23).

It reveals the need for salvation
By exposing the depth of human sinfulness and its consequences, it demonstrates that salvation is not optional but essential, as no one can be justified apart from God’s saving intervention through Christ (Romans 5:12; Ephesians 2:1–5).

It magnifies the grace of God
Understanding sin highlights the greatness of God’s grace, showing that salvation is entirely unmerited and flows from God’s love and mercy toward undeserving sinners (Romans 5:8; Ephesians 2:8–9).

It provides clarity on Christian living
A proper understanding of sin equips believers to recognize, resist, and overcome sin through the power of the Holy Spirit, leading to a life of holiness and obedience to God (Romans 6:11–14; Galatians 5:16)

Without a proper understanding of sin, the doctrines of salvation, grace, and redemption lose their depth and urgency.

Review Questions

What is the meaning of the term Hamartiology?

What are the four major areas studied in Hamartiology?

Why is the study of sin essential in systematic theology?

How does the doctrine of sin explain the human condition?

Assignments

Write a short essay (300–500 words):
“Why understanding sin is essential for understanding salvation.”

List and explain two ways a wrong understanding of sin affects Christian living.

CHAPTER 2 — THE REALITY OF SIN

2.1 Modern Denial of Sin

In contemporary theology and culture, sin is often minimized or redefined. Common false views include:

Sin is an illusion
This view, often rooted in certain Eastern philosophies or New Age thought, claims sin has no objective reality but is a mental construct or false perception created by the ego, dissolving when one achieves spiritual enlightenment or unity with the divine. But Scripture affirms that sin is a true moral reality with serious consequences, including guilt, judgment, and death (1 John 1:8; Romans 6:23).

Sin is merely selfishness
Here, sin gets reduced to self-centered behavior that harms others or society, framing it as a psychological flaw like greed or egoism rather than a moral or spiritual offense against God, emphasizing personal improvement over divine judgment. But reducing sin to selfishness ignores its deeper nature as rebellion against God and violation of His holy law (Isaiah 53:6; 1 John 3:4).

Sin is a social construct
Proponents argue sin isn't inherently wrong but defined by cultural, historical, or societal norms that evolve over time, so behaviors labeled sinful in one era or community (like certain dietary rules) might be acceptable elsewhere but the Bible teaches that sin is defined by God’s unchanging character and revealed will, not by human opinion (Romans 3:4).

Sin is only comparative (better/worse than others)
Measuring sin by comparison with others minimizes its seriousness, whereas Scripture declares that all have fallen short of God’s standard, making all equally accountable before Him (Romans 3:23).

Sin applies only to actions, not attitudes
This view ignores the biblical teaching that sin originates in the heart, including thoughts, motives, and desires, not merely outward behavior (Matthew 5:21–28; Mark 7:20–23).

These views distort biblical truth and remove accountability.

2.2 Biblical Affirmation of Sin

2.2.1 Sin is Real

Scripture affirms that sin is not imaginary but a present and active reality (1 John 1:8; Genesis 4:7).

2.2.2 Sin Produces Death

Sin is not harmless—it leads to:

Spiritual death

Physical death

Eternal separation (Romans 6:23; James 1:15)

2.2.3 Sin Separates from God

God is holy, and sin creates a barrier between man and God (Isaiah 59:1–2).

2.2.4 Sin is Ultimately Against God

Even when sin affects others, its ultimate offense is against God (Psalm 51:4).

Review Questions

List and explain two false views of sin.

How does the Bible affirm the reality of sin?

In what ways does sin affect humanity?

Why is sin ultimately against God?

Assignments

Compare: “Sin as defined by society vs sin as defined by Scripture.”

Reflect: Identify one modern belief that minimizes sin and evaluate it biblically.

CHAPTER 3 — THE ORIGIN OF SIN

3.1 God Did Not Create Sin

Sin is not a created substance. It is:

A privation (absence of good)
Sin is not something God created, but the absence or lack of righteousness where it ought to be, representing a failure to reflect God’s holy character (James 1:13; 1 John 1:5).

A corruption of what God made good
Sin is the distortion and perversion of God’s originally good creation, where what was created upright has been twisted away from its intended purpose through rebellion (Genesis 1:31; Ecclesiastes 7:29).

Just as darkness is the absence of light, sin is the absence of righteousness.

3.2 Why God Allowed Sin

God permits sin for His sovereign purposes:

To display His justice and mercy
God allows sin so that His righteous judgment against sin and His gracious mercy toward sinners may both be fully revealed, demonstrating the fullness of His holy character (Romans 9:22–23).

To accomplish divine plans (Genesis 50:20)
Even sinful actions are sovereignly governed by God and used to fulfill His purposes, as He overrules evil intentions to bring about His good and perfect will (Genesis 50:20; Acts 2:23).

To reveal His glory through redemption
The presence of sin provides the context for God to display the glory of His grace in redemption, particularly through the atoning work of Christ, which magnifies His love, wisdom, and power (Ephesians 1:6–10; Romans 5:20–21).

3.3 The Root of Sin: Pride and Free Will

Sin originated through:

Pride (self-exaltation)
Sin began with a desire to exalt oneself above God, as seen in Satan’s rebellion and later in humanity’s fall, where the creature sought independence and glory apart from the Creator (Isaiah 14:13–14; 1 Timothy 3:6).

Free will (choice against God)
Sin was actualized through the misuse of free will, where moral beings freely chose to disobey God’s command, turning from obedience to rebellion (Genesis 3:1–6; Romans 5:12).

3.4 The Entrance of Sin

3.4.1 Sin in Satan

Sin first appeared in Satan (Ezekiel 28:15–16).

3.4.2 Sin in Humanity

Sin entered humanity through Adam’s disobedience (Romans 5:12).

Review Questions

Did God create sin? Explain.

What is meant by sin as a “privation”?

How did sin originate in Satan and humanity?

What role did pride and free will play in the origin of sin?

Assignments

Explain Genesis 3 in your own words focusing on the entry of sin.

Write a theological note:
“God permits sin but is not the author of sin.”

CHAPTER 4 — THE DEFINITION OF SIN

4.1 Classical Definitions

  • Sin is any failure to conform to the moral law of God in act, attitude, or nature – Wayne Grudem
  • Sin is any want of conformity unto, or transgression of, the law of God - Louis Berkhof
  • Sin is anything in the creature which does not express, or which is contrary to, the holy character of the Creator - Charles C. Ryrie
  • Sin is a universal deformity of human nature, found at every point in every person – J I Packer IB

These theological definitions from evangelical scholars converge on sin as a profound breach against God's holy standard, encompassing not just outward actions but inner attitudes, nature, and any failure to align with His perfect moral law and character. Grudem and Berkhof emphasize non-conformity or transgression of God's law; Ryrie highlights deviation from or opposition to God's holiness; Packer stresses its universal corruption in human nature.

4.2 Theological Summary

Sin can be summarized as:

“Any deviation from the character, will, or law of God in thought, word, or deed.”

Sin unfolds like a shadow cast across the radiant canvas of creation, a tragic fracture in humanity's design where the soul, meant for harmony with the Infinite Holy One, twists into rebellion—marred not merely by deeds but by the heart's hidden wanderings and nature's deep-rooted flaw. Yet in this darkness gleams hope's dawn: the cross of Christ, where divine mercy redeems the broken, transforming sinners into saints through grace's boundless light

Review Questions

Compare the definitions of sin by Grudem and Berkhof.

How does Ryrie’s definition shift focus?

What does it mean that sin includes nature, attitude, and action?

Provide a comprehensive definition of sin in your own words.

📝 Assignments

Write your own precise theological definition of sin (2–3 lines).

Explain:
“Sin is more than behavior—it is a condition.”

CHAPTER 5 — THE DESCRIPTION OF SIN

5.1 Biblical Terms for Sin

Scripture uses various expressions to describe sin:

Missing the mark
Sin is the failure to attain God’s perfect standard of righteousness, falling short of His glory in thought, word, and deed (Romans 3:23).

Transgression
Sin is the deliberate crossing of God’s revealed boundaries, a willful violation of His law (Romans 2:23).

Disobedience
Sin is refusing to heed God’s commands, rejecting His authority and rightful rule over life (1 Samuel 28:18).

Rebellion
Sin is active resistance against God, expressing a heart attitude that opposes His rule and seeks independence (Isaiah 59:13).

Lawlessness
Sin is living as though there is no divine law, disregarding God’s moral order and authority (1 John 3:4).

Unrighteousness
Sin is any condition or action that is contrary to God’s perfect righteousness and justice (Romans 3:5).

Godlessness
Sin is living without regard for God, excluding Him from life and denying His rightful place (Romans 1:18).

Iniquity
Sin reflects inner moral corruption and twistedness of heart, indicating a deep-rooted depravity (Psalm 51:2).

Evil
Sin is that which is morally wrong and offensive in God’s sight, opposing His holy nature (Habakkuk 1:13).

Each term highlights a different dimension of sin.

5.2 Sin in Practice (Specific Sins)

Moral and Social Sins

Hatred, jealousy, anger

Envy, strife, deceit

Verbal Sins

Slander

Gossip

Corrupt speech

Sexual and Behavioral Sins

Immorality

Greed

Drunkenness

Sins of Omission

Not helping others

Not praying

Not acting in faith

5.3 Total Scope of Sin

Sin includes:

Thoughts
Sin originates in the inner life of the mind, where sinful imaginations, desires, and intentions are formed, even before they are expressed outwardly (Matthew 5:28; 2 Corinthians 10:5).

Attitudes
Sin is present in the heart’s disposition, including pride, unbelief, bitterness, and wrong motives, which shape one’s relationship with God and others (Proverbs 21:2; Hebrews 3:12).

Actions
Sin is manifested through outward behavior that violates God’s commands, whether in words or deeds, making visible what is rooted within (Galatians 5:19–21; James 1:15).

Omissions
Sin also includes failing to do what is right, neglecting God’s commands or opportunities to act in obedience and love (James 4:17; Matthew 25:41–45)

Review Questions

What does “missing the mark” mean?

Differentiate between transgression and rebellion.

What are sins of omission? Give examples.

How do thoughts and attitudes relate to sin?

Assignments

Create a chart:
Types of sin (thought, attitude, action, omission) with examples

Study Matthew 5:21–28 and explain how Jesus deepens the understanding of sin

CHAPTER 6 — THE NATURE AND STRATEGY OF SIN

6.1 Deceptive Nature of Sin

Sin deceives by:

Hiding consequences
Sin conceals its true outcomes, presenting itself as harmless or beneficial while masking the inevitable results of guilt, corruption, and judgment (Hebrews 3:13; James 1:14–15).

Distorting truth
Sin twists God’s Word and reality, leading individuals to doubt God’s character, misinterpret His commands, and justify disobedience (Genesis 3:1–5; 2 Corinthians 11:3).

Promising false satisfaction
Sin offers temporary pleasure and fulfillment but ultimately leaves the soul empty, dissatisfied, and further enslaved (Hebrews 11:25; Galatians 6:7–8)

6.2 Enslaving Power of Sin

Sin leads to bondage:

Habit formation
Repeated sinful choices gradually form patterns and habits, hardening the heart and making sin increasingly normal and difficult to resist (Hebrews 3:13; Romans 6:19).

Loss of control
What begins as a voluntary act can become dominating, where the individual loses moral and spiritual control and becomes mastered by sinful desires (2 Peter 2:19; Romans 7:15–20).

Spiritual slavery (John 8:34)
Sin ultimately enslaves the sinner, placing them under its power and authority, so that apart from Christ they are unable to free themselves or live in true righteousness (Romans 6:16–17).

6.3 Destructive Outcome of Sin

Sin ultimately destroys:

Life
Sin brings corruption and death into human existence, affecting both physical life and spiritual vitality, ultimately leading to separation from the life that is found in God (Romans 6:23; James 1:15).

Relationships
Sin disrupts and damages relationships—with God and with others—producing alienation, conflict, broken trust, and division (Isaiah 59:2; Genesis 3:12–13).

Eternal destiny
If unrepented of, sin results in eternal judgment, separating individuals from God forever and determining their destiny apart from His saving grace (Revelation 20:14–15; 2 Thessalonians 1:8–9)

Review Questions

How does sin deceive individuals?

What are the stages of sin’s progression?

How does sin lead to bondage?

In what ways does sin destroy life and relationships?

Assignments

Trace the temptation cycle from James 1:14–15.

Reflect: Describe a real-life example of how sin deceives and enslaves.

CHAPTER 7 — THE RESULTS OF SIN

7.1 Separation from God

Sin causes:

Alienation
Sin results in a state of estrangement from God, where humanity is cut off from fellowship with Him and stands outside His favor due to guilt and unrighteousness (Colossians 1:21).

Spiritual distance
Sin creates a barrier between God and man, hindering communion and intimacy with Him, as His holiness cannot coexist with sin (Isaiah 59:2).

Enmity with God
Sin places humanity in opposition to God, making sinners not merely distant but actively hostile in mind and disposition toward Him (Romans 5:10; Romans 8:7)

7.2 Spiritual Death

Man becomes:

Dead in sin
Spiritual death is the condition in which a person is separated from God and devoid of spiritual life, existing under sin’s power and unable to attain righteousness (Ephesians 2:1; Colossians 2:13).

Spiritually unresponsive
In this state, the individual is unable to perceive, understand, or respond to spiritual truths apart from God’s grace, being insensitive to His voice and will (1 Corinthians 2:14; Romans 8:7–8)

7.3 Condemnation

All humanity stands:

Guilty before God
Because all have sinned, every person stands legally guilty before God’s righteous standard, unable to justify themselves by their own works (Romans 3:19–23).

Under divine judgment
As a result of this guilt, humanity is subject to God’s just judgment, awaiting condemnation apart from the saving work of Christ (John 3:18; Romans 5:16)

7.4 Universal Condition “In Adam”

All humans:

Inherit sin
Through Adam’s fall, all humanity inherits a sinful nature, being born with a disposition inclined toward sin and rebellion against God (Romans 5:19; Psalm 51:5).

Share in death
As a result of Adam’s sin, death—both physical and spiritual—has passed to all people, making mortality a universal human experience (Romans 5:12; 1 Corinthians 15:21–22).

Live under curse
Humanity exists under the curse brought by sin, experiencing suffering, toil, and separation from God until redemption is found in Christ (Genesis 3:17–19; Galatians 3:10)

7.5 Eternal Consequences

Without redemption:

Eternal separation
Those who remain in sin are eternally separated from the presence and fellowship of God, excluded from His kingdom and the fullness of life in Him (2 Thessalonians 1:8–9).

Judgment
All unbelievers will stand before God’s righteous judgment, where their sins are justly evaluated and condemned according to His perfect justice (Hebrews 9:27; Revelation 20:11–12).

Lake of fire
The final destiny of the unredeemed is the lake of fire, representing eternal punishment and the ultimate expression of God’s justice against sin (Revelation 20:14–15)

Review Questions

What does separation from God involve?

Define spiritual death.

What is condemnation?

Explain the meaning of being “in Adam.”

What are the eternal consequences of sin?

Assignments

Write a paragraph explaining Romans 5:12–21.

Create a diagram:
Sin → Death → Judgment → Eternal Consequences

CHAPTER 8 — SIN AND THE UNBELIEVER

8.1 Threefold Problem of Sin

8.1.1 Inherited Sin Nature

Passed from Adam
The sin nature originates from Adam, the representative head of humanity. When Adam fell, his nature became corrupted, and this fallen nature is transmitted to all his descendants through natural generation. Thus, every human being is born not morally neutral, but already inclined away from God (Romans 5:19; Psalm 51:5; Ephesians 2:3).

Inclines man toward sin
The inherited sin nature is a deep-rooted inner disposition that bends the human will, mind, and affections toward sin and self rather than toward God. It affects every aspect of human personality (often described as total depravity), making man unable, apart from grace, to seek or please God (Romans 8:7–8; Jeremiah 17:9).
This explains why people sin—not merely by choice, but because they are sinners by nature.

The sin nature reveals that sin is not just what we do, but who we are by birth, emphasizing the necessity of regeneration (new birth).

Illustrations to Understand Inherited Sin

1. The Genetic Disease Illustration

To understand inherited sin, consider the analogy of a genetic disorder. Imagine a child born to a parent who carries a chronic, hereditary blood disease. The child did not choose the condition, nor did they do anything to acquire it. Yet, because they are the biological offspring of that parent, the disease is present in their very nature from the moment of conception.

Theological Truth:
This illustrates inherited sin. Scripture teaches that we are “born in sin” (Psalm 51:5). Sin is like a spiritual “inheritance” passed down from Adam, resulting in a natural inclination toward evil. We do not become sinners only when we commit acts of sin; rather, we commit acts of sin because we are born with a sinful nature.

2. The Poisoned Well Illustration

Consider a mountain spring that serves as the source of a flowing stream. If someone poisons the source, every drop of water that flows from it—no matter how far downstream—will be contaminated. The water is not merely occasionally polluted; it is corrupted at its very source.

Theological Truth:
This reflects the doctrine of inherited sin. The human heart, as the source of thoughts, words, and actions, is corrupted by sin. Therefore, everything that proceeds from it is affected by that corruption (Jeremiah 17:9; Mark 7:20–23). Sin is not merely external behavior but flows from an inwardly fallen nature

8.1.2 Imputed Sin

Adam’s guilt credited to all humanity
Imputed sin refers to the judicial act of God by which Adam’s original sin is credited (reckoned) to the account of all humanity. As the covenant head, Adam acted representatively; therefore, his disobedience is legally counted as the disobedience of all (Romans 5:12–14, 18–19).
This is a forensic (legal) reality, not merely experiential.

Results in physical death
Because of this imputed guilt, all humanity comes under the sentence of death, resulting in universal physical death. Death is not merely natural but judicial—the penalty of sin (1 Corinthians 15:21–22; Romans 6:23).
Even those who have not consciously sinned still die, demonstrating the reality of imputed guilt.

Imputed sin highlights humanity’s legal standing before God—guilty and condemned in Adam—thereby preparing the foundation for understanding justification, where Christ’s righteousness is imputed to believers.

Illustrations to Understand Imputed Sin

1. The Joint Bank Account Illustration

Imagine a father opens a joint bank account and takes out a massive line of credit, spending millions of rupees and accumulating a debt he can never repay. Because his children are legally connected to his account and estate, the bank imputes (credits) that debt to them. Legally, they are held responsible for the debt their father incurred, even though they were not present when he spent the money.

This illustrates imputed sin. Because Adam was our federal head (our legal representative), God has legally reckoned his act of rebellion to our account (Romans 5:12). Therefore, every human being stands in a forensic (legal) position of guilt before God—not because of personal actions alone, but because of Adam’s representative act.

2. The Exiled Family Illustration

Consider a kingdom where a man commits treason against the king. In response, the king declares not only the man but his entire family line to be exiled from the kingdom. Generations later, a grandson is born in a foreign land. He has never personally rebelled against the king, yet he is still born an exile, with no legal right to enter the king’s presence.

This reflects our condition in Adam. Because of Adam’s rebellion, all humanity is born in a state of legal exclusion and condemnation before God. Even before committing personal sins, we are already considered “outsiders” with respect to God’s kingdom (Romans 5:18).

We are not sinners only because we commit acts of sin; Rather, we commit acts of sin because we are already constituted sinners in Adam—both by imputed guilt and inherited sinful nature

8.1.3 Personal Sins

Individual acts of sin
Personal sins are the actual sins committed by individuals in thought, word, and deed. These include both acts of commission (doing what is wrong) and omission (failing to do what is right) (James 4:17; Galatians 5:19–21).

Confirm human guilt
Personal sins do not make a person a sinner; rather, they demonstrate and confirm the sinful nature and imputed guilt already present. They provide visible evidence of humanity’s fallen condition and justify God’s righteous judgment (Romans 3:23; Revelation 20:12)

Personal sins establish individual accountability and highlight the need for forgiveness through Christ’s atoning work, as each person is judged according to their deeds+

8.2 Summary Table

Cause Result Cure
Inherited Sin Spiritual Death Redemption
Imputed Sin Physical Death Justification
Personal Sin Spiritual Death Forgiveness

Review Questions

Define inherited sin nature.

What is imputed sin?

How do personal sins confirm human guilt?

Differentiate between inherited, imputed, and personal sins.

Assignments

Explain the “Joint Bank Account” illustration in your own words.

Write a short essay:
“Why man is a sinner by nature, by imputation, and by action.”

Compare:
In Adam vs In Christ (Romans 5:12–21)

CHAPTER 9 — SIN AND THE BELIEVER

9.1 Complete Forgiveness

The believer’s sins:

Past
All sins committed before salvation are fully forgiven through the atoning work of Christ, removing guilt and condemnation (Colossians 2:13–14).

Present
The believer’s current sins are already covered by Christ’s finished work, though they still require confession for the restoration of fellowship (1 John 1:7–9).

Future
Even sins yet to be committed have been judicially dealt with at the cross, ensuring that the believer’s standing before God remains secure (Hebrews 10:10–14).

Forgiveness is complete and final in terms of justification, though ongoing confession is necessary for fellowship.

9.2 The Sin Nature

Condemned at the cross
The believer’s sin nature was judged and rendered powerless in principle through Christ’s death, breaking its authority (Romans 8:1–3).

Not yet removed
Although its power is broken, the sin nature still resides in the believer’s mortal body, resulting in ongoing spiritual conflict (Galatians 5:16–17).

Will be removed in glorification
The sin nature will be completely eradicated when the believer is glorified, receiving a perfected nature free from sin (Philippians 3:20–21).

The believer lives in the tension of “already defeated, but not yet eradicated” sin.

9.3 Fellowship vs Relationship

Sin breaks fellowship, not relationship
When a believer sins, it disrupts communion and intimacy with God, but does not sever the established relationship as a child of God (1 John 1:6–7).

Salvation remains secure
The believer’s salvation is grounded in Christ’s finished work and God’s promise, not in human performance, ensuring eternal security (John 10:28–29; Romans 8:38–39).

Relationship is judicial and permanent; fellowship is experiential and variable.

9.4 Key Truths

Believer is dead to sin
Through union with Christ, the believer has died to sin’s ruling power and is no longer under its authority (Romans 6:6–11).

Sin no longer has dominion
Sin’s mastery has been broken, and the believer is no longer enslaved but is free to live in righteousness (Romans 6:14).

Holy Spirit empowers victory
The indwelling Holy Spirit enables the believer to resist sin, pursue holiness, and live a transformed life (Galatians 5:16; Romans 8:13).

Victory over sin is not achieved by human effort alone but through dependence on the Spirit and union with Christ.

Review Questions

What does complete forgiveness mean?

What is the present condition of the sin nature in believers?

Differentiate between fellowship and relationship.

What are the key truths about the believer and sin?

Assignments

Explain:
“Why a believer’s sin does not cancel salvation.”

Reflect: How does understanding forgiveness affect your spiritual life?

CHAPTER 10 — THE BELIEVER’S VICTORY OVER SIN

Basis of Victory

  • The believer’s victory over sin is not rooted in human effort, but in divine provision. It rests on the following foundational realities:

Union with Christ
The believer is spiritually united with Christ in His death and resurrection, sharing in His victory over sin. This union breaks sin’s authority and establishes a new identity in righteousness (Romans 6:5–11; 2 Corinthians 5:17).

Indwelling Holy Spirit
The Holy Spirit resides within the believer, providing the power to resist sin, overcome the flesh, and live a life pleasing to God (Romans 8:9–13; Galatians 5:16).

Knowledge of truth
Victory begins with understanding and believing God’s truth. As the believer renews the mind with Scripture, he is able to discern error, resist temptation, and live according to God’s will (John 8:32; Romans 12:2).

Victory is based on who the believer is in Christ, not merely on what the believer does

Means of Victory

  • While victory is grounded in divine provision, it is experienced through active participation and obedience:
  • Walking by the Spirit
    The believer must consciously depend on and yield to the Holy Spirit, allowing Him to guide thoughts, desires, and actions, thereby overcoming the desires of the flesh (Galatians 5:16–18).
  • Renewing the mind
    Transformation occurs as the believer continually replaces worldly thinking with biblical truth, reshaping attitudes, desires, and decisions (Romans 12:2; Ephesians 4:22–24).
  • Obedience to God’s Word
    Victory over sin is expressed through practical obedience to Scripture, as the believer submits to God’s commands and aligns life with His revealed will (James 1:22; John 14:15).
  • Sanctification involves both divine enablement and human responsibility

Daily Christian Living

  • Victory over sin must be lived out in everyday life through intentional spiritual discipline:

Put off the old self
The believer is called to reject and abandon former sinful patterns, attitudes, and behaviors that belong to the old nature (Colossians 3:5–9).

Put on righteousness
The believer must actively cultivate Christlike character, adopting new attitudes and actions that reflect God’s holiness (Ephesians 4:24; Colossians 3:10–14).

Pursue holiness
The Christian life is a continual pursuit of holiness, striving to live in a manner that is set apart for God and pleasing in His sight (Hebrews 12:14; 1 Peter 1:15–16).

Sanctification is both a decisive break with sin and a progressive growth in holiness.

Review Questions

What are the foundations of victory over sin?

How does the Holy Spirit enable victory?

What does it mean to walk by the Spirit?

Explain “put off” and “put on” in Christian living.

Assignments

Write a practical guide:
“Steps to overcome sin in daily life.”

Study Galatians 5:16–26 and summarize the contrast between flesh and Spirit.

CHAPTER 11 — PRACTICAL THEOLOGY OF SIN

11.1 When a Believer Sins

Even though the believer is justified and positionally righteous in Christ, the presence of the sin nature means that he may still fall into sin. God has provided a clear and gracious pathway for restoration of fellowship.

Restoration Process

Confession
Confession is the honest acknowledgment of sin before God, agreeing with His judgment concerning it without excuse or denial. It involves a humble recognition of wrongdoing and dependence on God’s mercy (1 John 1:9; Psalm 32:5).

Repentance
Repentance is a genuine turning away from sin and a turning toward God. It includes a change of mind, heart, and direction, resulting in a renewed commitment to obedience (Acts 3:19; 2 Corinthians 7:10).

Faith in Christ’s finished work
The believer must rest in the sufficiency of Christ’s atoning sacrifice, trusting that forgiveness has already been secured through His death and resurrection. Restoration is not achieved by human effort but by faith in Christ’s completed work (Hebrews 10:14; 1 John 2:1–2).

Restoration restores fellowship, not salvation, reaffirming the believer’s secure position in Christ

11.2 Ongoing Struggle

The Christian life involves a continuous conflict between the flesh and the Spirit. This struggle is normal and expected in the process of sanctification.

The flesh remains
The sinful nature continues to reside in the believer, opposing God’s will and producing inner conflict (Galatians 5:17; Romans 7:18–23).

The Spirit empowers
The indwelling Holy Spirit provides the strength necessary to resist sin and live in obedience, enabling the believer to overcome the desires of the flesh (Romans 8:12–13; Galatians 5:16).

Victory is progressive
Spiritual growth is gradual. Victory over sin is not instantaneous perfection but a continual process of transformation as the believer matures in Christ (2 Corinthians 3:18; Philippians 1:6).

Sanctification is a lifelong process marked by struggle, growth, and increasing conformity to Christ.

11.3 Final Victory

The believer’s ultimate victory over sin will be fully realized in the future, when salvation is brought to completion

Three Dimensions of Salvation:

Penalty removed (Justification)
At salvation, the believer is declared righteous before God, and the penalty of sin is completely removed through Christ’s substitutionary sacrifice (Romans 5:1; Romans 8:1).

Power broken (Sanctification)
In the present life, the power of sin is progressively broken as the believer lives under the influence of the Holy Spirit (Romans 6:14; Galatians 5:16).

Presence removed (Glorification)
In the future, the very presence of sin will be entirely removed, and the believer will be perfected in holiness, free from all sin forever (1 John 3:2; Philippians 3:20–21).

Salvation is past, present, and future, culminating in complete and final victory over sin.

Review Questions

What are the steps in restoring fellowship with God?

What is the role of confession and repentance?

Why is sanctification progressive?

Explain justification, sanctification, and glorification.

What does the doctrine of sin reveal about humanity?

How does sin highlight the grace of God?

Why is Christ’s work necessary?

How should believers respond to the doctrine of sin?

Assignments

Write a personal reflection:
“How should a believer respond after sinning?”

Create a chart:
Past–Present–Future Salvation

Write a final essay (800–1000 words):
“The Doctrine of Sin and Its Importance in Christian Theology and Life.”

Prepare a 10-minute teaching outline on:
“From Sin to Salvation.”

FINAL SUMMARY

The doctrine of sin reveals:

The depth of human depravity
It exposes the pervasive corruption of human nature, showing that sin affects every aspect of our being and leaves us incapable of attaining righteousness apart from God’s grace (Romans 3:10–12, 23).

The justice of God
It demonstrates that God is perfectly holy and righteous, and therefore must judge sin with absolute fairness, upholding His moral law and divine character (Romans 2:5–6; 2 Thessalonians 1:6).

The necessity of Christ’s work
It highlights that human effort is insufficient to overcome sin, making the atoning death and resurrection of Jesus Christ the only means of salvation and reconciliation with God (Romans 5:8–11; 2 Corinthians 5:21).

The power of grace in transformation
It reveals that God’s grace not only forgives sin but also transforms the believer, enabling a new life of righteousness through the work of the Holy Spirit (Titus 2:11–12; Romans 6:4)

A proper understanding of sin leads not to despair, but to:

Humility
Recognizing one’s sinfulness removes pride and fosters a humble acknowledgment of total dependence on God (James 4:6).

Dependence on God
Understanding the inability to overcome sin apart from divine help leads the believer to rely on God’s grace, Word, and Spirit (John 15:5).

Gratitude for salvation
Awareness of the seriousness of sin deepens appreciation for the salvation provided through Christ, producing thanksgiving and worship (Colossians 2:13–14).

Pursuit of holiness
A right understanding of sin motivates believers to live in obedience and pursue a life that reflects God’s holiness and character (1 Peter 1:15–16).

5

OT & NT Survey

The Bible Panorama — a visual survey from Creation to Eternity

The Bible Panorama wall chart, photographed and arranged in chronological order — a visual survey of the whole Bible from creation to the new heaven. The flow: Creation → Eden & the Fall → Flood & Babel → Abraham to Moses → Judges & Kings → Captivity & Return → Life of Christ → Cross & Resurrection → Acts → Church History → Eternity.

01. In the Beginning: The Seven Days of Creation

In the Bible

Genesis opens with the foundational claim of all Scripture: 'In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth' (Genesis 1:1). Creation unfolds in an ordered week — forming realms in the first three days (light; sky and waters; land, sea, and plant life) and then filling them in the next three (sun, moon, and stars; fish and birds; land animals and finally man, made in God's image, Genesis 1:26–27). Each stage closes with God's verdict 'it was good,' and the finished whole is 'very good.' On the seventh day God rests — not from fatigue but as the crown of a completed work, setting the pattern of the Sabbath (Genesis 2:1–3; Exodus 20:11).

Key notes on this panel

  • Day 1 — Creation of light (Genesis 1:3–5)
  • Day 2 — Heavens and the waters (Genesis 1:6–8)
  • Day 3 — Division of earth and sea; plant life (Genesis 1:11–12)
  • Day 4 — Sun and moon to light the day and night (Genesis 1:14–15)
  • Day 5 — Fish and birds (Genesis 1:20–23)
  • Day 6 — Land animals and man in God's image (Genesis 1:24–30)
  • Day 7 — 'Thus the heavens and the earth were finished' — GOD RESTS (Genesis 2:1–3)
  • The shaft of light from heaven striking the dark globe pictures God's word bringing order out of darkness

The world at this time

Creation itself precedes all datable history, but Genesis was heard by people surrounded by rival creation stories. Mesopotamia told of gods battling chaos monsters (the Babylonian Enuma Elish); Egypt had its own myths of gods emerging from primeval waters. Against that background Genesis 1 is strikingly different: one sovereign God, no rivals, no struggle — He simply speaks and it is so. The sun, moon, and stars, worshiped as deities by Israel's neighbours, are demoted to created lamps. Genesis 1 is therefore both revelation and polemic: it tells who made the world and quietly dethrones every idol of the ancient Near East.

02. The Creation of Man, Eden, the Fall & Cain

In the Bible

Man is created in God's image and given the mandate to 'be fruitful and increase in number... rule over the fish of the sea and the birds of the air' (Genesis 1:28). God places him in the Garden of Eden with one prohibition — the tree of the knowledge of good and evil: 'when you eat of it you will surely die' (Genesis 2:17). Through the serpent's temptation Adam and Eve fall, and sin and separation enter human history, bringing both a curse (Genesis 3:14–19) and a promise — the protoevangelium, the first announcement of the gospel: the offspring of the woman will crush the serpent (Genesis 3:15). God drives out the man, guarding the way to the tree of life (Genesis 3:24). Outside Eden the fall bears immediate fruit: Cain murders Abel, yet grace continues — 'God has granted me another child in place of Abel' (Genesis 4:25), and through Seth the godly line runs on toward Christ.

Key notes on this panel

  • Twin signposts 'The Curse' (Genesis 3:14–19) and 'The Promise' (Genesis 3:15) — the hinge of the whole chart: from the fall onward, redemption history begins
  • Two trees stand opposite each other: the Tree of Knowledge (temptation) and the Tree of Life (guarded after the fall)
  • The dark 'SIN — THE FALL' chasm splits the panorama — every later panel is God bridging this gap
  • The angel with the sword: 'God drove out the man' (Genesis 3:24)
  • Cain's box begins the line east of Eden; the promise-line continues through Seth

The world at this time

Genesis locates Eden by the Tigris and Euphrates rivers (Genesis 2:14) — the very river plain where the world's earliest cities later rise. It is no accident that Sumer, the first urban civilization, with the first writing (cuneiform, before 3000 BC), appears in that same Mesopotamian corridor. Ancient literatures across the world preserve the memory of a lost golden age and a broken relationship with the divine; Genesis gives the sober, historical account behind the echoes. The genealogies of Genesis 4–5 also record the beginnings of civilization itself — city-building, livestock herding, music, and metalwork (Genesis 4:17–22).

03. The Flood & the Tower of Babel

In the Bible

By Noah's day 'the Lord saw how great man's wickedness on the earth had become... every inclination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil all the time' (Genesis 6:5), and He announced judgment: 'I will wipe mankind, whom I have created, from the face of the earth' (Genesis 6:7). Noah, warned by God, prepares the ark and preaches to a perishing generation. God saves Noah and his sons because of His promise to Adam and Eve, and after the flood renews the creation mandate: 'Be fruitful and increase in number and fill the earth' (Genesis 9:1). From Shem, Ham, and Japheth come all the peoples scattered over the earth (Genesis 9:18–19). Instead of filling the earth, however, mankind gathers at Babel: 'Come, let us build ourselves a city, with a tower that reaches to the heavens, so that we may make a name for ourselves' (Genesis 11:1–4). God confuses their language and scatters them (Genesis 11:5–9) — languages and nations are the result of the confusion and scattering.

Key notes on this panel

  • The ark rests as the flood waters recede — one door, one way of salvation (a picture of Christ, 1 Peter 3:20–21)
  • Three roads stream from the ark — Shem, Ham, and Japheth — becoming the Semitic, Hamitic, and Japhetic peoples labeled across the map (Africa/Arabia, Asia Minor, Assyria)
  • Babel is drawn as a ziggurat — the stepped temple-tower of Mesopotamia
  • The rainbow arc over the ark recalls God's covenant never again to destroy the earth by flood (Genesis 9)

The world at this time

Mesopotamian tradition itself preserves a flood memory: tablet XI of the Epic of Gilgamesh tells of a man warned by deity to build a boat and save life from a great deluge — a pagan echo of the true event Genesis records. The tower of Babel has a concrete architectural background: the ziggurats of Sumer and Babylon, man-made mountains of baked brick and bitumen (compare Genesis 11:3, 'brick instead of stone, tar for mortar'). The Table of Nations in Genesis 10 reads like a map of the ancient world — Egypt, Cush, Canaan, Asshur (Assyria), Elam, Javan (Greece) — the very nations that will fill the rest of biblical history.

04. From Babel to Abraham, Egypt & Moses

In the Bible

Around 2000 BC God calls one man out of Ur of the Chaldees: 'All peoples on earth will be blessed through you' (Genesis 12:1–3) — the covenant that drives the rest of the Bible. God tells Abraham his descendants will be in bondage 400 years (Genesis 15:13). The line runs through Isaac (not Ishmael, father of the Arabs, Genesis 17:20) and Jacob (not Esau, father of the Edomites): Jacob becomes Israel, and his twelve sons the twelve tribes. Seventy persons go down into Egypt in Joseph's day; four hundred years later some two and a half million come out under Moses. The exodus — Passover, the Red Sea crossing, Sinai, Kadesh-Barnea, and the wilderness wanderings — turns a family into a nation and gives them the Law (Exodus–Deuteronomy).

Key notes on this panel

  • Ur of the Chaldees is drawn as a walled Sumerian city — Abraham left one of the great civilizations of his age by faith (Hebrews 11:8)
  • Follow the family ribbons: Abraham → Isaac (and Ishmael → the Arabs) → Jacob/Israel (and Esau → Edomites) → the twelve tribes fanning out
  • The road bends down into Egypt ('70 went into Egypt') and back out ('2.5 million came out... 400 years later')
  • Moses, the Red Sea, Marah/Elim, and Kadesh-Barnea mark the exodus route; the book bar reads Genesis 12–50 (Job?), Exodus (Leviticus)

The world at this time

Abraham's Ur was a real and splendid place — a leading Sumerian city whose great ziggurat (built under the Third Dynasty of Ur, c. 2100 BC) still stands in southern Iraq. His journey belongs to the Middle Bronze Age, the world of city-states along the Fertile Crescent; the Code of Hammurabi of Babylon (c. 1750 BC) shows the kind of law culture the patriarchs moved through. Joseph's rise fits the era when Semitic peoples (the Hyksos among them) held power in Egypt's delta. On the chart's early-date chronology the exodus falls about 1446 BC, in the days of Egypt's mighty Eighteenth Dynasty — the empire of the Thutmosids — while the alternative late date places it under Ramesses II (c. 1270 BC). Either way, Israel leaves the greatest superpower of the age, humbled by the God of slaves.

05. The Judges, the United Kingdom & the Kingdom Divided

In the Bible

After the conquest under Joshua (c. 1400 BC), Israel cycles through the era of the Judges — military leaders who helped regain the land — summarized bleakly: 'In those days Israel had no king; everyone did as he saw fit' (Judges 21:25). Samuel anoints Saul (1050 BC), and a united kingdom stands under Saul, David (1010 BC), and Solomon (970 BC) — forty years each. To David God gives the covenant of an everlasting throne (2 Samuel 7:12–16), the promise that finally rests on Christ. Samuel's word to Saul rings over the whole era: 'To obey is better than sacrifice' (1 Samuel 15:22). Under Solomon Israel reaches the peak of its power and builds the temple, but immediately after his death the decline is rapid and tragic: in 930 BC the kingdom divides — Jeroboam takes the north (Israel/Samaria, ten tribes, nineteen kings, all evil), Rehoboam the south (Judah, two tribes — Judah and Benjamin — nineteen kings and one queen, only eight good). Elijah and Elisha thunder in the north as 'speaking' prophets; Hosea, Amos, and Jonah write to Israel, Joel and Obadiah to Judah.

Key notes on this panel

  • The date line runs 1400 → 1390 → 1050 → 1010 → 970 → 930 BC — memorize these six anchors and the whole Old Testament falls into place
  • Three crowns mark Saul, David, and Solomon (40 years each); the temple sketch stands at Solomon's reign
  • The road literally forks at 'Kingdom Divided': Jeroboam's yellow marker goes north to Samaria, Rehoboam's south to Jerusalem
  • The map shows the split land: Northern (Samaria, ten tribes) and Southern (Judah and Benjamin, Jerusalem)
  • The book bar underneath tracks where you are reading: Judges–Ruth → 1 Samuel → 2 Samuel (Psalms) → 1 Kings 1–11 (Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, Song of Solomon) → 1 Kings 12–2 Kings 16

The world at this time

Israel's monarchy rises in a rare gap between empires. Around 1200 BC the Late Bronze Age world collapsed — the Sea Peoples (among them the Philistines, Israel's chief foe in Judges and Samuel) battered Egypt and destroyed the Hittite empire — leaving no superpower to dominate Canaan. Into that vacuum David and Solomon built a regional power. Solomon's temple was raised with help from Hiram of Tyre, whose Phoenician craftsmen and cedar were the finest of the age; the same Phoenicians were then spreading the alphabet around the Mediterranean. By the time the kingdom split in 930 BC, a revived Assyria was beginning the expansion that would eventually swallow the northern kingdom.

06. Captivity, Return & the 400 Silent Years

In the Bible

Because of its persistent disobedience the northern kingdom falls first: in 721 BC King Sargon II of Assyria carries Israel into captivity. Judah lasts until 586 BC, when Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon destroys Jerusalem and the temple — Isaiah, Micah, Nahum, Zephaniah, Jeremiah, and Habakkuk had warned Judah; Ezekiel and Daniel prophesy in Babylon itself (Daniel receives the vision of the Seventy Weeks, Daniel 9:24). But God preserves a remnant. King Cyrus' decree opens the way home: Zerubbabel returns in 536 BC to rebuild the temple, Ezra in 458 BC to restore the Law, Nehemiah in 444 BC to rebuild the walls — 'So the wall was completed on the twenty-fifth of Elul, in fifty-two days' (Nehemiah 6:15). Haggai and Zechariah preach to the returning remnant; Malachi, the last Old Testament prophet, closes the canon with a promise: 'See, I will send my messenger, who will prepare the way before me... I will send you the prophet Elijah before that great and dreadful day of the LORD comes' (Malachi 3:1; 4:5). Then — four hundred years of silence, from Malachi to the New Testament.

Key notes on this panel

  • Three dates crown the panel: 721 BC (Assyrian captivity), 586 BC (Babylonian captivity), 444 BC (Nehemiah's wall)
  • King Cyrus' decree is drawn as the turning point — the road bends home at that scroll
  • The wall-building scene above 'Nehemiah' pictures the fifty-two-day rebuild of Jerusalem
  • The great red '400 YEARS — Silence' banner ends the Old Testament side of the chart, with Malachi's messenger promise beside it pointing straight to John the Baptist
  • The book bar: 2 Kings 17–25 (Lamentations) → Ezra (Esther) → Nehemiah → 400 years of silence

The world at this time

This panel spans the great parade of empires. Assyria, the terror of the age, falls in turn when Nineveh is destroyed in 612 BC; Babylon under Nebuchadnezzar becomes the wonder of the world — then falls in a single night to Cyrus the Great of Persia in 539 BC. Cyrus' famous policy of returning exiled peoples to their homelands (recorded on the Cyrus Cylinder) is exactly what Ezra 1 describes. Esther's story unfolds at the Persian court between the returns. The 'silent' years are anything but empty: Alexander the Great conquers the East (332 BC) and Greek becomes the common tongue; the Scriptures are translated into Greek (the Septuagint, from c. 250 BC); the Seleucid king Antiochus IV desecrates the temple, provoking the Maccabean revolt (167 BC) and a century of Jewish independence; then Rome arrives — Pompey takes Jerusalem in 63 BC, and Herod the Great rules when the silence finally breaks. Synagogues, Pharisees, Sadducees, Greek roads and Roman peace — God spent four hundred quiet years setting the stage for the gospel.

07. The Life of Christ: Bethlehem to Golgotha

In the Bible

'Today in the town of David a Savior has been born to you; he is Christ the Lord' (Luke 2:11). The panel walks the road of the gospel: John the Baptist comes as the promised voice (Matthew 3:1–3 — Malachi's messenger at last); at Jesus' baptism the Father speaks — 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased' (Matthew 3:17); the temptations follow, and 'because he himself suffered when he was tempted, he is able to help those who are being tempted' (Hebrews 2:18). His public ministry astonishes: 'he taught them as one who had authority, not as the teachers of the law' (Mark 1:22). He appoints twelve 'that they might be with him and that he might send them out to preach' (Mark 3:14). Then the final week: the triumphal entry — 'See, your king comes to you, gentle and riding on a donkey' (Matthew 21:5); the Last Supper — 'Take and eat; this is my body... this is my blood of the covenant' (Matthew 26:26, 28); Gethsemane — 'Yet not as I will, but as you will' (Matthew 26:39); the trial — 'Are you the king of the Jews?' asked Pilate; and Golgotha.

Key notes on this panel

  • The panel is a chain of framed scenes: Birth → Baptism → Temptations → Public Ministry → Training of the Twelve → Triumphal Entry → Last Supper → Gethsemane → Trial → Golgotha
  • The yellow shaft from heaven at the baptism is the Father's voice (Matthew 3:17)
  • Under each scene the reference bar gives the parallel Gospel passages (e.g. Birth: Matthew 1–2, Luke 1–2; Ministry: Matthew 5–20, Mark 1–10, Luke 5–18, John 1–11) — a ready-made harmony of the Gospels
  • Bethlehem sits at the far left; the road climbs steadily toward Jerusalem and the cross at the right

The world at this time

Jesus is born under Caesar Augustus (the census of Luke 2:1) and ministers under Tiberius (Luke 3:1). Judea is a minor but troublesome Roman province: Pontius Pilate governs as prefect (AD 26–36), Herod Antipas rules Galilee, and the high-priestly family of Annas and Caiaphas manages the temple. Second-temple Judaism is in full flower — Pharisees and scribes in the synagogues, Sadducees controlling the temple, Essenes by the Dead Sea (whose scrolls were found in 1947), and messianic expectation running high. Crucifixion is Rome's public execution for rebels and slaves — which is precisely how the King of the Jews is made 'a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13).

08. Corner detail: Last Supper, Gethsemane, Trial & Golgotha

In the Bible

The chart turns its physical corner exactly at the turning point of history — the night of the betrayal. At the Passover table Jesus transforms the ancient feast into the memorial of a new covenant: 'Take and eat; this is my body... this is my blood of the covenant' (Matthew 26:26, 28). In Gethsemane the obedience of the second Adam is settled in prayer: 'Yet not as I will, but as you will' (Matthew 26:39). The trial follows — before the Sanhedrin by night and Pilate by morning: ''Are you the king of the Jews?' asked Pilate. 'Yes, it is as you say,' Jesus replied' (Mark 15:2) — and the black road runs up to Golgotha.

Key notes on this panel

  • Read this corner as one night and one morning: Supper (Matthew 26; Mark 14; Luke 22; John 13–17) → Gethsemane (Matthew 26; Mark 14; Luke 22; John 18) → Trial (Mark 15:2) → Golgotha
  • The road on the chart turns black from Gethsemane onward — sin-bearing has begun
  • The Passover setting is deliberate: the Lamb of God dies at the feast of lambs (1 Corinthians 5:7)

The world at this time

Jerusalem at Passover swelled to several times its normal population as pilgrims filled the city — which is why the arrest happens at night, away from the crowds. The double trial reflects the politics of occupation: the Sanhedrin could condemn but not execute, so Jesus is handed to the Roman governor, and the charge is shifted from blasphemy (a Jewish concern) to kingship (a Roman one). Pilate is a historically attested figure — his name was found inscribed in stone at Caesarea in 1961.

09. Corner detail: The Cross, the Empty Tomb & Mount of Olives

In the Bible

'It is finished' (John 19:30) — and then the words that changed everything: 'He is not here; he has risen' (Matthew 28:6). The panel gathers the heart of the gospel in Peter's words: 'For Christ died for sins once for all, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring you to God. He was put to death in the body but made alive by the Spirit' (1 Peter 3:18). On the Emmaus road the risen Lord opens the Scriptures: 'How foolish you are, and how slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken! Did not the Christ have to suffer these things and then enter his glory?' — and beginning with Moses and all the Prophets he explains what was said in all the Scriptures concerning himself (Luke 24:25–27). Forty days later, from the Mount of Olives, He ascends in glory.

Key notes on this panel

  • Three scenes stack up this corner: the cross ('It is finished'), the empty tomb with the stone rolled away, and the white-robed Lord rising from the Mount of Olives
  • The Emmaus panel (two disciples with the stranger) is the chart's key to reading itself: the whole Old Testament side points to Christ (Luke 24:27)
  • Resurrection references bar: Matthew 28; Mark 16; Luke 24; John 20–21

The world at this time

The resurrection was proclaimed publicly within weeks, in the same city where Jesus had been executed and buried — where an occupied tomb could have ended the claim at once. The rock-hewn tomb sealed with a rolling stone matches exactly the burial customs of wealthy first-century Judea (Joseph of Arimathea's tomb, Matthew 27:57–60). Within one generation the movement born at this corner of the chart had spread across the Roman world — a historical fact that itself demands the explanation the chart gives.

10. Resurrection, Pentecost & the Journeys of Paul

In the Bible

From the Mount of Olives the risen Christ commissions His Church: 'Therefore go and make disciples of all nations' (Matthew 28:19–20); 'Go into all the world and preach the good news to all creation' (Mark 16:15); 'You are witnesses of these things' (Luke 24:48); 'As the Father has sent me, I am sending you' (John 20:21). The angels promise His return: 'This same Jesus... will come back in the same way you have seen him go into heaven' (Acts 1:11). At Pentecost tongues of fire fall on the upper room, and the program of Acts 1:8 unfolds exactly: 'You will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth' — Philip to Samaria, then the conversion of Saul: 'This man is my chosen instrument to carry my name before the Gentiles and their kings and before the people of Israel' (Acts 9:15). Paul's three missionary journeys and voyage to Rome carry the gospel across the map of Asia Minor and Europe.

Key notes on this panel

  • The map is the message: dotted routes trace the First, Second, and Third Missionary Journeys across the Mediterranean
  • Acts 1–8 (Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria) runs under the left of the panel; Acts 9–28 ('to the ends of the earth') under the right — the book's own outline (Acts 1:8)
  • The journeys table links each phase to the letters written in it: 1st journey — Galatians, James; Jerusalem Council (Acts 15); 2nd — 1 & 2 Thessalonians; 3rd — 1 & 2 Corinthians, Romans; Paul's arrest and trip to Rome — Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, Philemon; release — Timothy, Titus; 2nd imprisonment — 2 Timothy; apostolic age ends — 1, 2, 3 John, Revelation
  • Tongues of fire above the upper room mark Pentecost as the birth of the Church

The world at this time

The gospel spreads along the arteries of the Pax Romana: fifty thousand miles of Roman roads, safe sea lanes, one common Greek tongue (koine), and Jewish synagogues in every major city gave Paul his route and his first audiences. Claudius and then Nero wear the purple during Acts; Nero's persecution (AD 64) claims Peter and Paul by tradition. In AD 70 — just after Acts closes — Titus destroys Jerusalem and its temple, ending the sacrificial system forever, exactly as Jesus had foretold (Luke 21:20–24). By then the faith had already taken root from Jerusalem to Rome.

11. Church History to the New Heaven

In the Bible

The final panel compresses the church age into one sweep. The conversion of Constantine (AD 313, the Edict of Milan) ends the persecutions and begins the age of the Holy Roman Empire; through the Dark Ages the Word is kept but chained. Then the light returns: John Wycliffe translates the Bible into English (c. 1380); Martin Luther the Reformer recovers the gospel — 'The just shall live by faith' (1517); William Tyndale publishes the first printed English Bible (1525); William Carey, the father of modern missions, carries it to the nations; and from 1870 to the present, advances in transportation, medicine, and communications help spread the gospel to the ends of the earth. The chart closes where the Bible closes: the Rapture (1 Thessalonians 4:13–18), the personal, visible, glorious return of Christ, resurrection and judgment (Matthew 25; Revelation 20:11–15), and the New Heaven and New Earth (Revelation 21) — noting honestly that 'various scholars and theological positions differ on what comes just before and right after the Second Coming of Jesus Christ.'

Key notes on this panel

  • The date ribbon runs 313 → 1380 → 1517 → 1525 → onward to 1870–present — from Constantine to the missionary age
  • Three icons carry the Reformation story: the Holy Bible on its stand (Wycliffe), the scroll 'THE JUST SHALL LIVE BY FAITH' (Luther), and the open printed Bible (Tyndale)
  • The vertical words DARK AGES and RAPTURE bracket the church age on either side
  • The panel ends with the New Heaven and New Earth — the panorama that began with 'In the beginning God created' ends with God making all things new (Revelation 21:1–5)

The world at this time

Constantine's edict (313) turned a persecuted minority into the empire's favoured faith within a century. The medieval church preserved Scripture in Latin, but the common people could not read it — which is why translation became the battle-line of reform. Gutenberg's printing press (c. 1455) changed everything: Luther's theses (1517) spread across Europe in weeks, and Tyndale's New Testament (1525) was smuggled into England in bales of cloth; Tyndale paid for it with his life (1536), praying 'Lord, open the king of England's eyes.' The modern missionary movement launched from Carey's voyage to India (1793), and the past two centuries of steamships, railways, printing, radio, and now the internet have carried the Scriptures into more than 3,500 languages — history itself serving the Great Commission until the King returns.

6

Bibliology

The Doctrine of the Bible

CONTENTS

Introduction: What is Bibliology?

Bibliology = 'Biblia' + 'ology', literally meaning “study of the books.”

Bibliology is the study of what the Bible is, how it came to be, why it is authoritative, and how it functions as God’s revelation to humanity.

Why Scripture is Foundational

  • A right theology begins with a right Bibliology — we must first establish how we know what we know.
  • Bibliology functions as the epistemological foundation.
  • If our understanding of Scripture is weak or confused, every other doctrine will be affected.

Chapter 1 – Revelation: The God Who Speaks and Makes Himself Known

Revelation

Revelation is the act of God unveiling Himself — truths that we could never discover on our own unless He chose to make them known.

  • Deuteronomy 29:29 — “The secret things belong to the Lord... but the things revealed belong to us.”
  • John 1:18 — No one has ever seen God... He has made Him known.

Revelation is not man’s search for God, but God’s gracious initiative to make Himself known to man. There are two main categories: General Revelation and Special Revelation.

General Revelation

1. Creation

Psalm 19:1–6 says, “The heavens declare the glory of God; the skies proclaim the work of His hands.”

  • It is continuous.
  • It is wordless.
  • It is worldwide.

Acts 14:17 — Paul told the people of Lystra that even without Scripture, God “has not left Himself without testimony; He has shown kindness by giving you rain from heaven and crops in their seasons.”

Romans 1:18–21 — God’s invisible attributes are evident: (1) eternal power, (2) divine nature. Creation reveals the power, wisdom, and goodness of God.

2. Conscience

Romans 2:14–15 teaches that even Gentiles who do not possess the Law demonstrate that its requirements are written on their hearts, with their consciences bearing witness.

God has placed within every person an inner moral compass — an innate awareness of right and wrong. This conscience serves as a built-in testimony that all people are accountable to Someone greater than themselves.

3. Providence

In Acts 17:26–27, Paul says, “From one man He made all the nations, that they should inhabit the whole earth; and He marked out their appointed times in history and the boundaries of their lands. God did this so that they would seek Him and perhaps reach out for Him and find Him.”

Matthew 5:45; Acts 14:15–17; James 1:17 — Every good and perfect gift is from above.

History is not random; God sovereignly directs it to fulfill His purposes — the rise and fall of nations, the spread of civilizations, and even our personal experiences are all under His control.

Through these providential acts, God continually reveals His power, justice, and mercy — inviting people to seek Him, as seen in His dealings with Israel (Deuteronomy 28:15–68), Egypt (Exodus 7–11), and the kingdoms of the world (Daniel 2:21, 31–43).

Limitations of General Revelation

Though general revelation is not sufficient to bring salvation, it serves as an important antecedent by revealing certain truths and aspects of God’s nature to all people.

Creation declares God’s glory, and nature and conscience reveal His existence, but none can declare His grace or explain the way of salvation — the stars can tell us there is a Creator, but not how to be reconciled to Him.

The natural man cannot understand the things of God (1 Corinthians 2:14). Though they knew God, they did not honor Him (Romans 1:21–23).

Looking at a painting reveals that there is a painter, but not his name, character, or how to personally know him.

Special Revelation

While general revelation is universal, special revelation is particular — it is God revealing Himself to specific people, at specific times, through specific means.

It is called special because it provides knowledge that cannot be discovered through human reason or nature — such as God’s character, His covenant, His plan of salvation, and the person of Jesus Christ.

Without special revelation, we could know that God exists but not who He is or how to be reconciled to Him.

Special Revelation is Progressive

  • Special revelation unfolded progressively through history.
  • God did not reveal all truth at once, but gradually, in stages, as His redemptive plan advanced.
  • In the Old Testament, God spoke through dreams, visions, angels, prophets, and historical acts — revealing more of His purpose step by step.
  • Each revelation built upon the previous one — from the covenant with Abraham, to the Law of Moses, to the promises of the Messiah.
  • This process is known as progressive revelation.

Scripture as God’s Special Revelation

The Scriptures stand as God’s written form of special revelation. What God once spoke through prophets and apostles has now been recorded and preserved in written form — the Bible.

2 Timothy 3:16–17 affirms that “All Scripture is God-breathed.” It carries divine authority and communicates God’s truth in human language.

The Bible is not merely a record about revelation — it is revelation. Through it, God continues to speak to every generation. Scripture is complete, sufficient, and unchanging.

The Word Made Flesh

“In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. He was with God in the beginning. The Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us.” — John 1:1–2, 14

Just as our “words” express our thoughts, Jesus expresses the mind and heart of God.

  • John 1:18 – “No one has ever seen God; the only Son… He has made Him known.”
  • Hebrews 1:3 – “He is the radiance of the glory of God and the exact imprint of His nature.”

Jesus is the living Word — the perfect and final revelation of God. In Him, God’s invisible nature was made visible. Christ’s words, works, death, and resurrection reveal the heart of God — His holiness, love, justice, and mercy.

Summary: General vs. Special Revelation

General Revelation Special Revelation
Universal, through creation and conscience Particular, through Scripture and Christ
Reveals God’s power and moral order Reveals God’s plan of redemption
Sufficient to condemn Sufficient to save

Chapter 2 – Inspiration: God Breathes – The Divine Origin of Scripture

Definition of Inspiration

Inspiration is defined as the supernatural influence of the Holy Spirit upon the human authors of Scripture, ensuring that what they wrote was exactly what God intended, without error, in the original manuscripts.

“Inspiration is God’s superintending of the human authors so that, using their own individual personalities, they composed and recorded without error His revelation to man in the words of the original autographs.” — Ryrie, Basic Theology

“The inspiration of Scripture means that God breathed out the words of Scripture. It is a way of saying that the very words of Scripture are God’s words.” — Grudem, Systematic Theology

Inspiration – Learning from Paul’s Teaching

“All Scripture is God-breathed and is useful for teaching, rebuking, correcting and training in righteousness, so that the servant of God may be thoroughly equipped for every good work.” — 2 Timothy 3:16–17

Inspiration is theopneustos (God-breathed). Just as human breath produces spoken words, Scripture is the expression of God’s mind and will.

If all Scripture is “God-breathed,” then:

  • It carries divine authority — to reject Scripture is to reject God’s word.
  • It is truthful and trustworthy — because God cannot lie or err.
  • It is sufficient and useful — for teaching, rebuking, correcting, and training in righteousness.

Inspiration – Learning from Peter’s Teaching
