“For prophecy never had its origin in the human will, but prophets, though human, spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit.” — 2 Peter 1:21

This describes divine guidance ensuring that what was written was precisely what God intended.

Berkhof underlines that inspiration involves a divine-human cooperation — writers were not passive instruments, yet God’s Spirit guaranteed the truthfulness of every part of Scripture.

False Views of Inspiration

1. Intuition Theory (Natural Inspiration)

The writers were simply religious geniuses with heightened spiritual insight — not supernatural, but writings of unusual men like Shakespeare.

2. Illumination Theory

The Holy Spirit did not directly inspire the words, but heightened the writers’ perception of truth.

3. Dynamic (Conceptual) Theory

God inspired the ideas or concepts, and the authors used their own words to express them.

4. Dictation Theory

God dictated the words directly, and the authors acted merely as secretaries.

Plenary Verbal Inspiration (Orthodox View)

Plenary = all Scripture is inspired; Verbal = the very words are inspired.

Summary: Scripture is both fully divine and fully human. God used human authors with their unique style and situation but directed the outcome so that every word written was what He intended.

Implications

  • Authority – Scripture carries God’s voice.
  • Inerrancy – Because God cannot err, His Word is without error.
  • Sufficiency – It contains all truth necessary for salvation and sanctification.

Chapter 3 – The Trustworthiness of the Bible: Inerrancy & Infallibility

Inerrancy

Definition: Inerrancy is that the Bible, in its original manuscripts, is without error in everything it affirms — whether it speaks about doctrine, history, ethics, or even matters of fact.

Focus: the Bible does not err. Biblical basis: 2 Timothy 3:16; John 17:17; Psalm 19:7.

“Being wholly and verbally God-given, Scripture is without error or fault in all its teaching, no less in what it states about God’s acts in creation, about the events of world history, and about its own literary origins under God, than in its witness to God’s saving grace in individual lives.” — The Chicago Statement on Biblical Inerrancy (1978)

Grudem clarifies that Scripture tells the truth in everything it affirms, not necessarily in how we might paraphrase or modernize its statements.

Ryrie adds that if Scripture is inspired by a perfect God, it must also be inerrant — otherwise, God would be the author of error.

  • John 17:17 — “Your word is truth.”
  • Psalm 19:7 — “The law of the Lord is perfect.”

Inerrancy: What It Means and What It Doesn’t Mean

Inerrancy means:

  • The Bible tells the truth in all it asserts, using ordinary language.
  • Any copy or translation that faithfully represents the original is reliable.
  • Proper interpretation (context, genre, grammar) is essential.

Inerrancy does not mean:

  • Writers used scientific precision or modern terminology.
  • There are no variations among manuscripts.
  • Every translation is perfect.

Infallibility

Definition: Infallibility means that Scripture cannot fail — it is completely trustworthy and unfailing in accomplishing God’s purpose and in communicating divine truth.

Infallibility focuses on trustworthiness (it cannot deceive), while inerrancy focuses on truthfulness (it does not err).

Infallibility of Scripture assures that the Bible will never fail to fulfill God’s purpose or lead us astray — because it is the very Word of the God who cannot lie. Rooted in the nature of God: “God who never lies” (Titus 1:2).

Inerrancy vs Infallibility

Doctrine Focus Core Meaning Rooted In
Inerrancy Truthfulness The Bible contains no error in its original manuscripts God’s Truth
Infallibility Trustworthiness The Bible cannot fail to accomplish its purpose or mislead God’s Faithfulness

Inerrancy Illustrated: The Christ–Scripture Analogy

Similarities:

  • Both are divine as well as human.
  • In both, the divine and human are united in one medium — the two natures of Christ are united in the same person; the two natures of Scripture are united in the same propositions. So the Bible is a theanthropic book as Christ is a theanthropic person.
  • In both, the human side is without error.
  • Both are the Word of God.

The Word of God – Two Living Revelations

Living Incarnate Word Living Written Word
Jesus Christ The Bible
Human Parents — Holy Spirit Overshadowing Human Authors — Holy Spirit Superintending
Without Sin Without Error

Jesus and Scripture

  • Jesus consistently treated the historical narratives of the Old Testament as straightforward records of fact.
  • Jesus often chose as the basis of His teaching those very stories that many modern skeptics find unacceptable (e.g., Adam and Eve, Noah’s flood, Sodom and Gomorrah, and Jonah).
  • For Jesus, Scripture was the final court of appeal in His disputes with the Pharisees and Sadducees.
  • Jesus taught that Scripture could not pass away (Matthew 5:17) or be broken (John 10:35).

Chapter 4 – Illumination

Illumination is the Holy Spirit’s supernatural work enabling believers to understand, embrace, and apply Scripture.

  • 1 Corinthians 2:14 — The natural man cannot understand spiritual truth.
  • Psalm 119:130 — God’s Word imparts light and understanding.
  • John 14:26 — The Spirit teaches and reminds believers.

The Need for Illumination

  • Moral antipathy: humans reject known truth due to sin.
  • Spiritual blindness: without the Spirit, truth remains unknowable and inaccessible.
  • Jesus opened the disciples’ understanding to Scripture (Luke 24:45).
  • Paul prays for spiritual wisdom to understand divine truths (Ephesians 1:17–18).

Practical Implications of Illumination

Why Illumination Matters for Us

  • Ensures we don’t rely on human wisdom alone.
  • Encourages prayer and dependence on the Spirit in Bible study.
  • Affirms the Bible’s power to transform when rightly understood.

What Illumination Is Not

  • It does not produce new revelation outside Scripture.
  • It is not a one-time event but ongoing in the believer’s growth.

How Illumination Complements Interpretation

  • Interpretation = human effort: study of language, culture, context.
  • Illumination = divine enabling: the Spirit opens minds and hearts.
  • Both work hand in hand; neither is sufficient alone.

Chapter 5 – Authority of Scripture

The Bible stands as the final and supreme standard for faith and practice — above tradition, reason, or human opinion. The believer’s submission to Scripture is, therefore, an act of submission to God’s own authority.

“All Scripture is God-breathed and is useful for teaching, rebuking, correcting and training in righteousness.” (2 Timothy 3:16)

Definitions

“The authority of Scripture means that all the words in Scripture are God’s words in such a way that to disbelieve or disobey any word of Scripture is to disbelieve or disobey God.” — Wayne Grudem

“Biblical authority means that the Bible, being God’s Word written, carries the same authority as God Himself. Therefore, it demands our belief and obedience.” — Charles C. Ryrie

  • Authority (Belief & Behaviour) means the God-given right and power of the Bible to tell us what to believe and how to live — both for every person and for the Church.
  • Authority (Source of Knowledge) means that the Bible alone has the divine right and power to give us true knowledge about God and reveal the spiritual nature of human beings.

The Authority of Jesus and the Word

“When Jesus had finished saying these things, the crowds were amazed at his teaching, because he taught as one who had authority, and not as their teachers of the law.” — Matthew 7:28–29

“Then Jesus came to them and said, ‘All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me.’” — Matthew 28:18

Chapter 6 – Canonicity of the Bible

Canonicity is not humans inventing the Bible. It is God’s people discovering what God already inspired (2 Timothy 3:16).

Canon = a “measuring rod” (like a ruler). It measures: “Does this book measure up to God’s truth?”

God wrote the Bible through 40+ people over 1,500 years — and He guided His people to recognize the right books.

Old Testament (OT) Canonicity

The OT has 39 books (Genesis to Malachi). These were written mostly in Hebrew, from ~1400 BC to 400 BC.

How did they become “official”? God inspired them first — no book gets in unless God breathed it out (2 Timothy 3:16). But how did God’s people know?

OT Canonicity – Three Tests

  • Is the author a prophet or eyewitness? — Moses wrote the Law (5 books) and spoke with God (Exodus 33:11).
  • Does it match God’s known truth? — All books echo: one God, love Him, love others (Deuteronomy 6:4–5).
  • God’s people accepted and used it — Psalm 119: “Your Word is my delight!” The Jews used it daily.

OT Canonicity – Historical Process

  • 1400–400 BC: Books written and collected.
  • ~400 BC: Jewish leaders locked the list at Jamnia (a school). No more books after Malachi — that’s why there are no “lost” OT books today.
  • Jesus confirmed it: in Luke 24:44, Jesus said, “Everything in the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms” (that’s our exact 39 books). He quoted them as Scripture 78 times.
  • The Dead Sea Scrolls (found 1947) match our OT word-for-word.

So, OT canonicity = God inspired → Tests passed → Jewish leaders recognized → Jesus sealed it.

New Testament (NT) Canonicity

The NT has 27 books (Matthew to Revelation), written in Greek, AD 40–100. How did they get chosen? God inspired them first — same as the OT, God-breathed. The apostles wrote under the Holy Spirit (John 14:26).

NT Canonicity – Four Tests

  • Is the author an apostle or close associate? — Written by an apostle (like Paul) or their helper (like Mark with Peter); eyewitnesses to Jesus. Paul in Galatians 1: “I saw Jesus myself!”
  • Does it match OT truth? — Fully agrees with the OT, no contradictions. Jesus: “I came to fulfill the Law” (Matthew 5:17).
  • Is the writing orthodox (true teaching)? — Teaches Jesus as God, died for sins, rose again; no heresies. 1 Corinthians 15 gives the core gospel: Christ died, was buried, and rose.
  • God’s Church accepted and used it widely — read in churches everywhere for 100+ years. Early letters: “Read Colossians in all churches” (Colossians 4:16).

NT Canonicity – Historical Process

  • AD 40–100: Books written (Gospels, letters).
  • AD 100–300: Churches copied and shared. Some fakes appeared (like the Gospel of Thomas) — rejected!
  • AD 367: Athanasius listed the exact 27 books in his Easter letter.
  • AD 397: Councils (Hippo & Carthage) agreed unanimously: “These 27 are canon,” based on what churches already used.
  • Key point about the councils: they were not “inventing” — just agreeing on what God’s Spirit had already shown.

So, NT canonicity = God inspired → Tests passed → Churches recognized → Councils confirmed.

Canonisation of OT & NT – Summary

Old Testament New Testament
Books 39 27
Language Hebrew Greek
Time Written 1400–400 BC AD 40–100
Tests 3 (Author, Matches Truth, Accepted) 4 (Apostle, Matches OT, Orthodox, Accepted)
Who Recognized? Jewish Leaders (Jamnia, 400 BC) + Jesus Early Churches + Councils (AD 397)
Final Seal Jesus quoted all (Luke 24:44) Holy Spirit in churches (Rev. 22:18–19)

Comparison of Ancient Texts

Author Date Written Earliest Copy Number of Copies Accuracy of Copy
Caesar 1st Cent BC 900 AD 10 —
Livy 1st Cent BC — 20 —
Tacitus 100 AD 1100 AD 20 —
Thucydides 5th Cent BC 900 AD 8 —
Herodotus 5th Cent BC 900 AD 8 —
Demosthenes 4th Cent BC 1100 AD 200 —
Mahabharata — — — 90%
Homer 9th Cent BC — 643 95%
New Testament 1st Cent AD (50–100 AD) 2nd Cent AD (130 AD) 5000 99+%

Why Does Canonicity Matter?

  • Trust: You can read Genesis to Revelation — it’s all God’s Word. No guessing!
  • Protection: God kept fakes out (300+ rejected books). Your Bible is pure.
  • Power: Inspired words change lives (Hebrews 4:12 — “sharper than any sword”).
  • For you: When doubts hit (“Is this real?”), remember: Jesus + the Holy Spirit said YES!

List of Apocryphal Books

Tobit, Judith, Wisdom, Sirach, Baruch, 1 Maccabees, 2 Maccabees, Additions to Esther, Additions to Daniel.

Chapter 7 – Various Views of the Bible

Four major views of the Bible: Liberalism, Neo-Orthodoxy, Roman Catholicism, and the Conservative, Evangelical Orthodox view.

1. Liberalism

Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) and Friedrich Schleiermacher (1768–1834) were prominent liberal theologians. Liberalism holds that:

  • The Bible is not divine supernatural revelation from God but the fallible product of human authors.
  • It contains a record of men’s thoughts about God.
  • The truth of the Bible is subject to the test of human reason and experience.
  • Man himself, not God, is the final authority on truth.

2. Neo-Orthodoxy

The founder of the movement was Karl Barth (1886–1968). Neo-Orthodoxy holds that:

  • The Bible may contain the Word of God, but it is not the Word of God.
  • Christ is the Word of God, but only as one experiences Him in a personal encounter.
  • God’s revelation of Himself comes only through personally experiencing Him, not through the Bible.
  • The truth of the Bible is relative and subjective.

3. Roman Catholicism

The traditions and interpretative views of the Church about the Bible have the same authority and value as the Bible itself. Thus, the Bible teaches what the Church says it teaches.

4. Conservative, Evangelical Orthodox View

  • The Bible is God’s divine, supernatural revelation of Himself.
  • The inerrant words of the Bible are inspired of God and therefore authoritative.
  • The Bible is God’s objective, absolute truth by which everything can be tested.
  • The Bible is not subjective or relative.
  • While the Bible should be carefully and intelligently studied, its truth and accuracy do not depend on human reason or experience.
7

Theology Proper

The Doctrine of God — Student Notes

Who Is God? — Questions That Stir the Soul

Why these questions matter

Every person, knowingly or unknowingly, builds their life on what they believe about God.

Our view of God shapes our identity, values, decisions, worship, and lifestyle.

Key reflection questions

Who is God, and how can we know Him?
God is knowable because He reveals Himself (Romans 1:19–20; Psalm 19:1–4; Hebrews 1:1–2).

If someone watched your life, what God would they see?
Our conduct reflects our concept of God (Matthew 5:16).

Why do all cultures believe in some form of God?
Because God placed eternity in human hearts (Ecclesiastes 3:11) and a moral sense in all people (Romans 2:15).

Can a person believe in God but not know Him?
Yes—head knowledge is not heart relationship (James 2:19).

Quotes

A.W. Tozer: “What comes into our minds when we think about God is the most important thing about us.”

Mortimer Adler: More consequences flow from belief or unbelief in God than from any other question.

Why Theology Proper Matters

Knowing About God is not the same as Knowing God

True knowledge of God is relational, not merely intellectual.

Reference: John 17:3 – “This is eternal life: that they know You…”

Summary: Studying God helps us know Him personally, not just collect facts.

Theology Proper is rooted in God’s self-revelation

God reveals Himself in:

Creation – shows His power and divinity (Romans 1:19–20).

Scripture – His written revelation (2 Timothy 3:16–17).

Jesus Christ – the perfect revelation (John 14:9).

Worship flows from knowing God

Worship becomes real and meaningful when we understand who God is.

Jeremiah 9:23–24 – True boasting is in understanding and knowing God.

Our world is confused about God

Therefore, clear doctrine helps believers avoid deception and worship God truthfully (John 4:24).

Approaches to Knowing Truth (and God)

Below are notes on six major approaches, their flaws, and the biblical response.

Agnosticism — “We cannot know God.”

Flaw:

Self-defeating: claiming “we cannot know” is itself a knowledge claim.

Bible:

God has made Himself known (Romans 1:19–20).

God wants to be known (Jeremiah 9:24).

Rationalism — “Reason is final authority.”

Flaw:

Rationalism assumes that the human mind is trustworthy enough to be the ultimate judge of truth—but this itself is a belief statement, not a proven fact.

Even the claim “Reason is the final authority” cannot be proven by reason; it must be accepted by faith.

Therefore, rationalism is self-contradictory because it depends on the very thing it claims to avoid—faith—and ignores God’s revelation, which alone can give full knowledge.

  • Human reason is limited and affected by sin (Proverbs 16:25).
  • We cannot reason our way to all spiritual truths.

Bible:

Scripture is the final authority (2 Timothy 3:16).

Reason is good, but not ultimate.

Fideism — “Just believe without evidence.”

Flaw:

  • Blind faith can justify anything, even contradictory or false beliefs.
  • When a fideist is asked, “Why should I believe what you believe?”, they must eventually give reasons—which contradicts their claim that faith needs no reason.
  • Thus, fideism becomes self-defeating, because it denies the very rational foundation it is forced to depend on.
  • Biblical faith is not a blind leap; it is trust based on God’s revealed character and truth (Hebrews 11:1).

Bible:

Faith is based on evidence and God’s character (Hebrews 11:1).

Believers must give reasons for their hope (1 Peter 3:15).

Experientialism — “Truth is what I feel.”

Flaw:

Feelings are subjective, inconsistent, and deceptive.

Bible:

Experiences must be tested (1 John 4:1).

Scripture is the standard for truth.

Evidentialism — “Only what is proven is true.”

Flaw:

Not all truth is physical or empirical.

It demands material proof for spiritual realities.

Bible:

Faith without seeing is commended (John 20:29).

Evidence supports faith, but revelation explains it.

Pragmatism — “Truth is what works.”

Flaw:

Lies sometimes appear to “work.”

Utility does not define truth.

Bible:

Truth is a Person (John 14:6).

Truth produces fruit because it is real.

Major Worldviews About God

Monism

Monism teaches that all reality is one, and any distinction between God, humans, and the world is ultimately an illusion. Everything is part of a single divine or impersonal substance. Individuality is not ultimate—unity is. Many Eastern religions (like Advaita Hinduism) hold monistic ideas.

Dualism

Dualism teaches that reality consists of two eternal, independent forces—often good vs. evil, or spirit vs. matter. These two principles constantly oppose each other. It struggles to explain which one is ultimate or how the conflict ends. Christianity does not teach dualism because God alone is eternal and sovereign.

Humanism

Humanism exalts human reason, dignity, and self-sufficiency as the highest authority. It often rejects divine revelation and builds morality on human wisdom. Purpose is defined by human achievement, not God. In secular humanism, man becomes his own measure of truth and meaning.

Atheism

Atheism asserts that there is no God or supernatural being. Reality is purely physical and self-existent. Moral values are human inventions, not absolutes. Atheism must explain the universe, life, morality, and order without any Creator, which raises many philosophical problems.

Agnosticism

Agnosticism claims that we cannot know whether God exists. It suspends judgment and treats divine knowledge as impossible or unknowable. However, saying “God cannot be known” is itself a knowledge claim. Scripture teaches that God has revealed Himself clearly through creation and conscience.

Materialism (Naturalism)

Materialism teaches that matter is all that exists—there is no spirit, soul, or supernatural realm. Everything is explained through physical laws and natural processes. Human beings are reduced to biological machines. This worldview cannot account for morality, consciousness, or meaning.

Pantheism

Pantheism teaches that God is identical with the universe—everything is God and God is everything. There is no personal Creator; the universe itself is divine. Good and evil lose meaning because all is one. Many Eastern religions and New Age philosophies embrace aspects of pantheism.

Polytheism

Polytheism teaches that there are many gods, each with limited power and authority. These gods often compete, struggle, or contradict one another. Ancient Greek, Roman, and Hindu traditions include polytheistic elements. This worldview cannot explain ultimate unity or a single moral standard.

Syncretism

Syncretism blends multiple religions or belief systems into one. It accepts contradictory ideas without committing to one true worldview. It is popular in modern culture (“all religions lead to God”), but ignores the exclusive truth claims of Scripture. Syncretism weakens biblical doctrine and leads to spiritual confusion.

Deism

Deism teaches that God created the universe, set its laws, and then stepped back—He does not intervene in human affairs. Miracles, revelation, and prayer are denied. God is like a watchmaker who winds the clock and leaves it. This contradicts the Bible’s teaching of a God who speaks, acts, and loves His people.

Theism

Theism teaches that there is one personal, infinite, sovereign God who created and sustains the universe. He is involved in human history, reveals Himself, hears prayer, and performs miracles. Christianity, Judaism, and Islam are theistic. Biblical theism presents the one true God who seeks a relationship with humanity

Arguments for the Existence of God

The Cosmological Argument

The Cosmological Argument states that everything that begins to exist must have a cause.
Nothing starts by itself. Every effect requires something else to bring it into existence.

1. Everything that begins to exist has a cause

This is the basic and universal law called the Law of Causality.
We never see anything—houses, trees, machines, humans—coming into existence without a cause.

2. The universe began to exist

Scientific evidence like the Big Bang Theory, and philosophical reasoning, show that the universe is not eternal.
It had a beginning—space, time, matter, and energy all came into existence.

3. Therefore, the universe must have a cause

Since it began, something must have caused it.
But the cause must be outside the universe, because time and matter began with the universe.

4. The Cause must be:

Timeless — because time began with creation

Immaterial — because matter began with creation

Powerful — enough to create everything out of nothing

Personal — only a will can choose to create

Uncaused — an infinite chain of causes is impossible

These qualities perfectly match the God of the Bible.

5. Biblical Support

Genesis 1:1 — “In the beginning God created…”

Hebrews 11:3 — The universe was formed at God’s command

Exodus 20:11 — God made everything in heaven and earth

Summary Statement

The universe began to exist; whatever begins must have a cause; therefore, the universe has a Cause — and that Cause is God.

Teleological Argument — Design

The Teleological Argument says that the order, complexity, and purpose we see in the universe point to an intelligent Designer.
Just as design in machines or buildings shows the presence of a designer, the design in creation shows the existence of God.

1. Design and order always imply a designer

Whenever we see structure, precision, and purpose—like a watch, a computer, or even a simple tool—we immediately conclude that someone designed it.
No one believes a smartphone came about by chance.
Order does not arise from chaos.

2. The universe displays incredible design

From the largest galaxies to the smallest DNA molecule, creation shows complexity, harmony, and precision.
Examples include:

The fine-tuning of the universe’s physical constants

The complexity of the cell

The information in DNA

The beauty and order of nature

This level of design cannot be the product of random chance.

3. Therefore, the universe must have a Designer

Just as a house requires an architect, and a computer requires a programmer, the universe requires a super-intelligent Creator.
This Designer must be:

Intelligent – to plan and order creation

Purposeful – because design reveals purpose

Powerful – to create and sustain all things

Personal – because impersonal forces cannot design with purpose

The design argument leads us to a wise, intelligent, personal God.

4. Biblical Support

Psalm 19:1 – “The heavens declare the glory of God; the skies proclaim the work of his hands.”

Romans 1:19–20 – God’s eternal power and divine nature are evident in creation.

Job 38:1–7 – God’s wisdom in creation is displayed through His questions.

Summary Statement

The universe exhibits remarkable order, beauty, and complexity; design always requires a designer; therefore, the universe points to the existence of an intelligent Designer—God

Anthropological Argument — Human Nature

The Anthropological Argument says that the unique qualities of human beings — our personality, morality, rationality, emotions, and spiritual awareness — point to a personal and moral Creator.
Human nature reflects something beyond matter; it reflects the image of God.

1. Humans possess personality: mind, will, and emotions

Humans are self-conscious persons, not just physical machines.
We think, choose, love, create, dream, and communicate in complex ways.
Impersonal matter cannot produce personal beings; only a personal Cause can explain personal creatures.

2. Humans have a moral nature and moral awareness

All cultures show a sense of right and wrong, justice, duty, and guilt.
We have a conscience that accuses or approves our actions (Romans 2:14–15).
Moral laws require a moral Lawgiver, because matter cannot produce morality.

3. Humans seek meaning, purpose, worship, and transcendence

Human beings naturally seek God or something higher than themselves.
We ask questions animals never ask: Why am I here? What is my purpose? What happens after death?
This spiritual hunger points to the One who created us in His image (Genesis 1:27).

4. Therefore, human nature points to a personal, moral Creator

Since humans possess:

personality,

rationality,

moral conscience,

spiritual longing,

and dignity,

the Cause of humans must be a personal, rational, moral, spiritual, and purposeful God.
We reflect God because we are made in His image (Imago Dei).

5. Biblical Support

Genesis 1:27 – God created man in His own image.

Psalm 8:3–6 – God crowned humans with glory and honor.

Romans 2:14–15 – God’s law is written on the human heart.

Romans 1:18, 21–23 – Humans naturally seek God but often distort Him.

Summary Statement

Human beings possess personality, conscience, moral awareness, reason, and spiritual longing that cannot arise from matter or chance. These qualities reflect the nature of a personal and moral Creator—God

Theological Definitions of God

Westminster Shorter Catechism:

“God is spirit, infinite, eternal, and unchangeable in His being, wisdom, power, holiness, justice, goodness, and truth.”

Berkhof:

God is one, absolute, unchangeable, infinite in knowledge, wisdom, goodness, love, grace, mercy, righteousness, and holiness.

A.H. Strong:

God is the infinite and perfect Spirit who is the source, support, and end of all things.

The Essence of God — What Makes God, God

God is Spirit

  • Not physical; invisible; unlimited (John 4:24).

God is Life

  • Source of all existence (Acts 17:25).

God is Perfect

  • Complete, lacking nothing (Matthew 5:48).

God is Unique

  • None like Him (Isaiah 46:9).

God is Eternal

  • No beginning, no end (Psalm 90:2).

The Attributes of God

Incommunicable Attributes (belong only to God)

Holiness – set apart, morally pure (Isaiah 6).

Transcendence – above and beyond creation.

Self-sufficiency – needs nothing (Acts 17:25).

Infinity – without limits.

Immutability – unchanging (Malachi 3:6).

Omnipotence – all-powerful (Jeremiah 32:17).

Omnipresence – everywhere (Psalm 139:7–10).

Omniscience – all-knowing (Psalm 147:5).

Sovereignty – rules everything (Daniel 4:35).

Faithfulness – keeps His word (Lamentations 3:22–23).

Biblical Descriptions of God (Moral/Relational Attributes)

God is love (1 John 4:8).

God is light (1 John 1:5).

God is truth (John 14:6).

God is good (Psalm 119:68).

God is wise (Romans 16:27).

God is just (Deuteronomy 32:4).

God is merciful (Psalm 103:8).

God is gracious (Ephesians 2:4–5).

God is wrathful against sin (Romans 1:18).

God is forgiving (Psalm 86:5).

God is patient (2 Peter 3:9).

God is righteous (Psalm 145:17).

Questions to Ponder:

1. Why is the question “Who is God?” foundational for all areas of theology and life? Explain using both biblical and practical reasons.

2. Distinguish between knowing about God and knowing God personally. How does John 17:3 shape our understanding of true knowledge of God?

3. Describe the biblical basis for God’s self-revelation through creation, Scripture, and Jesus Christ. Why is each essential?

4. Identify and explain the major flaws in at least three approaches to knowing truth (e.g., Agnosticism, Rationalism, Fideism, Experientialism, Evidentialism, Pragmatism). Give examples where possible.

5. Choose any three worldviews (e.g., Monism, Humanism, Materialism, Pantheism, Deism, Theism) and briefly explain how each one misunderstands or distorts the nature of God.

6. State and explain the Cosmological Argument. How does it support belief in an eternal, personal Creator?

7. State and explain the Teleological Argument. Give at least two examples from nature that demonstrate design.

8. Explain the Anthropological Argument for God's existence. How do human personality, morality, and spirituality point to a Creator?

9. Using the definitions of Westminster, Berkhof, and A.H. Strong, summarize the essential theological characteristics of God. What common truths do these definitions highlight?

10. List and explain any five “Essence of God” truths (e.g., God is Spirit, Life, Perfect, Unique, Eternal). How do these shape our understanding of who God is?

11. Describe the difference between God’s incommunicable attributes and His moral/relational attributes. Provide at least two examples from each category and explain their importance.

12. Select three biblical descriptions of God (e.g., God is love, light, truth, good, just, merciful, righteous) and explain how each one affects Christian faith and daily living.

7

The Trinity

Theology Proper — The Doctrine of the Trinity

Introduction: Why Study the Trinity?

We do not study the Trinity because the Church enjoys complex ideas, but because God has revealed Himself in a way that cannot be reduced without distortion. The doctrine of the Trinity arises not from human speculation, but from listening carefully to everything Scripture says about God.

The word “Trinity” does not appear in the Bible. However, the reality it describes is revealed throughout Scripture. The Trinity is not something the Church invented; it is something the Church confessed after receiving God’s self-revelation.

Purpose of This Study

We are not trying to explain God fully

We are seeking to understand faithfully what God has revealed

Scripture defines God, not human assumptions

Why This Doctrine Matters

Our understanding of God shapes:

Worship

Prayer

Salvation

Love and relationships

The Trinity is not a problem to solve, but a truth to confess and worship.

LESSON 1: THE NATURE OF THE TRIUNE GOD

1. Why the Trinity Feels Difficult

The Trinity feels difficult because:

God is infinite, and we are finite

We think using created categories

God is uncreated, eternal, and beyond time

Scripture gives us true revelation, not exhaustive explanation.

Mystery does not mean contradiction.
Limited understanding does not mean false doctrine.

Theology should lead us not only to clarity, but also to humility. We do not master the Trinity; we confess it.

2. Definition of the Trinity

The Trinity means that there is one true God who eternally exists as three distinct persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit.

Each person:

Is fully God

Shares the same divine nature

Is equal in power and glory

Yet:

There are not three Gods, but one God

The persons are distinct in relationship and work, not in essence

3. Fourfold Orthodox Emphasis

Historic Christian theology holds four truths together:

Oneness of Essence – God is one in being

Threeness of Persons – Father, Son, Spirit

Functional Order – Distinct roles without inequality

Eternality – All three are eternal

There is voluntary and perfect submission within the Godhead, without inferiority of essence.

4. Oneness of God (Essence / Being)

Christianity teaches one God, not three gods.

What God is → one divine essence

Who God is → three persons

God is not:

Three beings

Three wills

Three powers

God is one being, with one will, one power, and one glory.

5. Inseparable Works of the One God

All external works of God are the works of the one God, though each person is involved distinctly.

Creation

Father: initiates (Genesis 1:1)

Son: creates life (John 1:3)

Spirit: orders and sustains (Genesis 1:2)

Redemption

Father plans

Son accomplishes

Spirit applies

Revelation

Father speaks

Son is the Word

Spirit inspires Scripture

God does not act as a committee.
One work. One God. Distinct persons. Inseparable action.

LESSON 2: GOD IN THREE PERSONS

1. Full Deity of Each Person

Scripture clearly declares:

The Father is God (John 6:27)

The Son is God (Hebrews 1:8)

The Holy Spirit is God (Acts 5:3–4)

Yet Scripture also presents them together:

Baptismal formula (Matthew 28:19)

Apostolic blessing (2 Corinthians 13:14)

This is unity without confusion and distinction without division.

2. Eternal Existence of the Trinity

God is eternal, meaning:

No beginning

No end

No succession of moments

The Father, Son, and Spirit:

Always existed

Never came into being

Were never without one another

God did not become triune.
God has always been triune.

3. Eternal Relationships, Not Roles

Father, Son, and Spirit are:

Eternal relationships

Not temporary roles God adopted later

A role can change.
A relationship defines who someone is.

The Father has always been Father.
The Son has always been Son.
The Spirit has always been Spirit.

4. Equality and Distinction

All three persons are:

Equal in essence

Equal in glory

Equal in power

Yet they relate differently:

The Father sends

The Son is sent

The Spirit proceeds and glorifies the Son

Obedience does not imply inferiority.
It reflects relationship, not rank.

LESSON 3: BIBLICAL FOUNDATION OF THE TRINITY

1. Old Testament Foundations

Genesis 1

Elohim is plural in form, singular in action

“Let us make mankind” (Gen 1:26)

“So God created” (Gen 1:27)

Plural deliberation. Singular action.

Other Key Passages

Genesis 3:22; 11:7

Isaiah 6:8

Daniel 7 (Ancient of Days and Son of Man)

Psalm 110:1

The Old Testament does not explain the Trinity, but it anticipates it.

2. New Testament Clarity

The New Testament brings what was hinted into full light:

Jesus’ baptism (Matthew 3:16–17)

The Great Commission (Matthew 28:19)

The Word who is God (John 1:1,14)

Trinitarian blessings (2 Corinthians 13:14)

The Trinity is revealed, not argued.

LESSON 4: ERRORS TO AVOID

Common Heresies

Unitarianism

Denies the Trinity

Rejects full deity of Son and Spirit

Modalism

One person, three masks

Denies real relationships within God

Tritheism

Three separate gods

Denies biblical monotheism

All three fail to hold oneness and threeness together.

LESSON 5: THE NICENE CONFESSION

The Nicene Creed safeguards biblical truth:

Jesus is God from God

Begotten, not made

Of one being with the Father

It protects:

The full deity of Christ

The unity of God

The truth of salvation

LESSON 6: WHY THE TRINITY MATTERS

1. Doctrinal Importance

Atonement depends on Christ’s full deity

Worship depends on Christ being God

Salvation must be God’s work, not a creature’s

2. Relational and Practical Importance

God is personal apart from creation

Unity and diversity find their source in God

Church life reflects triune unity

Conclusion: Confessing the Triune God

The Trinity teaches us that:

God is eternally loving

God is relational in Himself

Unity and diversity are not opposites

We do not explain the Trinity away.
We receive it, confess it, and worship the God who has revealed Himself as Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.

8

Angelology

The Doctrine of Angels

1. Introduction to Angelology

Angelology is the systematic and biblical study of angels, both holy and fallen. This section introduces the meaning, scope, and theological importance of the doctrine. It explains why angels must be studied carefully and biblically, avoiding speculation while also avoiding neglect. Scripture presents angels as real beings actively involved in God’s redemptive history. A proper understanding of Angelology strengthens doctrinal clarity and spiritual maturity. This foundation prepares students to approach the subject with balance and reverence.

1.1 Definition of Angelology

Angelology comes from the Greek word angelos, meaning “messenger.” It is the branch of theology that studies angels, their origin, nature, ministry, fall, and destiny. The subject includes both elect angels and fallen angels under Satan. It does not rely on mythology, mysticism, or imagination, but on divine revelation. Sound doctrine must always be derived from Scripture (2 Timothy 3:16).

1.2 Biblical Foundation

The Bible contains hundreds of references to angels from Genesis to Revelation. They appear in key redemptive events such as the Fall (Genesis 3:24), the Law (Acts 7:53), the incarnation (Luke 1:26–38), and the second coming (Matthew 25:31). Both narrative and didactic portions of Scripture affirm their reality. Therefore, Angelology is not peripheral but integrated into biblical theology.

1.3 Importance of the Study

A correct understanding prevents angel worship (Colossians 2:18) and fear-driven superstition. It also guards believers from ignoring spiritual warfare (Ephesians 6:12). The doctrine ultimately magnifies God, not angels. All angelic activity is under divine authority.

2. The Existence of Angels

This section establishes the reality of angels through biblical testimony. Their existence is not symbolic but historical and doctrinal. Both Testaments provide consistent evidence of angelic appearances and activity. Most importantly, Christ Himself affirmed their existence. Recognizing the reality of angels strengthens confidence in Scripture and awareness of the unseen realm.

2.1 Old Testament Evidence

Angels appear throughout Israel’s history. They guarded Eden (Genesis 3:24), ministered to Hagar (Genesis 16:7), delivered Lot (Genesis 19:1), and protected Daniel (Daniel 6:22). The Psalms frequently reference angelic protection (Psalm 34:7; 91:11). These accounts are presented as historical events, not allegory.

2.2 New Testament Evidence

Angels announced the birth of John and Jesus (Luke 1:11–38). They ministered to Christ after temptation (Matthew 4:11) and announced His resurrection (Matthew 28:5–7). In Acts, angels released apostles from prison (Acts 5:19; 12:7–11). The New Testament church clearly believed in their active ministry.

2.3 Christ’s Testimony

Jesus Christ referred to angels as real beings who worship God (Matthew 18:10), do not marry (Matthew 22:30), and will accompany Him at His return (Matthew 25:31). His teaching confirms their literal existence.

3. The Creation and Origin of Angels

Angels are created beings, not eternal entities. They were brought into existence by God and therefore depend entirely upon Him. This section explains when they were created and their original state of holiness. It also clarifies that angels are numerous beyond human calculation. Understanding their origin reinforces God’s sovereignty over them.

3.1 Created by God

Angels were created by Christ and for Christ (Colossians 1:16; Psalm 148:2,5). They are not divine beings. Their existence points to God’s creative majesty.

3.2 Time of Creation

They were present at the foundation of the earth (Job 38:4–7). This indicates they were created before humanity.

3.3 Number of Angels

Scripture describes them as innumerable (Daniel 7:10; Hebrews 12:22; Revelation 5:11). Their vast number reflects the grandeur of heaven.

3.4 Original Holiness

All angels were created good (Genesis 1:31). Their fall was not due to defect in creation but misuse of will (Jude 6).

4. The Nature and Characteristics of Angels

This section examines what angels are like in essence and function. Angels are spirit beings with personality, intelligence, and moral responsibility. Though powerful, they are finite and limited. They differ from humans in nature and purpose. Recognizing their characteristics prevents exaggeration of their abilities.

4.1 Spirit Beings

Angels are ministering spirits (Hebrews 1:14). Though essentially spiritual, they can appear visibly (Genesis 18:2; John 20:12).

4.2 Personality

They possess intellect (1 Peter 1:12), emotions (Luke 15:10), and will (Jude 6). These qualities demonstrate personal existence, not impersonal force.

4.3 Power and Limitations

They excel in strength (Psalm 103:20). One angel destroyed 185,000 Assyrians (2 Kings 19:35). Yet they lack omniscience (Matthew 24:36) and operate under divine authority.

4.4 Immortality and Distinction from Humans

They do not die (Luke 20:36) and do not marry (Matthew 22:30). Humans do not become angels after death.

5. Organization and Classification of Holy Angels

Angels function in divine order rather than chaos. Scripture indicates ranks, roles, and structured authority. Certain categories are specifically mentioned. This organization reflects God’s nature as a God of order. Understanding classification helps interpret biblical references accurately.

5.1 Archangel

Michael is identified as the archangel (Jude 9; 1 Thessalonians 4:16). He appears as a defender of God’s people (Daniel 10:13).

5.2 Cherubim

Associated with guarding God’s holiness (Genesis 3:24; Ezekiel 10). They are closely connected with God’s throne.

5.3 Seraphim

Described in Isaiah 6:1–7 as proclaiming God’s holiness. Their ministry centers on worship.

5.4 Living Creatures

Described in Revelation 4:6–8 as surrounding God’s throne in ceaseless praise.

5.5 Elect Angels

Holy angels who did not fall (1 Timothy 5:21). They remain faithful servants.

6. The Ministry of Holy Angels

Holy angels actively serve God’s redemptive purposes. Their ministry extends to God, Christ, believers, nations, and final judgment. They worship, deliver messages, protect, and execute divine commands. Their service demonstrates obedience and submission. This section highlights the breadth of their work.

6.1 Ministry to God

They worship continually (Isaiah 6:3; Revelation 5:11–12). They serve in His presence (Daniel 7:10). Their existence centers on glorifying God.

6.2 Ministry to Christ

They announced His birth (Luke 2:8–14), strengthened Him (Luke 22:43), and will accompany Him at His return (Matthew 25:31). Their ministry affirms Christ’s authority.

6.3 Ministry to Believers

They minister to heirs of salvation (Hebrews 1:14). They protect (Psalm 34:7), deliver (Acts 12:7–11), and observe church worship (1 Corinthians 11:10).

6.4 Ministry in Judgment

Angels execute divine judgment (Genesis 19:13; Revelation 8–9). They carry out God’s righteous decrees.

7. Satan: Origin and Fall

Satan was originally a high-ranking created being. His fall was rooted in pride and rebellion. This section examines biblical passages describing his original position and downfall. His rebellion initiated spiritual conflict in the universe. Understanding his fall explains the origin of evil in the angelic realm.

7.1 Original Position

Satan is described symbolically in Ezekiel 28:12–15 and Isaiah 14:12–15 as exalted before his fall.

7.2 Cause of Fall

Pride and self-exaltation (1 Timothy 3:6). He desired equality with God (Isaiah 14:13–14).

7.3 Result of Fall

He was cast down (Luke 10:18; Revelation 12:7–9). His authority became corrupt and adversarial.

8. The Work of Satan

Satan actively opposes God’s plan across history. His hostility is directed toward God, Christ, unbelievers, and believers. He employs deception, accusation, temptation, and counterfeit religion. Though powerful, he is neither omnipotent nor sovereign. This section carefully categorizes his operations.

8.1 Against God

He opposes God’s truth (Genesis 3:1–5). He promotes false worship (2 Corinthians 11:14).

8.2 Against Christ

He tempted Christ (Matthew 4:1–11). He influenced Judas (Luke 22:3).

8.3 Against Unbelievers

He blinds minds (2 Corinthians 4:4). He deceives nations (Revelation 20:3).

8.4 Against Believers

He accuses (Revelation 12:10), tempts (1 Thessalonians 3:5), and seeks to devour (1 Peter 5:8). He attempts to divide and discourage.

9. Fallen Angels (Demons)

Demons are angels who followed Satan in rebellion. They share in his opposition to God’s purposes. Scripture presents them as personal and active spirits. Their power is real but limited by divine authority. Christ’s victory guarantees their ultimate defeat.

9.1 Their Fall

Revelation 12:4 and Jude 6 describe their rebellion and judgment.

9.2 Their Activity

They afflict (Matthew 10:1) and promote false doctrine (1 Timothy 4:1).

9.3 Their Limitation

They operate only within God’s permission (Job 1:12). Christ triumphed over them (Colossians 2:15).

10. The Believer’s Defense Against Satan

Spiritual warfare is a present reality for believers. However, Scripture provides clear instruction for victory. The believer’s strength rests in Christ, not personal ability. God supplies armor, authority, and spiritual resources. This section outlines biblical strategies for resisting Satan.

10.1 The Armor of God

Ephesians 6:10–18 describes truth, righteousness, faith, salvation, the Word, and prayer.

10.2 Resistance and Submission

Submit to God and resist the devil (James 4:7; 1 Peter 5:9).

10.3 Use of Scripture

Christ modeled victory through Scripture (Matthew 4:4,7,10).

10.4 Watchfulness and Prayer

Spiritual vigilance is essential (Matthew 26:41).

11. Destiny of Satan and Fallen Angels

Though presently active, Satan’s future is certain. Scripture predicts his binding, judgment, and eternal punishment. Fallen angels share in this destiny. God’s justice will ultimately prevail. This section emphasizes divine sovereignty over evil.

11.1 Binding and Judgment

Revelation 20:1–3 describes his temporary binding.

11.2 Final Doom

He will be cast into the lake of fire (Revelation 20:10).

11.3 Judgment of Fallen Angels

They are reserved for judgment (Matthew 25:41; Jude 6).

12. Angels in Eschatology

Angels play a major role in end-time events. They accompany Christ, gather the elect, and execute judgment. Their ministry extends into eternity. Eschatology cannot be fully understood without angelic participation. This section completes the doctrine by looking at their future role.

12.1 Second Coming

Angels accompany Christ (Matthew 25:31).

12.2 Gathering and Separation

They gather the elect (Matthew 24:31) and separate the wicked (Matthew 13:39–41).

12.3 Eternal Worship

They continue in worship before God (Revelation 7:11–12).

Summary

Angelology reveals a structured and active spiritual realm under God’s sovereign rule. Holy angels faithfully execute divine purposes. Satan and fallen angels oppose God but remain defeated foes. Believers are called to stand firm in Christ’s victory.

This doctrine deepens reverence for God, sharpens spiritual awareness, and strengthens confidence in the triumph of Christ.

9

Anthropology

The Doctrine of Man

CHAPTER 1 — THE SCOPE AND FOUNDATION OF ANTHROPOLOGY

1.1 What is Theological Anthropology?

The word anthropology comes from two Greek words:

anthropos (man)

logos (study or doctrine)

In theology, anthropology refers to the study of man as revealed by God in Scripture. It is not merely a scientific or philosophical study of human beings, but a theological understanding of who man is in relation to God.

This distinction is important. Modern academic anthropology studies culture, evolution, and behavior. But biblical anthropology asks deeper questions:

Who created man?

Why does man exist?

What is man’s relationship with God?

Why is man sinful?

What is man’s final destiny?

Thus, anthropology is not an isolated doctrine. It is deeply connected to:

The doctrine of God (Theology Proper)

The doctrine of creation (Cosmology)

The doctrine of sin (Hamartiology)

The doctrine of salvation (Soteriology)

If we misunderstand man, we will misunderstand all of these.

1.2 Anthropology and Cosmology: An Inseparable Relationship

The study of man cannot be separated from the study of the universe. This is because our understanding of human origin depends on our understanding of the universe’s origin.

Consider the following logical connections:

If the universe is accidental, then man is also accidental.

If the universe is created, then man is created.

If God is eternal, then man is accountable to Him.

This means anthropology is not neutral. It is shaped by worldview.

Why This Matters

If we begin with a wrong view of the universe, we will end with a wrong view of man. For example:

If matter is all that exists, then man is just matter.

If there is no Creator, then there is no ultimate purpose.

If there is no purpose, then morality becomes subjective.

But Scripture begins with a radically different foundation:

“In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth” (Genesis 1:1)

This statement immediately establishes:

God as Creator

Creation as intentional

Man as dependent

Therefore, anthropology must begin with God, not man.

1.3 The Fundamental Questions of Human Existence

Every human being, regardless of culture, education, or religion, wrestles with certain foundational questions. These are not optional questions. They are unavoidable.

1. Origin — Where did we come from?

This question addresses our beginning. Are we:

Created by God?

Products of evolution?

Eternal beings?

The answer determines whether life has purpose or randomness.

2. Nature — What are we really like?

This question examines human identity. Are we:

Basically good?

Basically sinful?

Merely biological organisms?

The Bible teaches that man is both:

Created good

Fallen in sin

Without this balance, we either become:

Too optimistic (ignoring sin), or

Too pessimistic (denying God’s image)

3. Purpose — Why were we created?

This is perhaps the most important question. If man is self-created, then man defines his own purpose. But if God created man, then God defines man’s purpose.

Scripture clearly answers:

“Everyone… whom I created for my glory” (Isaiah 43:7)

Man exists not for himself, but for God’s glory.

4. Problem — What went wrong?

Human life is clearly broken:

Suffering exists

Evil exists

Death exists

Why?

The Bible answers this through the doctrine of sin. Without this explanation, human suffering remains a mystery.

5. Destiny — What is our future?

What happens after death?

Nothing?

Reincarnation?

Judgment?

The answer shapes how we live today.

6. Meaning — Where do we find fulfillment?

Even people who achieve wealth, power, and success often feel empty. Why?

Because man was not created for temporary satisfaction, but for eternal relationship with God.

1.4 The Universality of These Questions

These questions are not limited to Christians or theologians. They are universal.

Every culture asks them

Every religion tries to answer them

Every philosopher reflects on them

Every human being lives according to some answer

Even a person who says, “I don’t believe in doctrine,” still lives by a practical doctrine of man.

Important Insight

A person’s life is shaped not by what they claim to believe, but by what they actually assume about:

God

Life

Purpose

Morality

This means anthropology is not theoretical. It is deeply practical.

1.5 The Limits of Human Systems

Throughout history, humans have tried to answer these questions through different systems:

Philosophy reasons about existence

Religion searches for the divine

History observes human events

Tradition passes down beliefs

Experience interprets feelings

Science measures physical reality

Each of these contributes something valuable. However, they all share a limitation:

They begin with man, not God.

Why Human Systems Are Limited

Human systems cannot fully answer:

Why we exist

What ultimate purpose is

What happens after death

Because:

Human knowledge is limited

Human reasoning is affected by sin

Human perspective is finite

1.6 Revelation: God Speaks About Man

The Bible presents a completely different approach. Instead of man searching upward, God speaks downward.

“Let us make man in our image…” (Genesis 1:26)

This is the foundation of biblical anthropology.

Key Principle

We do not discover truth about man by:

Observation alone

Reason alone

Experience alone

We know the truth about man because God has revealed it.

Implication

This means:

Anthropology is not speculation

Anthropology is revelation-based

Anthropology is authoritative

1.7 Why the Doctrine of Man Matters

This doctrine is not optional. It is foundational to the entire Christian faith.

Consider the following chain:

If we misunderstand man → we misunderstand sin

If we misunderstand sin → we misunderstand salvation

If we misunderstand salvation → we misunderstand Christ

Example

If man is basically good:
→ Then sin is not serious
→ Then salvation is not necessary
→ Then Christ’s death becomes unnecessary

But if man is:

Created in God’s image

Fallen into sin

Separated from God

Then:

Sin is serious

Salvation is necessary

Christ is essential

1.8 Conclusion

The doctrine of man answers one of the most important questions in theology:

Who are we, according to God?

The Bible teaches that man is:

Created by God

Made in His image

Designed for His glory

Fallen in sin

In need of redemption

This foundation prepares us to examine the next major issue:

How did man originate?

CHAPTER 2 — COMPETING VIEWS ON THE ORIGIN OF MAN

2.1 The Search for Human Origins

From the beginning of human history, people have tried to understand where we came from. This search is not merely intellectual; it is deeply connected to identity and purpose.

Different disciplines approach this question differently:

Philosophy uses reasoning to explain existence

Science studies observable processes

Religion seeks divine explanation

History traces human development

Tradition preserves inherited beliefs

Each of these provides partial insights, but none can give a complete and final answer on their own.

The Core Issue

The central problem is this:

Can human reasoning alone explain human origin?

The Bible answers: No.
True understanding comes through divine revelation, not human speculation.

2.2 Two Foundational Worldviews

All views on human origin ultimately fall into two broad categories:

1. Atheistic / Naturalistic View

2. Theistic (Biblical) View

These are not just scientific positions; they are worldviews that shape how we understand reality.

2.3 The Atheistic / Naturalistic View

This view begins with one fundamental assumption:

There is no Creator.

Core Beliefs

Matter is the ultimate reality

The universe has no divine purpose

Life arose through natural processes

Man is an advanced animal

Morality is socially constructed

In this view, human beings are not uniquely created. Instead, they are the result of impersonal processes over time.

2.4 Origin of Life in Naturalism (Abiogenesis)

Naturalism teaches that life began through a process called abiogenesis, which means life from non-life.

The proposed sequence is:

Simple chemicals existed on early earth

Energy sources (lightning, heat, radiation) caused reactions

These reactions produced organic molecules

Organic molecules formed complex structures (like RNA)

These structures eventually became living cells

In simple terms:

Chemicals → molecules → complex systems → life

2.5 The Central Problem of Abiogenesis

At first glance, this explanation seems scientific. However, the real challenge is not the presence of chemicals.

The real question is:

How did non-living matter become organized, meaningful, and alive?

The Problem of Information

Living cells contain DNA, which carries information. This is not random—it is structured, meaningful, and functional.

Consider this illustration:

If you throw thousands of letters into the air, will they fall into:

A meaningful sentence?

A complete book?

Obviously not.

Why?

Because information requires order, structure, and purpose.

The Key Issue

Abiogenesis must explain how matter became:

Information-bearing

Self-replicating

Self-regulating

Functionally integrated

This is not simply a chemical problem. It is a design problem.

2.6 Challenges to Evolution

Even if one assumes life already exists, evolution attempts to explain how simple life became complex.

However, several major questions remain:

1. Origin of Information

Mutations can alter existing information, but:

Can they create entirely new, complex information?

2. Transitional Forms

Evolution predicts gradual transitions, yet:

Clear, continuous transitions are debated

3. Human Consciousness

Even if biological evolution explains physical development, can it explain:

Self-awareness

Rational thinking

Abstract reasoning (mathematics, philosophy)

Moral responsibility

Religious awareness

These qualities go beyond mere biology.

2.7 The Theistic (Biblical) View

In contrast, the Bible presents a completely different foundation.

Core Beliefs

God is ultimate reality

The universe had a beginning

Creation is intentional

Life is designed

Man is created by divine act

This view does not begin with matter. It begins with God.

2.8 Revelation as the Foundation

The Bible declares:

“Let us make man in our image…” (Genesis 1:26)

This statement reveals that:

Man is not accidental

Man is intentionally created

Man reflects God

The origin of man is therefore not a mystery to be guessed, but a truth to be received by revelation.

2.9 Conclusion

The difference between these two views is not small. It is foundational.

Naturalism Biblical Theism
No Creator God is Creator
Life is accidental Life is intentional
Man is animal Man is image-bearer
No ultimate purpose God-defined purpose

This chapter leads us to the next question: What does Scripture specifically teach about creation?

CHAPTER 3 — THE BIBLICAL DOCTRINE OF CREATION

3.1 Introduction: Why Creation Matters

The doctrine of creation is not merely about how the world began. It forms the foundation of all Christian theology.

If creation is misunderstood:

Human identity becomes unclear

Sin loses its historical basis

Salvation becomes unnecessary

Christ’s work loses its meaning

Thus, creation is not a secondary doctrine. It is structural to the entire biblical worldview.

3.2 The Biblical Foundation of Creation

The Bible begins with a clear declaration:

“In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth” (Genesis 1:1)

This verse establishes several foundational truths:

1. Creation Has a Beginning

The universe is not eternal. It had a starting point.

2. God is Pre-existent

God exists before and independent of creation.

3. Creation is Intentional

The universe is not random. It is designed.

4. Creation is Dependent

Everything that exists depends on God for its existence.

3.3 Creation by Divine Word

Genesis 1 repeatedly emphasizes:

“And God said…”

This reveals that creation is:

Effortless for God

Governed by His authority

Ordered by His wisdom

“By the word of the Lord the heavens were made” (Psalm 33:6)

Creation is therefore not a struggle, but a sovereign command.

3.4 The Special Creation of Man

Unlike the rest of creation, man is formed uniquely:

“The Lord God formed man from the dust… and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life” (Genesis 2:7)

This reveals a dual origin:

Material → formed from dust

Immaterial → given the breath of life

Thus, man is both:

Physical

Spiritual

This distinguishes man from all other creatures.

3.5 The Need for Interpretation

While the Bible clearly teaches that God created, questions arise regarding how to understand the “days” of creation in Genesis 1.

Over time, theologians have proposed various interpretations. Your PPT correctly identifies five major theistic interpretations, which we will now examine in depth.

3.6 THE FIVE THEISTIC INTERPRETATIONS OF CREATION

3.6.1 Alternate Day-Age Theory

Explanation

This theory proposes that:

The six days of creation are literal 24-hour days

But between each day, there are long periods of time (millions of years)

So the sequence becomes:
Day → long gap → next day → long gap → and so on

Why It Is Proposed

This view attempts to:

Maintain literal “days”

Also accommodate geological ages

Theological Evaluation

While creative, this view faces serious problems:

1. Lack of Biblical Evidence

Genesis does not mention any gaps between the days.

2. Literary Structure of Genesis

The repeated phrase:

“Evening and morning”
suggests continuity, not interruption.

3. Artificial Reading

The theory introduces something into the text that is not naturally present.

Conclusion

The Alternate Day-Age Theory is:

An attempt at harmonization

But lacks strong textual support

3.6.2 Day-Age Theory

Explanation

This theory interprets each “day” (Hebrew: yom) as a long geological period, not a literal 24-hour day.

Arguments Used

The word yom can mean a long period

Psalm 90:4 / 2 Peter 3:8 suggest:

“A thousand years… like a day”

Theological Problems

1. Conflict with Exodus 20:11

The creation week is directly linked to the human work week:

Six days of work

One day of rest

This suggests ordinary days, not long ages.

2. Order of Creation

The sequence in Genesis does not match evolutionary timelines.

3. Death Before Sin

If ages include death before Adam:

This contradicts Romans 5:12

Death becomes natural, not a result of sin

Conclusion

The Day-Age Theory:

Attempts to align with science

But weakens the natural reading of Scripture

Raises serious theological concerns

3.6.3 Eden Only Theory

Explanation

This theory suggests:

Genesis describes only the creation of the Garden of Eden

The rest of humanity developed elsewhere (possibly through evolution)

Why It Is Proposed

This view tries to:

Combine biblical narrative with evolutionary theory

Preserve Adam while allowing broader human evolution

Theological Problems

1. Contradicts Biblical Unity of Humanity

“From one man He made all nations” (Acts 17:26)

This affirms:

A single origin for all humanity

2. Undermines Original Sin

If humans existed outside Adam:

Are they sinners through Adam?

Are they included in salvation through Christ?

This creates major doctrinal confusion.

3. No Strong Scriptural Support

The text of Genesis presents:

A universal account

Not a localized one

Conclusion

The Eden Only Theory:

Attempts compromise

But undermines core doctrines like sin and salvation

3.6.4 Revelation Days Theory

Explanation

This theory teaches:

Creation occurred over long ages

But God revealed it to Moses in six literal days

Why It Is Proposed

This view tries to:

Preserve six literal days

While allowing scientific timelines

Theological Problems

1. Misreading of the Text

Genesis does not say:

“God revealed in six days”

It clearly says:

“God created in six days”

2. Adds External Idea

This theory introduces something not found in the text.

Conclusion

The Revelation Days Theory is:

Speculative

Not supported by the natural reading of Scripture

3.6.5 Gap Theory (Ruin–Reconstruction Theory)

Explanation

This theory proposes:

Genesis 1:1 → Original perfect creation

A long time gap follows

A catastrophic event (often linked to Satan’s fall) destroys the earth

Genesis 1:2 onward → God reconstructs creation in six days

Why It Is Proposed

This view attempts to:

Allow for geological ages

Maintain six literal days

Arguments Used

“Formless and void” suggests judgment

Allows explanation of fossils and ancient earth

Theological Problems

1. Weak Linguistic Support

The Hebrew text does not require a gap.

2. Contradiction with Isaiah 45:18

God did not create the earth:

“formless” or chaotic

3. Death Before Adam

If fossils predate Adam:

Death exists before sin

This contradicts Romans 5:12

Conclusion

The Gap Theory:

Was historically popular

But lacks strong exegetical support

Creates theological inconsistencies

3.7 The Evangelical Doctrine of Creation

After evaluating these theories, evangelical theology affirms several non-negotiable truths:

3.7.1 Creation by Divine Act

Genesis 1:1 — God created

Creation is:

Not accidental

Not self-generated

Not eternal

3.7.2 Creation by Divine Word

God creates through command, demonstrating:

Authority

Power

Intentionality

3.7.3 Historical Adam

Adam is:

A real person

The first human

The representative head

Supported by:

Luke 3:38

Romans 5

1 Corinthians 15

3.7.4 Death Through Sin

Romans 5:12

Death is:

Not natural

Not original

But the result of sin

3.7.5 Christ as the Second Adam

Christ:

Restores what Adam lost

Represents redeemed humanity

3.8 Theological Synthesis

Creation must be understood as:

Historical

Theological

Purposeful

Christ-centered

It is not merely about beginnings, but about:

Identity

Responsibility

Redemption

3.9 Conclusion

The doctrine of creation teaches that:

God is the source of all existence

Man is intentionally created

Humanity is unified in Adam

Sin disrupted creation

Christ restores creation

Thus, creation is not only about the past.
It is the foundation for understanding:

Who we are

Why we exist

What God is doing in redemption

CHAPTER 4 — THE NATURE OF LIFE

4.1 What is Life?

Scientifically, life is defined by several characteristics:

Organization

Metabolism

Growth

Response

Reproduction

Genetic information

However, these descriptions do not fully explain what life is.

4.2 Life is More Than Chemicals

A living cell contains:

Structure

Function

Information

But a dead cell still contains the same chemicals.

What is missing?

Coordination

Energy regulation

Functional unity

Illustration

A computer without power still has:

Hardware

Software

But it does not function.

In the same way:
Life is not just components, but organized activity.

4.3 Biblical Understanding of Life

Scripture provides the ultimate explanation:

“God breathed into his nostrils the breath of life” (Genesis 2:7)

Life is not accidental. It is:

Given by God

Sustained by God

Dependent on God

4.4 Conclusion

Life cannot be fully explained by science alone. It must be understood through God’s revelation.

CHAPTER 5 — THE IMAGE OF GOD (IMAGO DEI)

5.1 Introduction: The Foundational Identity of Man

The doctrine of the Imago Dei (Image of God) is the central truth of biblical anthropology. Before Scripture describes what man does, what man feels, or even what man has become through sin, it first defines who man is:

“Let us make man in our image, after our likeness” (Genesis 1:26)

This statement is not incidental. It is foundational.

Why This Matters

Every other doctrine about man depends on this truth:

Human dignity depends on the image

Human sin is serious because it is rebellion by an image-bearer

Salvation is restoration of the image

Christ is the perfect image

If the doctrine of the image of God is misunderstood, the entire structure of theology becomes unstable.

5.2 Exegetical Foundations of the Imago Dei

5.2.1 Genesis 1:26–27

The primary text reads:

“So God created man in his own image, in the image of God he created him; male and female he created them.”

This passage reveals several key truths:

1. The Image is Universal

All human beings bear the image of God, not just believers.

2. The Image is Essential

It is not something added to man; it defines what man is.

3. The Image is Shared by Both Sexes

Both male and female equally bear the image.

5.2.2 Hebrew Terms: “Image” and “Likeness”

Tselem (Image) → representation, statue, visible expression

Demuth (Likeness) → similarity, resemblance

Together, they emphasize that man is:

A real representation of God

Not identical to God

But meaningfully reflective of Him

5.3 Historical Development of the Doctrine

Understanding how the church has interpreted the image helps us think more clearly.

5.3.1 Early Church (Patristic View)

Church fathers often distinguished between:

Image → retained after the fall

Likeness → lost and restored through salvation

This distinction helped explain:

Human dignity after sin

The need for redemption

5.3.2 Medieval Theology

Thinkers like Augustine emphasized:

The image in the soul, especially:

Memory

Understanding

Will

This focused on the inner structure of man.

5.3.3 Reformation Theology

Reformers like Calvin emphasized:

The image includes the whole person

The image is corrupted by sin, not destroyed

They also stressed:

True knowledge of God is part of the image

5.3.4 Modern Evangelical View

Most evangelical theologians today adopt an integrated view, recognizing that the image includes:

What man is (structure)

How man relates (relationship)

What man does (function)

5.4 Major Theological Views of the Image

To understand the doctrine deeply, we must examine the main interpretations.

5.4.1 The Substantive View (Structural View)

This view teaches that the image of God consists in certain qualities within man, such as:

Rationality

Morality

Spiritual capacity

Strength

It recognizes real differences between humans and animals.

Limitation

It risks reducing the image to only one aspect of man, rather than the whole person.

5.4.2 The Relational View

This view emphasizes that the image of God is expressed in:

Relationship with God

Relationship with others

It highlights that:

Man reflects the relational nature of the Trinity

Strength

It captures the importance of love, community, and covenant.

Limitation

It may overlook the structural and functional aspects.

5.4.3 The Functional View

This view focuses on what man is called to do:

Rule over creation

Exercise dominion

Represent God on earth

Strength

It connects the image directly to Genesis 1:28 (dominion mandate).

Limitation

It may reduce identity to activity.

5.4.4 The Integrated (Evangelical) View

The most balanced understanding is that the image includes:

Structural (what man is)

Relational (how man connects)

Functional (what man does)

Key Insight

Man images God not in one isolated way, but as a whole person living before God.

5.5 The Dimensions of the Image (Expanded Theology)

5.5.1 The Spiritual Dimension (God-ward Orientation)

Man has the unique capacity to:

Know God

Worship God

Respond to divine revelation

Ecclesiastes 3:11 — God has set eternity in the human heart

This explains why:

Humans seek transcendence

Religion exists in all cultures

Theological Insight

This dimension reflects that man is:
created for communion with God

5.5.2 The Rational Dimension (Cognitive Capacity)

Man possesses:

Abstract reasoning

Logical thinking

Language

Creativity

Isaiah 1:18 — “Come, let us reason together”

Theological Significance

Human rationality reflects:

God’s wisdom

God’s order

This is why:

Science is possible

Learning is meaningful

Truth can be known

5.5.3 The Moral Dimension (Ethical Awareness)

Humans possess:

Conscience

Moral awareness

Sense of justice

Romans 2:14–15 — the law written on the heart

Theological Insight

Man is not morally neutral. He is:

Accountable

Responsible

Answerable to God

5.5.4 The Relational Dimension (Community and Love)

Man is created for:

Relationship

Community

Covenant

Genesis 2:18 — “It is not good for man to be alone”

Trinitarian Reflection

Because God is relational (Father, Son, Spirit), man reflects:

Love

Fellowship

Unity

5.5.5 The Functional Dimension (Dominion and Stewardship)

Man is given authority:

“Fill the earth and subdue it” (Genesis 1:28)

This includes:

Stewardship

Cultural development

Responsible rule

Key Distinction

Rational = ability

Functional = responsibility

Man is not only capable—he is commissioned.

5.6 What the Image of God Is NOT

Clarifying misunderstandings is crucial.

The image is NOT:

1. Physical Form

God is spirit (John 4:24)

2. Divinity

Man reflects God but is not God

3. Moral Perfection

Man can sin

4. A Single Part (like the soul only)

The image refers to the whole person

5.7 The Image and Human Dignity

The doctrine of the image of God is the foundation of human worth.

Ethical Implications

All humans are equal

Racism is sinful

Caste superiority is unbiblical

Life is sacred (including unborn life)

James 3:9 — Humans are made in God’s likeness

5.8 The Image After the Fall

One of the most important theological questions is:

What happened to the image after sin?

5.8.1 The Image is Not Destroyed

Genesis 9:6 (after the fall) affirms the image
James 3:9 confirms it again

Thus:

Man is still an image-bearer

5.8.2 The Image is Distorted

The fall results in:

Moral corruption

Relational breakdown

Functional failure

5.8.3 Reformed Theological Distinction

Image (broad sense) → retained

Likeness (moral sense) → damaged

5.9 Christ as the Perfect Image

Colossians 1:15 — Christ is the image of the invisible God

Christ is:

The perfect revelation of God

The true human

5.10 Restoration of the Image in Salvation

Salvation is not only forgiveness. It is:

Restoration of the image of God

Romans 8:29 — conformed to the image of Christ

Three Stages

Justification → legal restoration

Sanctification → moral restoration

Glorification → complete restoration

5.11 Theological Importance of the Imago Dei

Without this doctrine:

Sin loses seriousness

Human dignity collapses

Christ’s incarnation loses meaning

Salvation loses purpose

5.12 Conclusion

The image of God defines:

Who man is

Why man matters

What man has lost

What Christ restores

Final Insight

Man is not merely:

Biological

Psychological

Social

Man is fundamentally:
Theological

He is:

Created by God

Designed to reflect God

Restored through Christ

CHAPTER 6 — MALE AND FEMALE DESIGN

6.1 Introduction: Gender in the Doctrine of Man

The doctrine of man cannot be fully understood without addressing the reality that:

“Male and female He created them” (Genesis 1:27)

This statement is not a minor detail in creation. It is a theological declaration. Gender is not an afterthought or a cultural development. It is part of God’s intentional design for humanity.

To understand man rightly, we must understand:

why God created two sexes

how they relate to each other

what this reveals about God Himself

6.2 Gender as a Creational Reality

6.2.1 Created by God, Not Constructed by Culture

Scripture presents gender as something:

given, not chosen

created, not constructed

Genesis 1:27 — “God created man in His own image… male and female He created them”

This means:

Gender is rooted in creation, not society

It reflects divine intention, not human preference

Theological Insight

To reject God’s design of male and female is not merely a social issue. It is a theological issue, because it challenges the Creator’s authority.

6.3 The Equality of Male and Female

6.3.1 Equal in the Image of God

The Bible clearly teaches that both man and woman are:

Created in God’s image

Equal in dignity

Equal in worth

Genesis 1:27 — both male and female are created in God’s image

This was a radical truth, especially in ancient cultures where women were often seen as inferior.

6.3.2 Equal in Redemption

The New Testament affirms this equality:

Galatians 3:28 — “There is neither male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus”

This does not erase gender distinctions, but it establishes:

Equal standing before God

Equal access to salvation

Equal value in Christ

6.3.3 Theological Clarification

Equality does not mean:

sameness

identical roles

Instead, it means:

equal dignity

equal worth

equal image-bearing

Key Principle

Equality of essence does not eliminate distinction of function

6.4 Complementarity: Designed Difference

6.4.1 Created Different, Not Deficient

After creating Adam, God declares:

Genesis 2:18 — “It is not good that the man should be alone”

This is the first statement in creation where something is “not good.”

Why?

Because man alone does not fully express:

relational fullness

human completeness

Thus, God creates woman.

6.4.2 The Meaning of “Helper” (Ezer)

The woman is described as:

“A helper fit for him” (Genesis 2:18)

The Hebrew word ezer does NOT mean inferior assistant. In fact:

It is often used for God Himself (Psalm 33:20)

Thus, the woman is:

a strong partner

a corresponding counterpart

an essential companion

6.4.3 Complementarity Defined

Complementarity means:

difference with harmony

distinction with unity

interdependence without inequality

Biblical Pattern

Man and woman together reflect God’s image

Neither is complete in isolation

6.5 The Relational Dimension: Covenant and Community

6.5.1 Marriage as Divine Institution

God establishes marriage in creation:

Genesis 2:24 — “The two shall become one flesh”

Marriage is:

not a human invention

but a divine institution

6.5.2 One Flesh Union

This union includes:

physical union

emotional unity

spiritual covenant

It reflects:

deep intimacy

mutual belonging

shared identity

6.5.3 Marriage as Theological Symbol

The New Testament reveals a deeper meaning:

Ephesians 5:31–32 — Marriage reflects Christ and the Church

This means:

Marriage is not only social

It is Christological (points to Christ)

Theological Insight

Gender and marriage are living illustrations of the gospel:

Christ (husband) → sacrificial love

Church (wife) → responsive devotion

6.6 The Reproductive Dimension: Participation in Creation

6.6.1 The Mandate to Multiply

God commands:

Genesis 1:28 — “Be fruitful and multiply”

This reveals that:

Human reproduction is intentional

It is part of God’s design

6.6.2 Participation in God’s Creative Work

Through reproduction:

Humans participate in God’s creative purpose

New image-bearers are brought into existence

This is not merely biological. It is theological.

6.6.3 Beyond Biology: Spiritual Fruitfulness

Reproduction also has a spiritual dimension:

Discipleship

Church growth

Spiritual multiplication

Matthew 28:19 — “Make disciples of all nations”

6.7 The Trinity and Human Relationships

6.7.1 Reflection of the Triune God

God is:

One in essence

Three in persons

Similarly:

Humanity is one (human nature)

Expressed in relational plurality

6.7.2 Unity in Diversity

Male and female together reflect:

unity

diversity

relationship

This mirrors:

the relational nature of the Trinity

6.8 Distortion of Gender Through Sin

6.8.1 Effects of the Fall

Sin distorts:

relationships

roles

identity

Genesis 3:16 — conflict enters the male-female relationship

6.8.2 Distortions Seen Today

Because of sin:

domination replaces love

confusion replaces clarity

independence replaces interdependence

6.8.3 Theological Insight

Gender confusion is not merely cultural. It reflects:

deeper spiritual disorder

rebellion against God’s design

6.9 Restoration in Christ

6.9.1 Christ Restores Relationships

Through salvation:

relationships are healed

love is restored
