purpose is renewed

6.9.2 New Identity in Christ

Believers are called to:

live according to God’s design

reflect Christ in relationships

6.9.3 Marriage Redeemed

Christian marriage becomes:

a testimony of the gospel

a reflection of Christ’s love

6.10 Theological Summary

The doctrine of male and female teaches:

Gender is created by God

Both sexes equally bear God’s image

Differences are intentional and meaningful

Relationships reflect divine nature

Marriage reflects Christ and the Church

Sin distorts gender relationships

Christ restores God’s design

6.11 Conclusion

Male and female are not merely biological categories. They are:

theological realities

covenantal partners

image-bearing reflections of God

Final Insight

To understand gender rightly is to understand:

creation

relationship

redemption

Because ultimately:

Man and woman together reveal the glory of God in ways neither can alone.

CHAPTER 7 — THE CONSTITUTION OF MAN

7.1 Introduction: What is Man Made Of?

One of the central questions in anthropology is:

What constitutes a human being?

Is man:

merely a physical organism?

a soul trapped in a body?

a combination of parts?

The Bible presents a view that is both simple and profound:

“The Lord God formed man from the dust of the ground and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life, and man became a living being” (Genesis 2:7)

This verse provides the foundation for understanding human nature.

7.2 The Twofold Nature of Man

Genesis 2:7 reveals that man has two essential aspects:

1. Material Aspect — “Dust of the ground”

2. Immaterial Aspect — “Breath of life”

These two together form:

“A living being”

7.2.1 Not Two Separate Entities, but One Unified Person

It is important to understand that the Bible does not present man as:

a body plus a soul living independently

Instead, man is a unified being:

embodied

integrated

indivisible in experience

Theological Clarification

Man is not:

a soul trapped in a body (Greek philosophy)

Man is:

an embodied soul

a unified person

7.3 The Material Aspect: The Body

7.3.1 Origin of the Body

Genesis 2:7 — “formed from the dust”

This teaches:

The body is created by God

It is part of creation

It is not eternal

7.3.2 Nature of the Body

The body is:

Physical — interacts with the material world

Finite — limited and dependent

Visible — part of observable creation

Through the body, we:

see, hear, touch

act in the world

relate physically to others

7.3.3 The Goodness of the Body

Genesis 1:31 — “God saw all that He had made, and it was very good”

This is crucial.

The Bible does NOT teach:

the body is evil

matter is corrupt

That idea comes from Greek dualism, not Scripture.

Theological Insight

The body is:

good

created

purposeful

7.3.4 The Purpose of the Body

The body is not incidental. It has divine purpose:

1. Instrument of Obedience

Romans 6:13 — present your members to God

The body is used to:

obey God

serve righteousness

2. Means of Worship

Romans 12:1 — present your bodies as a living sacrifice

Worship is not only spiritual. It is physical and embodied.

3. Future Resurrection

1 Corinthians 15 — the body will be raised

Christian hope is not escape from the body, but:
redemption of the body

7.4 The Immaterial Aspect: Soul and Spirit

7.4.1 Biblical Terminology

Scripture uses several terms to describe the inner life:

Soul

Spirit

Heart

Mind

Will

Conscience

These are not rigid compartments, but different expressions of the inner person.

7.4.2 Functions of the Inner Person

1. Mind (Seat of Thinking)

The mind enables:

reasoning

understanding

reflection

Romans 12:2 — “be transformed by the renewing of your mind”

2. Heart (Seat of Emotions and Desires)

In Scripture, the heart represents:

emotions

desires

inner motivations

Proverbs 4:23 — “Guard your heart”

3. Will (Seat of Decision)

The will is the capacity to:

choose

act

commit

Joshua 24:15 — “Choose this day whom you will serve”

4. Conscience (Moral Awareness)

The conscience:

evaluates actions

produces guilt or approval

Romans 2:15 — conscience bearing witness

5. Spirit (God-awareness)

The spirit enables:

relationship with God

spiritual perception

1 Corinthians 2:11 — the spirit of man

7.5 The Dichotomy vs Trichotomy Debate

This is one of the major theological discussions in anthropology.

7.5.1 Dichotomy View

Man consists of:

Body

Soul/Spirit (interchangeable)

Biblical Support

Scripture often uses:

“soul” and “spirit” interchangeably

7.5.2 Trichotomy View

Man consists of:

Body

Soul

Spirit

1 Thessalonians 5:23 is often cited

7.5.3 Evaluation

While trichotomy highlights distinctions, it risks:

over-dividing human nature

Dichotomy better reflects:

biblical flexibility in terminology

7.5.4 Balanced Biblical Position (Preferred)

The most accurate approach is:

Holistic Dualism

Man has two aspects (material & immaterial)

But functions are described in multiple ways

Key Principle

The Bible emphasizes:
unity without confusion, distinction without separation

7.6 Unity of the Human Person

7.6.1 Against Dualistic Errors

Some philosophies teach:

body = bad

soul = good

This is unbiblical.

7.6.2 Biblical Unity

The Bible presents man as:

integrated

unified

whole

Implications

Sin affects the whole person

Salvation involves the whole person

Worship involves the whole person

7.7 Theological Implications

7.7.1 Sin Affects the Whole Person

Mind → darkened (Ephesians 4:18)

Will → enslaved (Romans 6)

Heart → deceitful (Jeremiah 17:9)

Body → subject to death

7.7.2 Salvation Restores the Whole Person

Mind → renewed

Heart → transformed

Will → freed

Body → resurrected

7.7.3 Resurrection Affirms the Body

Christian hope is not:

escaping the body

But:

glorification of the body

7.8 Why This Doctrine Matters

Understanding the constitution of man protects us from errors:

1. Materialism

(Man is only body)

2. Dualism

(Body is evil)

3. Reductionism

(Man is only psychological or biological)

7.9 Conclusion

The Bible presents man as:

Created from dust

Animated by God’s breath

Living as a unified being

Final Insight

Man is:

physical yet spiritual

earthly yet God-related

finite yet eternal in destiny

He is not divided into disconnected parts, but exists as a whole person before God.

CHAPTER 8 — THE PURPOSE OF MAN

8.1 Introduction: The Question of Purpose

Among all the questions in human life, perhaps the most pressing is:

“Why do we exist?”

This question is not merely philosophical—it is deeply personal. Every human being, whether consciously or unconsciously, seeks:

meaning

fulfillment

direction

purpose

People may achieve:

wealth

education

status

Yet still experience emptiness. This reveals an important truth:

Purpose is not found in achievement, but in alignment with God’s design.

8.2 The Source of Purpose

Before defining purpose, we must ask:

Who determines human purpose?

There are only two possibilities:

1. Man Defines His Own Purpose

This leads to:

subjectivity

confusion

constant change

2. God Defines Human Purpose

This leads to:

objective meaning

stability

ultimate fulfillment

Biblical Answer

Scripture clearly teaches that:

God, as Creator, determines the purpose of man

Revelation 4:11 — “You created all things… and by Your will they exist”

8.3 The Primary Purpose of Man

The Bible gives a clear and unified answer:

Man’s primary purpose is:

To glorify God and to enjoy fellowship with Him

8.3.1 To Glorify God

Isaiah 43:7 — “Everyone… whom I created for my glory”
1 Corinthians 10:31 — “Do all to the glory of God”

What Does It Mean to Glorify God?

To glorify God means:

to reflect His character

to honor His name

to live in obedience to Him

It does NOT mean:

adding something to God’s glory

Rather, it means:

displaying the glory that already belongs to Him

How Do We Glorify God?

We glorify God through:

Worship → acknowledging His worth

Obedience → submitting to His will

Character → reflecting His holiness

Life choices → living according to His truth

Theological Insight

God’s glory is the ultimate purpose of all creation, not just humanity.

However, humans uniquely glorify God:

consciously

willingly

relationally

8.3.2 To Enjoy Fellowship with God

Purpose is not only about duty. It is also about relationship.

Genesis 3:8 — God walking with man
Psalm 16:11 — “In Your presence is fullness of joy”
John 17:3 — eternal life is knowing God

What is Fellowship?

Fellowship means:

personal relationship

communion with God

ongoing interaction

This includes:

prayer

worship

obedience

intimacy with God

Important Insight

Man’s highest purpose is not merely:

working for God

But:

walking with God

Joy as Part of Purpose

Enjoyment is not optional. It is central.

God created man to:

delight in Him

find satisfaction in Him

experience joy in His presence

8.4 Purpose and Identity

Purpose is directly connected to identity.

If man is:

self-created → purpose is self-defined

God-created → purpose is God-defined

Biblical Principle

You cannot understand your purpose until you understand:
who created you

8.5 Secondary Purposes of Man

While glorifying God and enjoying Him is primary, Scripture also reveals several secondary purposes that express this primary goal.

8.5.1 Stewardship of Creation

Genesis 1:28 — “Fill the earth and subdue it”

Meaning of Stewardship

Man is given authority over creation, but this authority is:

delegated

accountable

responsible

Man is not the owner of creation, but a:
steward (manager under God)

Responsibilities Include

caring for the environment

using resources wisely

developing culture and society

Theological Insight

Stewardship reflects:

God’s rule

God’s order

God’s care for creation

8.5.2 Fellowship with Others

Genesis 2:18 — “It is not good for man to be alone”

Human Relational Nature

Man is created for:

community

relationships

companionship

This includes:

family

church

society

Theological Insight

Human relationships reflect:

the relational nature of the Trinity

Practical Implication

Isolation contradicts human design.
We are created to:

love

serve

live in community

8.5.3 Obedient Living

Ecclesiastes 12:13 — “Fear God and keep His commandments”

Meaning of Obedience

Obedience is:

submission to God’s authority

alignment with God’s will

Why Obedience Matters

Obedience is not legalism. It is:

an expression of love

a response to God’s authority

John 14:15 — “If you love me, keep my commandments”

Theological Insight

True purpose cannot be fulfilled apart from obedience.

8.5.4 Reflecting God’s Character

Matthew 5:16 — “Let your light shine…”

What Does This Mean?

Man is called to reflect:

love

holiness

justice

mercy

Theological Insight

Man does not create moral truth. He reflects:
God’s moral nature

8.6 The Distortion of Purpose (Because of Sin)

8.6.1 The Shift from God-Centered to Self-Centered

Sin fundamentally distorts human purpose.

Instead of:

glorifying God

Man begins to:

glorify himself

8.6.2 Worship Replaced by Idolatry

Romans 1:25 — “They worshiped the creature rather than the Creator”

This is the essence of sin:

misdirected worship

8.6.3 Effects of Distortion

Because of sin:

Man avoids God

Man misuses creation

Man breaks relationships

Man loses true meaning

Theological Insight

Sin is not just doing wrong things.
It is living for the wrong purpose.

8.7 Restoration of Purpose in Christ

8.7.1 Christ Restores What Was Lost

Colossians 1:16 — created through Him
Ephesians 2:10 — created for good works

8.7.2 Salvation as Restoration of Purpose

Salvation is not only:

forgiveness of sins

It is:

restoration of identity

restoration of purpose

8.7.3 New Life in Christ

Believers are called to:

live for God’s glory

walk in obedience

reflect Christ

8.8 The Ultimate Fulfillment of Purpose

Human purpose will be fully realized in eternity.

perfect worship

perfect fellowship

perfect joy

8.9 Conclusion

The doctrine of the purpose of man teaches that:

Man is created by God

Man exists for God

Man finds fulfillment in God

Final Insight

Man’s greatest problem is not:

lack of success

But:

misdirected purpose

And man’s greatest need is not:

self-improvement

But:

realignment with God

CHAPTER 9 — DISTORTION AND RESTORATION

9.1 Introduction: What Went Wrong?

Up to this point, we have seen that man is:

created by God

made in His image

designed for relationship

given purpose and responsibility

Yet, human experience tells a different story:

broken relationships

moral failure

suffering and death

inner emptiness

This raises a critical question:

If man was created good, what went wrong?

The Bible answers this through the doctrine of sin and the fall.

9.2 The Nature of Sin

9.2.1 Sin as Rebellion Against God

Sin is not merely:

a mistake

a weakness

a social failure

At its core, sin is:

rebellion against God’s authority

Romans 1:25 — “They exchanged the truth of God for a lie and worshiped the creature rather than the Creator”

9.2.2 Sin as Misdirected Worship

Human beings are created to worship God. Sin occurs when that worship is redirected toward:

self

possessions

power

creation

Thus, sin is fundamentally:
a disorder of love and worship

9.2.3 Sin as Missing the Intended Purpose

The word “sin” (Greek: hamartia) means:

to miss the mark

Man was created for:

God’s glory

Sin causes man to:

live for himself

9.3 The Fall of Man

9.3.1 The Historical Event

The fall is described in Genesis 3.

It is not:

symbolic only

mythological

It is:

historical

theological

foundational

9.3.2 What Happened in the Fall

Adam and Eve:

disobeyed God’s command

chose autonomy over obedience

trusted the creature over the Creator

9.3.3 Theological Meaning of the Fall

The fall represents:

rejection of God’s authority

desire for self-rule

breakdown of trust

9.4 The Effects of Sin on Human Nature

Sin does not affect only one part of man. It affects the entire person.

9.4.1 Total Depravity (Correct Understanding)

This doctrine does NOT mean:

man is as evil as possible

It means:

sin affects every aspect of human nature

9.4.2 Effects on the Mind

Ephesians 4:18 — “darkened in their understanding”

The mind becomes:

confused

resistant to truth

distorted in reasoning

9.4.3 Effects on the Heart

Jeremiah 17:9 — “The heart is deceitful”

The heart becomes:

disordered in desires

inclined toward sin

9.4.4 Effects on the Will

Romans 6:16 — enslaved to sin

The will becomes:

weakened

inclined toward disobedience

9.4.5 Effects on Relationships

Sin breaks:

relationship with God

relationship with others

relationship with creation

Genesis 3 shows:

hiding from God

blaming others

conflict in relationships

9.4.6 Effects on the Body

suffering

decay

death

Romans 5:12 — death entered through sin

9.5 The Noetic Effects of Sin

9.5.1 Sin Affects Thinking

“Noetic” refers to the mind.

Sin causes:

suppression of truth

distorted reasoning

moral blindness

Romans 1:21 — “their thinking became futile”

9.5.2 Implication

Man is not neutral in thinking.
He is:

biased against God

inclined toward self-justification

9.6 The Distortion of Human Purpose

9.6.1 From God-Centered to Self-Centered

Instead of:

glorifying God

Man begins to:

glorify himself

9.6.2 Misuse of Creation

Instead of:

stewarding creation

Man:

exploits

abuses

idolizes creation

9.6.3 Breakdown of Relationships

Instead of:

love and unity

We see:

conflict

division

selfishness

9.6.4 Loss of True Meaning

Without God:

purpose becomes unclear

life feels empty

9.7 The Justice of God and the Problem of Sin

9.7.1 God’s Holiness Requires Judgment

God is:

holy

just

righteous

Therefore:

sin cannot be ignored

9.7.2 The Seriousness of Sin

Sin is serious because:

it is against God

it corrupts His image

it brings death

9.8 Restoration Through Christ

9.8.1 Christ as the Second Adam

1 Corinthians 15:22 — “In Adam all die… in Christ all will be made alive”

Christ:

represents a new humanity

restores what Adam lost

9.8.2 The Work of Christ

Christ restores through:

His life (perfect obedience)

His death (atonement for sin)

His resurrection (victory over death)

9.9 The Three Stages of Restoration

9.9.1 Justification (Legal Restoration)

Declared righteous before God

Sin forgiven

Romans 5:1 — justified by faith

9.9.2 Sanctification (Moral Restoration)

Gradual transformation

Growth in holiness

2 Corinthians 3:18 — being transformed

9.9.3 Glorification (Complete Restoration)

Final perfection

Freedom from sin

resurrection body

9.10 Restoration of the Image and Purpose

Through Christ:

the image of God is renewed

relationships are restored

purpose is realigned

Ephesians 2:10 — created for good works

9.11 The New Life in Christ

Believers are called to:

live for God’s glory

walk in obedience

reflect Christ

9.12 Theological Summary

The doctrine of distortion and restoration teaches:

Man fell through sin

Sin affects the whole person

God judges sin justly

Christ redeems completely

Salvation restores purpose

9.13 Final Conclusion

Human history can be understood in three stages:

Creation → Fall → Redemption

Creation → man made in God’s image

Fall → image distorted

Redemption → image restored in Christ

Final Insight

Man’s greatest tragedy is not:

suffering

But:

separation from God

And man’s greatest hope is not:

self-effort

But:

restoration through Jesus Christ

10

Soteriology

The Doctrine of Salvation

PREFACE

The doctrine of salvation, or Soteriology, occupies the ultimate and central place in Christian theology. While the serious reality and universality of sin cast a dark shadow over human history, the gospel shines as the brilliant light of divine rescue. Any meaningful understanding of God's character, grace, and the work of Jesus Christ culminates in a clear and biblical understanding of how God saves sinners. It is only when the breathtaking depth, scope, and perfection of salvation are rightly grasped that the beauty and necessity of the Christian faith can be fully appreciated. This book has been prepared with that foundational conviction.

Purpose of the Book

The primary purpose of this textbook is to provide a clear, systematic, and biblically grounded study of the doctrine of salvation. It seeks to guide students through the essential aspects of Soteriology, including the divine initiative, the substitutionary atonement, the subjective work of the Holy Spirit, and the multi-faceted benefits of redemption—such as justification, regeneration, adoption, and sanctification—as they unfold from conversion into eternity.

This work is designed not merely to inform the mind but to shape theological understanding in a way that is faithful to Scripture and dynamically applicable to life and ministry. By carefully examining what the Bible teaches about salvation, this book aims to equip students to think theologically, teach accurately, and minister effectively with a gospel-centered focus

Target Audience

This book is specifically written for Fusion Bible College students who are being trained for ministry, teaching, and leadership within the church. It assumes a commitment to the absolute authority of Scripture and seeks to present doctrine in a manner that is both academically responsible and spiritually edifying.

At the same time, the content has been structured in a way that is highly accessible to all serious students of the Word of God. Even those who may not have attended formal classes should be able to follow the material naturally and gain a clear understanding of each concept through its step-by-step development.

Approach: Biblical and Systematic

The approach of this book is both biblical and systematic. Each doctrine is firmly anchored in the teaching of Scripture, with careful exegetical attention given to key passages that form the unshakeable basis of our theological understanding. At the same time, the material is organized systematically, allowing students to trace the logical flow of the ordo salutis (the order of salvation) and see how various aspects of God's redemptive provision relate to one another in a coherent, balanced framework.

Care has been taken to present truth with clarity and precision, while avoiding unnecessary complexity. Definitions, structural explanations, and summary charts are provided to help students grasp both the profound depth and the intensely practical implications of each concept. The ultimate goal is not only to present sound doctrine but to make it thoroughly understandable and teachable for the next generation of leaders.

Encouragement to Students

As you study this subject, it is important to approach it with both humility, reverence, and a spirit of doxology. The doctrine of salvation is not merely an abstract, intellectual puzzle to solve—it is the living reality that secures our eternal destiny. A proper understanding of soteriology should lead the soul away from human pride and anchor it completely in the matchless grace of God.

Let this study lead you to:

A greater awareness of your absolute dependence on the Triune God.

A deeper, unending gratitude for the complete salvation provided through the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ.

A renewed, passionate commitment to personal holiness, progressive sanctification, and radical obedience.

An urgent, loving burden to faithfully and boldly communicate the message of reconciliation to a lost world.

May this book serve not only as a rigorous academic resource but also as a powerful tool for your personal spiritual growth and future ministry. As you engage with these glorious truths, may the Holy Spirit guide you into all truth, open your eyes to the riches of His inheritance, and transform your life for the glory of God

Soli Deo Gloria
For the Glory of God Alone

Chapter 1 — Introduction to Soteriology

The grand narrative of Scripture moves from creation to consummation, but its dramatic core is the rescue of humanity. This rescue is the subject of Soteriology—the theological study of salvation. Derived from the Greek words soterios (salvation, savior) and logos (word, study, or discourse), soteriology is not merely an intellectual exercise; it is the heartbeat of Christian theology and the ultimate demonstration of God’s character, justice, and love.

For the Evangelical, soteriology is deeply personal yet grandly cosmic. It addresses the fundamental crisis of human existence—our alienation from a holy God—and outlines the definitive solution provided through the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. As we embark on this study, we must approach it with both academic rigor and spiritual humility, recognizing that we are examining the very depths of God's grace.

The Meaning and Scope of Salvation

To understand soteriology, one must first understand the breadth of the word "salvation." In everyday language, to be saved implies being delivered from a state of imminent danger, ruin, or destruction. Scripturally, this definition holds true but carries a multi-dimensional weight.

Evangelical systematic theology recognizes that salvation is not a single, isolated event, but a comprehensive work of God that spans eternity. It encompasses the past, the present, and the future:

The Past Dimension (Justification): The believer has been saved from the penalty of sin. Through faith in Christ, the legal verdict of condemnation is overturned, and the righteousness of Christ is imputed to the sinner.

The Present Dimension (Sanctification): The believer is being saved from the power of sin. Through the ongoing work of the Holy Spirit, the Christian is progressively conformed to the image of Christ, breaking the dominion of habitual sin in daily life.

The Future Dimension (Glorification): The believer will be saved from the presence of sin. This is the ultimate culmination of salvation, where the believer receives a resurrected body, completely free from the moral and physical decay of the fall.

Thus, soteriology is the study of how God initiates, executes, and finalizes this three-fold deliverance for His chosen people.

The Crucial Need for Salvation

A house is only built where shelter is required; a medicine is only valued where disease is present. In the same way, the doctrine of salvation is entirely unintelligible without a proper understanding of the human condition. The immediate necessity of soteriology is driven by three grim realities of the human state:

1. Man's Lostness

The foundational premise of salvation is that humanity is fundamentally lost. Following the historical fall of Adam in Genesis 3, sin entered the world, bringing spiritual and physical death. In Evangelical theology, this is understood through the lens of total depravity. This does not mean that every person is as wicked as they could possibly be, but rather that sin has corrupted every faculty of human nature—the mind, the will, the emotions, and the body. Humanity is spiritually blind, dead in trespasses and sins (Ephesians 2:1), and naturally hostile toward God. We are not merely sick individuals needing a physician; we are spiritually dead captives needing resurrection.

2. Man's Inability to Save Himself

Because of total depravity, humanity suffers from absolute spiritual inability. A person drowning in the middle of the ocean cannot pull themselves up by their own hair; a spiritually dead corpse cannot breathe life into its own lungs. Scripture explicitly denies that human willpower, intellect, or ancestry can bring about reconciliation with God. As the Apostle Paul writes, "No one is righteous, no no one; no one understands; no one seeks for God" (Romans 3:10-11). The moral debt owed to a holy God is infinite, and finite, corrupted human beings possess no spiritual currency to pay it.

3. The Futility of Human Attempts to Save Himself

Despite this inherent inability, human history is a chronicle of desperate, failed attempts at self-salvation. These attempts typically manifest in three ways:

Moralism and Legalism: The belief that keeping a set of rules, ethics, or religious laws can earn God's favor.

Ritualism: The reliance on religious ceremonies, sacraments, or traditions to wash away guilt.

Intellectualism and Secular Humanism: The modern attempt to save humanity from its problems through education, political systems, or technological progress.

Systematic theology exposes the utter futility of these endeavors. Isaiah 64:6 reminds us that even our most righteous deeds are like "polluted rags" in the sight of a perfectly holy God. Human effort cannot bridge the infinite chasm created by sin. Salvation must come from the outside—it must be entirely a work of divine grace.

The Ultimate Purpose of Soteriology: The Glory of God

Why did God undertake the costly enterprise of human salvation? While our rescue is an act of profound love toward us, the ultimate, supreme purpose of soteriology is the manifestation of the glory of God.

In salvation, God harmonizes and displays His seemingly conflicting attributes. His flawless justice demands that sin be punished, while His infinite love and mercy desire to spare the sinner. At the cross of Jesus Christ, these attributes meet perfectly: justice is completely satisfied because the penalty of sin is paid in full, and mercy is fully extended because Christ pays it on behalf of the believer.

Every step of the soteriological framework—from election before the foundation of the world to the final glorification of the saints in the new heavens and the new earth—is designed so that humanity can boast in nothing except the Lord. As Paul triumphantly concludes in Ephesians 1, the entire plan of redemption is executed "to the praise of his glorious grace."

Chapter 2 — The Need for Salvation

The necessity of a cure can only be fully appreciated when one understands the lethality of the disease. In Evangelical systematic theology, the doctrine of salvation (soteriology) does not stand in isolation; it is functionally dependent upon a realistic, biblically grounded assessment of the human condition (hamartiology). To understand why man must be saved, we must examine the depth of human ruin. This chapter explores the threefold reality of the human predicament: the tragic state of man’s lostness, the absolute inability of man to alter his own spiritual condition, and the utter futility of human attempts at self-salvation.

1. Man's Lostness and the Problem of Righteousness

To look at humanity apart from the sovereign grace of Jesus Christ is to witness a state of profound, universal tragedy. Scripture does not paint human beings as basically good individuals who occasionally err; rather, it describes a comprehensive state of spiritual ruin.

The Universal Verdict: Lost, Condemned, and Alienated

Apart from Christ, the biblical verdict on humanity is absolute, unyielding, and sweeping in its scope. This natural condition is defined by three sobering realities:

All men are lost and helpless: Humanity has wandered off the path of life and is fundamentally disoriented, lacking the compass required to find its way back to its Creator.

All men are condemned to die in their sins: The structural consequence of transgression is death—both immediate spiritual death and ultimate eternal separation from the source of life (Romans 6:23).

All men are separated and alienated from God: Because of sin, a profound relational and moral chasm exists between humanity and God. We are not born as friends of God who merely need a bit of guidance; we are born as rebels alienated from His fellowship (Colossians 1:21).

The Core Crisis: The Problem of Righteousness

At the heart of man’s lostness lies an insurmountable moral and legal crisis: the problem of righteousness.

Theological Definition: Righteousness, in its forensic and systematic sense, denotes "rightness"—perfect conformity to God's holy character and law, and a valid, unstained legal standing before His tribunal.

Because God is perfectly holy (Isaiah 6:3), His unchanging justice dictates that only those who possess flawless righteousness can exist in His presence and enjoy His fellowship. This creates the primary dilemma of human existence: How can unrighteous men gain the righteousness necessary to properly relate to a holy, righteous God?

The doctrine of salvation is God’s definitive answer to this crisis. True biblical soteriology demonstrates how God provides a way to deal with man's lost, sinful state in a manner that does not violate His own holiness and justice. God cannot simply ignore or dismiss sin, for to do so would compromise His perfect justice. The penalty must be paid, and righteousness must be maintained.

2. Man's Inability to Save Himself

If humanity is lost, the immediate human impulse is to look for a way out. However, Evangelical systematic theology asserts the doctrine of total inability (often linked with total depravity). This means that the effects of the Fall are so pervasive that human beings are completely incapable of doing anything to achieve, initiate, or contribute to their own salvation.

The Bondage of the Will and Spiritual Death

Man's inability is not a mere lack of effort or a weakness of resolve; it is an absolute lack of spiritual capacity. Scripture describes unregenerate humanity not as morally sick or injured, but as being "dead in trespasses and sins" (Ephesians 2:1). A corpse cannot respond to external stimuli, nor can it choose to make itself alive.

Because the human mind, heart, and will are fundamentally corrupted by sin, the natural man possesses no inherent desire for true, biblical righteousness. The Apostle Paul explicitly details this spiritual paralysis and hostility in his letter to the Romans:

The mind set on the flesh is hostile to God, for it does not submit to God's law; indeed, it cannot. – Romans 8:7

Left to ourselves, we lack both the moral will and the spiritual power to turn to God. As Jesus plainly stated in John 6:44, "No one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws him." Humanity is entirely dependent on a prior, sovereign work of the Holy Spirit to regenerate our dead hearts before we can ever exercise saving faith.

3. Human Attempts at Self-Salvation

Despite this inherent inability, human history is a chronicle of desperate, ongoing attempts at self-salvation. Realizing that something is fundamentally broken, humanity continually manufactures systems designed to earn right standing with the Divine or achieve personal peace. These attempts generally fall into four distinct categories, all of which are exposed by Scripture as utterly futile.

Attempt #1: By Being Good (The Illusion of Relative Morality)

The first and most common human attempt is the pursuit of personal goodness or moral reformation. It operates on the assumption that an individual can satisfy God by simply trying their best, living an upright life, and remaining "better than average."

Systematic theology dismantles this illusion by highlighting the absolute standard of God. God does not grade on a curve; His standard is absolute perfection. The Apostle Paul sums up the universal failure of this attempt by quoting the Psalmist:

Romans 3:10–12: "None is righteous, no, not one; no one understands; no one seeks for God. All have turned aside; together they have become worthless; no one does good, not even one."

Attempt #2: By Doing Good (Legalism and Meritorious Works)

When general morality proves insufficient, humanity shifts toward legalism—the systematic performance of good works, charity, and keeping religious or ethical laws to accumulate merit. This mindset assumes that a person's good deeds can outweigh or cancel out their bad deeds.

Scripture repeatedly and explicitly denies that human deeds can alter one's legal standing before God's judgment seat. The law was given to expose sin, not to erase it:

Romans 3:20: "For by works of the law no human being will be justified in his sight, since through the law comes knowledge of sin."

Galatians 2:16: "...knowing that a man is not justified by the works of the law but by faith in Jesus Christ... for by the works of the law no flesh shall be justified."

Attempt #3: Because of Who They Are (Heritage, Pedigree, and Ritual)

The third attempt relies on external identity—heritage, religious ancestry, ethnicity, or participation in sacred rituals (such as baptism, circumcision, or church membership). This error assumes that God grants salvation based on family lines, covenant lineage, or external association.

John the Baptist fiercely rebuked this external confidence, warning his hearers not to say to themselves, "We have Abraham as our father" (Matthew 3:9). True salvation is not a biological or ecclesiastical inheritance; it requires supernatural regeneration:

John 1:12–13: "But to all who did receive him, who believed in his name, he gave the right to become children of God, who were born, not of blood nor of the will of the flesh nor of the will of man, but of God."

Attempt #4: Because of What They Know (Intellectualism and Human Wisdom)

The fourth attempt turns inward to human wisdom, intellect, philosophy, or superior knowledge (gnosis). In both ancient gnosticism and modern secular humanism, the underlying myth is that humanity’s primary problem is ignorance, and that salvation can be achieved through mental enlightenment, education, or scientific advancement.

While human intellect is a valuable gift for navigating the created order, it has severe structural limitations when searching for the transcendent God. Left to its own devices, human wisdom rejects the gospel as foolishness:

1 Corinthians 1:21: "For since, in the wisdom of God, the world did not know God through wisdom, it pleased God through the folly of what we preach to save those who believe."

1 Corinthians 2:14: "The natural person does not accept the things of the Spirit of God, for they are folly to him, and he is not able to understand them because they are spiritually discerned."

Matthew 16:16–17: When Peter confesses Christ, Jesus immediately notes that this knowledge did not come through human intellect: "Flesh and blood has not revealed this to you, but my Father who is in heaven."

The Divine Verdict on Self-Righteousness

Every human attempt to bridge the infinite chasm between a sinful creature and a holy Creator ends in catastrophic failure. The prophet Isaiah provides the definitive, crushing systematic verdict on all human efforts at self-justification:

"We have all become like one who is unclean, and all our righteous deeds are like a polluted garment." — Isaiah 64:6

Even our very best efforts—our highest moral achievements, our deepest religious rituals, our heritage, and our grandest intellectual pursuits—are thoroughly stained by sin when held up to the blinding light of God’s absolute holiness. If salvation is to happen at all, it cannot originate from man; it must come entirely to man as a supernatural gift of sovereign grace.

Chapter 3 — God's Provision

Having established the gravity of the human predicament—our total lostness, spiritual inability, and the futility of our self-driven efforts—we now turn to the glorious resolution. If salvation were dependent upon human effort, the story of humanity would end in despair. However, Evangelical systematic theology is essentially a "theology of hope" because it recognizes that where man is unable, God is able. This chapter explores the divine response to the Fall, focusing on the sovereign initiative, the gracious foundation, the substitutionary basis, and the instrumental means of God’s provision for sinners.

1. The Initiative of God: The Seeking Savior

The first movement of salvation does not begin with a man seeking God, but with God seeking man. This is a foundational principle of biblical soteriology: because sin has blinded the human mind and hardened the human heart, no person possesses the natural inclination to seek the true God (Romans 3:11). Therefore, if a relationship is to be restored, God must be the initiator.

The Seeking God in the Garden

This divine initiative is woven into the very first pages of Scripture. Immediately following the Fall, Adam and Eve hid themselves in fear. It was not the man who called out for his Creator; it was God who entered the garden and initiated the search:

Genesis 3:9: "But the Lord God called to the man and said to him, 'Where are you?'"

This question was not for God's information, but for man's conviction. It established the paradigm of redemption: God is the one who comes to the hiding sinner.

God’s Initiative through the Patriarchs and Prophets

Throughout the Old Testament, we see God continually stepping into human history to initiate covenants that man did not deserve and could not have conceived.

The Patriarchal Call: God initiated the redemption of the nations by sovereignly calling Abram out of pagan Ur (Genesis 12).

The Prophetic Voice: Even when Israel was consistently rebellious, God sent His prophets to call the people back to Himself. As seen in Ezekiel 34:11, God declares, "For thus says the Lord God: Behold, I, I myself will search for my sheep and will seek them out."

Soteriology is, therefore, monergistic in its origin—it is a "single-work" of God. From the first promise in Eden (Protoevangelium) to the coming of Christ, salvation is the result of a God who pursues His people.

2. The Foundation of Salvation: Love and Grace

If the initiative of salvation is found in God's seeking, the foundation—the internal motivation of God—is found in His character, specifically His love and His grace.

The Motivation of Love

Why would a holy and just God seek out a rebellious creature? The Bible provides no other reason than His own nature.

John 3:16: "For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son..."

God’s love is not a response to our loveliness; it is an overflow of His own being. This love is described as "steadfast" (Hesed)—a covenantal loyalty that persists even when the recipient is unfaithful.

The Principle of Grace

The systematic foundation of our rescue is Grace (Charis). Grace is often defined as "unmerited favor," but in a soteriological context, it is more: it is favor shown to those who actually deserve the opposite.

Ephesians 2:4–5: "But God, being rich in mercy, because of the great love with which he loved us, even when we were dead in our trespasses, made us alive together with Christ—by grace you have been saved."

Salvation is founded upon God's sovereign decision to set His affection upon us, a decision made "before the foundation of the world" (Ephesians 1:4). It is not earned by merit; it is grounded in the benevolent will of the Father.

3. The Basis of Salvation: The Work of Jesus Christ

While the foundation of salvation is God’s love, the basis—the legal and moral ground upon which God can save—is the finished work of Jesus Christ. As we noted in Chapter 2, God’s holiness and justice cannot be compromised. He cannot simply "forgive" without the penalty of sin being satisfied.

The Problem Solved: Satisfaction and Substitution

The basis of our salvation is the Substitutionary Atonement. Jesus Christ, the God-man, acted as our representative. He lived the life of perfect righteousness that we failed to live, and He died the death that we deserved to die.

2 Corinthians 5:21: "For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God."

The Forensic Basis

Our standing before God is not based on an internal change in our nature (which is the work of sanctification), but on a legal declaration based on Christ’s work.

Romans 3:24–25: We are "justified by his grace as a gift, through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus, whom God put forward as a propitiation by his blood, to be received by faith."

"Propitiation" means that Christ’s sacrifice turned away the righteous wrath of God by satisfying the demands of justice. Thus, Christ is the only valid basis for salvation.

4. The Means of Salvation: Faith and Repentance

If Christ’s work is the basis (the provision), how is that provision applied to the individual sinner? Systematic theology distinguishes between the accomplishment of redemption (by Christ) and the appropriation of redemption (by the believer). The means of appropriation are faith and repentance.

The Instrument of Faith (Pistis)

Faith is not the cause of salvation, but the instrument or "hand" that receives it. We are saved by grace through faith. Saving faith is more than mere intellectual assent (notitia); it involves:

Knowledge (Notitia): Understanding the facts of the Gospel.

Assent (Assensus): Agreeing that these facts are true.

Trust (Fiducia): A personal reliance on Christ alone for salvation.

Romans 5:1: "Therefore, since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ."

The Necessity of Repentance (Metanoia)

Faith and repentance are two sides of the same coin (conversion). Repentance is a "change of mind" that leads to a change of direction. It is a turning from sin and a turning to God.

Acts 3:19: "Repent therefore, and turn back, that your sins may be blotted out."

Salvation is a Gift, Not a Work

To ensure that man cannot boast in the means of salvation, Scripture clarifies that even our faith is a gift resulting from God’s work in us.

Ephesians 2:8–9: "For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast."

In summary, God’s provision is complete and perfect. He initiates the rescue because of His love and grace, provides the legal basis through the death of His Son, and grants the means of faith so that the sinner may be reconciled to Him forever.

Summary Table: The Framework of God's Provision

Category Theological Component Key Scriptural Proof
The Initiative God seeks the sinner first Genesis 3:9; Ezekiel 34:11
The Foundation God's Love and Grace John 3:16; Ephesians 2:4-8
The Basis Christ's Substitutionary Work 2 Corinthians 5:21; Romans 3:24
The Means Faith and Repentance Romans 5:1; Acts 3:19; Eph 2:8

Chapter 4 — The Work of the Holy Spirit in Salvation

The execution of God’s redemptive plan is a profoundly Trinitarian enterprise. While the Father initiates the decree of salvation and the Son achieves redemption objectively on the cross, it is the Holy Spirit who subjectively applies this finished work to the heart of the individual sinner. Without the specific, supernatural operations of the Holy Spirit, Christ’s sacrifice would remain an unappropriated, historical reality. This chapter examines the critical, distinct phases of the Holy Spirit’s work in executing salvation in the life of the believer: Conviction, Regeneration, and Indwelling.

1. Conviction: The Preliminary Work of the Spirit

Before a soul can look to Christ for healing, it must first realize it is mortally wounded. Because humanity naturally dwells in spiritual blindness and self-righteousness, the sinner is incapable of accurately assessing their true guilt before God. Therefore, the Holy Spirit's first subjective movement in the economy of salvation is conviction.

This work is foretold by Jesus in His Upper Room Discourse, outlining a precise, threefold diagnostic criterion that the Spirit applies to the world:

"And when he comes, he will convict the world concerning sin and righteousness and judgment..." — John 16:8

The Holy Spirit’s Conviction:

  • Of Sin: Unbelief Exposed John 16:9
  • Of Righteousness: Christ’s Standard John 16:10
  • Of Judgment Satan Condemned John 16:11

The Three Dimensions of Conviction

Concerning Sin (John 16:9): The Spirit does not merely point out individual moral lapses; He exposes the root sin: "because they do not believe in me." The Spirit reveals that ultimate condemnation rests on the rejection of the Son of God, exposing the heart’s rebellion and its total lack of saving faith.

Concerning Righteousness (John 16:10): Jesus states this occurs "because I go to the Father, and you will see me no longer." By ascending to the Father, Christ was vindicated as perfectly righteous. The Spirit holds up Christ's flawless standard against human "polluted rags" (Isaiah 64:6), shattering the illusion of human goodness and demonstrating that relative morality cannot satisfy God.

Concerning Judgment (John 16:11): This is executed "because the ruler of this world is judged." Through the cross, Satan's dominion was legally broken. The Holy Spirit convicts the sinner that if the prince of this world stands condemned under divine justice, an unrepentant humanity remaining in his kingdom faces the exact same terrifying, inevitable judgment.

2. Regeneration: The Quickening Work of the Spirit

Once the sinner is brought to an awareness of their ruin through conviction, the Holy Spirit performs the miraculous act of regeneration—the instantaneous imparting of spiritual life to a dead soul. This is the theological reality colloquially known as being "born again."

The New Birth (Palingenesia)

Regeneration is a divine, supernatural act. In Evangelical systematic theology, it is recognized as completely monergistic; it is a work of God alone, wherein the human will is the recipient, not the contributor. Just as an infant does not cooperate in its own physical birth, a spiritually dead person cannot cooperate in their spiritual birth.

John 3:5–6: Jesus emphasizes the absolute necessity of this work to Nicodemus: "Truly, truly, I say to you, unless one is born of water and the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God. That which is born of the flesh is flesh, and that which is born of the Spirit is spirit."

The Sovereign Nature of the Spirit (John 3:8): Jesus compares the Spirit’s regenerating work to the wind: "The wind blows where it wishes... So it is with everyone who is born of the Spirit." God moves sovereignly to quicken dead hearts according to His perfect will.

The Theological Effects of Regeneration

When the Holy Spirit regenerates an individual, a radical internal metamorphosis occurs:

Passage from Death to Life: The spiritual corpse is made alive (Ephesians 2:5).

The Granting of a New Nature: In fulfillment of the New Covenant promise in Ezekiel 36:26, the Spirit removes the unyielding "heart of stone" and replaces it with a responsive "heart of flesh."

The Enabling of Conversion: It is this instant renewal of the faculties that changes the human disposition, allowing the newly quickened mind and will to freely and gladly exercise saving faith and repentance.

3. Indwelling: The Permanent Presence of the Spirit

The Holy Spirit does not merely visit the believer to quicken them and then depart; rather, He takes up permanent residence within the redeemed soul. The indwelling of the Holy Spirit is the hallmark of New Covenant salvation, serving as the basis for the Christian's security, sanctification, and ultimate assurance.

The Body as a Temple

In the Old Testament economy, the presence of God dwelt in a localized, physical sanctuary—first the Tabernacle, then the Temple. In the New Covenant era, the locus of God's presence has shifted dramatically. The individual believer’s body is now the sacred sanctuary of the living God.

1 Corinthians 6:19: "Or do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you, whom you have from God?"

Because the Holy Spirit permanently resides within the believer, salvation carries an ongoing relational intimacy. He is the Paraclete—the Comforter, Counselor, and Advocate—who guides, instructs, and empowers the Christian to live a life pleasing to God.

The Guarantee and Seal of Salvation

The permanent indwelling of the Spirit serves a vital legal and pastoral function in soteriology, operating as both a seal and a pledge:

The Divine Seal: In the ancient world, a seal denoted ownership, authenticity, and security. The Holy Spirit is the seal stamped upon the believer, marking them out as God's secure possession.

The Guarantee/Down Payment (Arrabon): Scripture describes the indwelling Spirit as the "guarantee" or "earnest money" of our inheritance.

Ephesians 1:13–14: "...in whom also, when you believed, you were sealed with the promised Holy Spirit, who is the guarantee of our inheritance until we acquire possession of it, to the praise of his glorious grace."

The presence of the Spirit in the believer’s life today is God’s non-refundable deposit, guaranteeing that the work begun in regeneration will inevitably culminate in final glorification.

Summary Table: The Holy Spirit's Work Matrix

Phase Core Functional Meaning Primary Scriptural Proof Soteriological Result
1. Conviction Exposing human guilt, lack of righteousness, and impending judgment. John 16:8–11 Strips away self-righteousness; reveals the absolute need for Christ.
2. Regeneration Sovereign, monergistic imparting of eternal life to a spiritually dead soul. John 3:5–8; Ezekiel 36:26 Transforms the human nature; enables faith and repentance.
3. Indwelling Taking up permanent, personal residence within the body of the believer. 1 Corinthians 6:19; Ephesians 1:13–14 Seals ownership; guarantees final glorification and provides power for holiness.

Chapter 5 — The Doctrine of Atonement

If soteriology is the crown of systematic theology, the doctrine of the Atonement is the brightest jewel in that crown. Having recognized the desperate depth of human lostness and the subjective, quickening work of the Holy Spirit, we must now examine the objective, historical event that made reconciliation possible: the sacrificial death of Jesus Christ on the cross.

The word "atonement" (literally at-one-ment) refers to the work Christ did in His life and death to earn our salvation and reconcile holy God with sinful humanity. In this chapter, we will examine the nature, Old Testament foundations, semantic imagery, and cosmic extent of this pivotal event.

1. The Nature of the Atonement

To understand the cross, we must look beyond the physical Roman execution and peer into the profound theological reality taking place. Evangelical systematic theology views the cross not as a tragic accident of history, or a mere example of political martyrdom, but as a deliberate, divinely orchestrated cosmic transaction.

The Problem of the Cross

The central problem that the atonement addresses is the apparent tension within the character of God Himself. As established in Chapter 2, God is perfectly holy and infinitely just; therefore, He cannot simply ignore, dismiss, or bypass sin. To do so would make Him an unjust judge. Yet, He is also infinitely loving and merciful, desiring to save sinners.

The cross is the ultimate, breathtaking solution where these attributes meet:

God’s Justice is fully satisfied because the penalty of sin (death) is paid in full.

God’s Love is fully expressed because He pays that penalty Himself in the person of His Son.

Substitutionary Atonement

The foundational framework for understanding the nature of the cross is Vicarious or Substitutionary Atonement (often termed Penal Substitutionary Atonement).

Penal: Christ bore the legal penalty mandated by God's broken law.

Substitutionary: Christ stood in our place, acting as our representative.

Christ's Righteousness Imputed to Us - Our Sin Imputed to Christ

Through this divine exchange, Jesus takes our guilt and gives us His perfect standing.

2. The Old Testament Picture of Atonement

The New Testament does not invent the concept of sacrifice; it fulfills it. To accurately interpret the death of Christ, an academic student must understand the deep roots of the Old Testament sacrificial system, which served as a grand shadow and schoolmaster leading to Christ.

The Principle of Life for Life

From the moment sin entered the world, God established that the penalty for treason against Him was death. However, in His mercy, He allowed for a system of substitution. The underlying principle of the entire Levitical system is found in Leviticus 17:11:

"For the life of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it for you on the altar to make atonement for your souls, for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life."

Blood represents life. The pouring out of blood signifies that a life has been given to pay a debt.

The Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur)

The apex of the Old Testament picture is found in the rituals of Yom Kippur (Leviticus 16), which perfectly illustrated the double aspect of how God handles sin through two specific goats:

The Sacrificial Goat (Propitiation): This goat was slaughtered, and its blood was brought into the Holy of Holies and sprinkled on the Mercy Seat. This signified that God’s righteous wrath against Israel's sin was appeased by a life given.

The Scapegoat (Expiation): The High Priest would lay his hands on the head of this second goat, confessing all the sins of Israel over it. The goat was then led out into the wilderness, never to return. This visually demonstrated the literal removal of sin from the presence of God.

Despite its beauty, the writer of the Hebrews reminds us that this system was structurally incomplete: "For it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins" (Hebrews 10:4). They could only cover (kaphar) sin temporarily; they could never permanently eradicate it. They pointed forward to the ultimate Lamb of God.

3. Biblical Imagery and Terms of the Atonement

To convey the multi-faceted glory of what Christ accomplished on the cross, the New Testament authors use a rich tapestry of distinct, descriptive theological terms drawn from various areas of ancient life.

Dimensions of Atonement

  • Propitiation - Temple Imagery - God's Wrath Appeased
  • Redemption - Market Imagery - Sinner Bought Back
  • Reconciliation - Court/Family Imagery - Enmity Turned to Peace

Propitiation: The Appeasement of Divine Wrath

To fully grasp the mechanics of the atonement, the student must navigate the rich sanctuary imagery of the New Testament, which centers on the crucial concept of propitiation—defined theologically as the turning away, appeasement, or satisfaction of righteous divine wrath through a fitting sacrificial offering. Within a contemporary culture that often minimizes the gravity of sin, this concept can be easily misunderstood or viewed with discomfort. However, a robust Evangelical soteriology recognizes that sin is not a minor moral lapse or a mere relational faux pas; it is an act of cosmic treason that provokes the holy, unyielding, and righteous displeasure of an absolute God. Because God is perfectly holy, His justice cannot simply overlook or dismiss rebellion. His holy wrath is not a petulant, human emotional outburst, but a necessary, judicial response to moral evil, demanding that the legal penalty for sin be fully satisfied before reconciliation can occur.

It is against this intense backdrop of divine judgment that the beauty of Christ's work becomes clear. The target of propitiation is not man, but God Himself. Christ’s sacrificial death was directed toward God the Father to satisfy His judicial wrath against the sins of humanity. This is dynamically illustrated by the Apostle Paul in Romans 3:25, where he states that God put Christ forward as a propitiation by His blood, to be received by faith. On the cross, Jesus acted as our ultimate substitute, absorbing the full weight of the divine anger that we deserved. By pouring out His life's blood, He completely appeased the demands of the broken law and satisfied divine justice. Consequently, for those who are in Christ Jesus, the terrifying cloud of divine wrath is entirely removed, giving way to an unshakeable state of peace and favor with the Father

Expiation: The Removal and Cleansing of Guilt

Expiation is defined theologically as the wiping away, cleansing, or total removal of sin and its attendant guilt from the sinner. While both terms propitiation and expiation are rooted deeply in the sacrificial language of the Old Testament sanctuary, they possess distinct directional focuses that together accomplish our reconciliation. Whereas propitiation is God-ward, satisfying His judicial wrath against sin, expiation is sinner-ward, dealing directly with the spiritual defilement, weight, and legal liability of the transgressor.

In Evangelical systematic theology, sin leaves a devastating dual legacy: it provokes divine judgment, and it thoroughly stains the human conscience, leaving the sinner unholy and unfit for the presence of a pristine God. Expiation represents the merciful antidote to this spiritual pollution. Through this work, sin is not merely covered up or overlooked; it is entirely cancelled and driven out of the camp. The beautiful reality for the student to grasp is that on the cross, Jesus did not only absorb the penalty of our treason, but He also acted as our ultimate scapegoat, carrying our moral filth far away from us.

This total eradication of guilt is captured with profound pastoral beauty by the Psalmist in Psalm 103:12, which states that as far as the east is from the west, so far does He remove our transgressions from us. Because the distance between east and west is infinite, this image assures the believer that their sins have been cast into an absolute oblivion. Through the expiating blood of Christ, the sinner's defilement is thoroughly washed away, their conscience is cleansed, and they are presented before a holy Creator completely spotless, untainted, and legally free from the burden of past guilt

Redemption: The Ransom from Bondage

Redemption is defined theologically as buying back, liberating, or rescuing an individual from a state of captive bondage through the payment of a specific ransom price. While propitiation and expiation address the legal and spiritual defilement of sin before God, redemption directly addresses the helpless state of the sinner. In Evangelical systematic theology, the natural human condition apart from grace is viewed not as one of relative freedom, but as absolute spiritual slavery. Humanity is structurally enslaved to three cruel masters: the power of sin, the finality of spiritual death, and the crushing condemnation of the broken law.

Because of total inability, a slave cannot accumulate the currency required to purchase their own freedom. If liberation is to happen, an outside benefactor must step into the marketplace to pay the price. The glorious truth of the gospel is that Jesus Christ entered the slave market of human history to pay the ultimate purchase price (lutron) to set us free. He did not achieve this rescue through a display of mere raw power, but through a costly legal transaction, substituting His own life as the payment to satisfy the terms of our release.

This marketplace transaction is explicitly detailed by our Lord in Mark 10:45, where He declares that the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give His life as a ransom for many. The exact nature of this ransom price is further clarified by the Apostle Paul in Galatians 3:13, which states that Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us. By absorbing the judicial curse of the law in His own person on the tree, Jesus completely paid our outstanding moral debt. Consequently, the chains of spiritual bondage are broken, the slave prison is opened, and the believer is brought into the permanent, unshakeable liberty of the children of God

Reconciliation: The Restoration of Divine Fellowship

Reconciliation is defined theologically as the total restoration of a broken relationship and the transformation of former enemies into beloved friends. While propitiation removes divine wrath, expiation cleanses guilt, and redemption breaks our bondage, reconciliation is the glorious end to which they all point. In Evangelical systematic theology, sin is understood not merely as a legal transgression, but as a devastating relational catastrophe. It fractured the harmony between Creator and creature, establishing an objective state of alienation, hostility, and spiritual distance. Apart from grace, human beings are not born in a state of neutral innocence; they are spiritually positioned as hostile outlaws walking in active rebellion against a holy King.

Because the barrier of sin is infinite, humanity possesses no relational leverage to bridge this chasm. If peace is to be made, the offended party must make the first move. The heart of the gospel is that God did not wait for His enemies to sue for peace; rather, He sovereignly orchestrated the terms of our return. On the cross, Jesus Christ completely removed the legal barrier of our trespasses, satisfying the judicial demands that fueled the separation.

This relational overhaul is beautifully articulated by the Apostle Paul in 2 Corinthians 5:19, which declares that in Christ, God was reconciling the world to Himself, not counting their trespasses against them, and entrusting to us the ministry of reconciliation. By refusing to count our sins against us—and instead counting them to Christ—the structural enmity is decisively slain. Reconciliation shifts our cosmic reality from the battlefield to the family table. The war is over, the veil is torn, and the former outlaw is brought home, possessing not just a formal pardon, but permanent access, deep fellowship, and objective peace with God forever

4. The Extent of the Atonement

Few topics in systematic theology have sparked as much intense debate as the question of the extent of the atonement. The core question is not about the value of Christ's death (all orthodox theologians agree it is of infinite worth), but rather its design and intention: For whom did Christ die?

Evangelical systematic theology generally divides into two primary historical viewpoints on this issue.

Viewpoint A: Particular Redemption (Limited/Definite Atonement)

Maintained historically by Reformed/Calvinistic theologians, this view argues that the atonement was definite in its design.

The Core Argument: Christ died with the specific intention of securing the salvation of those whom the Father had chosen (the elect). His death did not merely make salvation possible; it actually secured and accomplished salvation for His people.

Scriptural Support: * John 10:15: "I lay down my life for the sheep."

Ephesians 5:25: "Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her."

Matthew 1:21: "He will save his people from their sins."

Theological Defense: If Christ died for every single individual universally, and some people still go to hell, then their sins are being punished twice (once on Christ and once on them), which would violate divine justice.

Viewpoint B: Universal Redemption (General/Unlimited Atonement)

Maintained historically by Arminian, Provisionalist, and many general Evangelical theologians, this view argues that the atonement was universal in its scope.

The Core Argument: Christ died for every single individual who has ever lived, without exception. His death paid the penalty for all human sin, making salvation universally available to everyone, though it is only applied and made effective when an individual exercises faith.

Scriptural Support:

1 John 2:2: "He is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world."

1 Timothy 2:6: "...who gave himself as a ransom for all..."

John 1:29: "Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!"

Theological Defense: God genuinely desires all people to be saved (1 Timothy 2:4) and sincerely offers the gospel invitation to all. Therefore, the provision must be genuinely co-extensive with the human race.

Conclusion on the Extent

Whether one leans toward a definite or general view of the design of the cross, all Evangelicals unite around the functional reality: the gospel must be preached to every creature, Christ's sacrifice is completely sufficient for any sinner who repents, and salvation is found in no other name under heaven.

Summary: The Atonement Terms

Theological Term Original Domain Core Dynamic Target of the Action
Substitution Legal/Representation Standing in the place of the guilty The Sinner's Place
Propitiation Temple Sanctuary Appeasing righteous divine wrath God's Holy Wrath
Expiation Temple Sanctuary Washing away and removing moral guilt The Sinner's Guilt
Redemption Slave Marketplace Paying a ransom price to buy back The Bondage of Sin
Reconciliation Relational/Domestic Bringing enemies into peace and fellowship The Broken Relationship

Chapter 6 — The Electing Purpose of God in Salvation

Few doctrines in systematic theology evoke as much pastoral passion, intellectual rigor, and intense debate as the doctrine of divine election. As students of Scripture, we must approach this topic not with a spirit of academic combativeness, but with deep humility, reverence, and a desire to submit our minds to the total counsel of God's Word.

The purpose of this chapter is to navigate the biblical data regarding election. We will explore its definition, unpack the key truths that define it, analyze the two primary historical frameworks (the Calvinist and Arminian perspectives), and directly confront the natural theological tensions that arise. Our goal is to understand these views clearly so that we can preach, teach, and minister with theological balance and scriptural integrity.

1. The Paradox: Two Parallel Biblical Truths

When navigating the electing purpose of God, the student of systematic theology is immediately confronted with what appears to the human mind to be a paradox. Scripture simultaneously and unapologetically teaches two distinct truths. Like two parallel railroad tracks, they run side-by-side throughout the biblical narrative, seemingly meeting only at the distant horizon of God's infinite wisdom.

The Sovereign Election of God: God sovereignly chooses and predestines individuals for salvation before time began (Eph 1:4; Rom 9)

The Genuine Responsibility of Man: Human beings are genuinely accountable to respond to the gospel invitation unbelief is fully blamed on man (John 3:18).

Truth A: God's Absolute Sovereignty in Election

The Bible repeatedly asserts that God is the supreme King of the universe who works all things according to the counsel of His own will (Ephesians 1:11). In the realm of soteriology, Scripture explicitly states that God chooses individuals for salvation before the foundation of the world, and that this choice is rooted entirely in His sovereign grace, not in human merit or initiation.

Truth B: Man's Genuine Responsibility and Free Will

Simultaneously, Scripture addresses human beings as moral agents who are genuinely responsible for their actions, choices, and ultimate response to the gospel. The invitation to believe in Jesus Christ is extended sincerely to all people, and those who reject Him are held fully accountable and blamed for their own condemnation (John 3:18, John 5:40).

Navigating the Tension

An authentic Evangelical systematic theology must resist the temptation to "solve" this tension by destroying or minimizing either track.

To emphasize sovereignty at the absolute exclusion of human responsibility slides into hyper-Calvinism or fatalism, stripping the gospel of its universal call.

To emphasize human responsibility at the absolute exclusion of divine sovereignty slides into Pelagianism or strict open theism, stripping God of His kingly supremacy.

A faithful theologian learns to hold both truths in a biblically balanced tension, recognizing that what seems like a contradiction to human logic is perfectly harmonized within the transcendent mind of God.

2. Defining Election: God's Eternal Choice

To ground our study, we must establish a precise, workable theological definition of election based on the overarching witness of the New Testament.

Theological Definition: Election is God's pre-temporal, sovereign choice, originating in His infinite grace, by which He determines to save a people out of fallen, rebellious humanity to be conformed to the image of His Son.

Key Aspects of the Definition

It is Pre-Temporal: Election is not a divine "reaction" to events occurring in human history. It was finalized before time, space, or matter came into existence.

It is Sovereign: The ultimate source of election is found within the counsel of God’s own will, not in any external pressure or obligation.

Its Context is a Fallen World: God does not elect innocent creatures; He elects out of a mass of humanity that is already viewed as fallen, guilty, and entirely undeserving of rescue.

3. Key Scriptural Truths About Election

To build a solid biblical foundation, we must analyze the specific descriptive traits that the New Testament attributes to this divine decree.

Truth #1: It is God's Prerogative and Business

Election belongs exclusively to the hidden and revealed counsel of God. It is His divine business, not man's. Only God knows perfectly who the elect are (2 Timothy 2:19). Our duty is not to peer into the hidden archives of heaven to decode the book of life, but to faithfully proclaim the gospel to all people, trusting that God will draw His own.

Truth #2: It is Unconditional

In Evangelical theology, election is widely understood to be unconditional regarding human merit. God does not choose an individual because of any foreseen righteousness, good works, or moral superiority. It is based solely on His mercy. As Paul explicitly argues regarding Jacob and Esau:

Though they were not yet born and had done nothing either good or bad- implies "The older will serve the younger."— Romans 9:11-12

Truth #3: It Eliminates "Comparative Righteousness"

Because election is completely unmerited, it systematically demolishes any human concept of comparative righteousness or pride. No person can claim that God chose them because they were smarter, more spiritually sensitive, or more morally upright than their neighbor. It levels the ground at the foot of the cross, forcing humanity to confess that salvation is entirely of the Lord.

Truth #4: It Highlights Divine Mercy, Not Human Effort

The definitive scriptural summary of this dynamic is found in Paul’s letter to the Romans, where he states:

"So then it depends not on human will or exertion, but on God, who has mercy." > — Romans 9:16

Salvation does not originate in the running or willing of man, but in the sovereign, comforting mercy of God.

4. The Two Major Theological Viewpoints

To help the Christian navigate church history and contemporary theology, it is necessary to examine the two primary interpretive systems that explain how election functions in the economy of salvation.

Viewpoint 1: The Conditional View (Arminian / Wesleyan Perspective)

The conditional view of election argues that God's choice of individuals before the foundation of the world is based upon His divine foreknowledge of how those individuals would respond to the gospel.

The Mechanism: God, existing outside of time, looks down the corridors of history. He foresees who will, when presented with the gospel and aided by enabling grace (prevenient grace), freely choose to repent and believe in Jesus Christ. Based upon this foreseen faith, God elects them to salvation.

Key Scriptural Pillar: 1 Peter 1:1–2, which addresses believers as those who are "elect according to the foreknowledge of God the Father." (Also Romans 8:29).

Theological Focus: This view places a heavy emphasis on preserving the genuine free will of man, the universal love of God for all people, and the sincere offer of the gospel to every individual.

Viewpoint 2: The Unconditional View (Calvinistic / Reformed Perspective)

The unconditional view of election argues that God's choice is independent of any foreseen action or faith on the part of the human being.

The Mechanism: God chooses individuals based strictly on the good pleasure of His own sovereign will and grace. In this view, "foreknowledge" in passages like Romans 8:29 does not mean mere cognitive data about what a person will do; rather, it carries the Hebrew sense of "fore-loving" or entering into a relational commitment with someone beforehand. Therefore, foreseen faith is the result of election, not the cause of it.

Key Scriptural Pillar: Ephesians 1:4–5: "even as he chose us in him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and blameless before him. In love he predestined us for adoption to himself as sons through Jesus Christ, according to the purpose of his will."

Theological Focus: This view focuses intensely on the absolute sovereignty of God, the radical depth of man's spiritual inability (total depravity), and the reality that salvation is a monergistic gift from start to finish.

5. Scriptural Support and Exegesis

An authentic academic textbook must directly confront the primary texts that form the battlefield for this doctrine.

The Ephesians 1 Framework

In Ephesians 1:3–11, Paul uses an undeniable cluster of soteriological terms: chose us (v. 4), predestined us (v. 5), purpose of his will (v. 5), and according to the purpose of him who works all things according to the counsel of his will (v. 11). The structural flow of this passage indicates that election is the foundational anchor for every spiritual blessing the believer possesses.

The Romans 9 Defense

In Romans 9, Paul addresses the ultimate sovereignty of God in election by utilizing historical examples from Israel's lineage. When dealing with the question of whether God's word had failed, Paul points out that God's electing purpose has always been specific:

He chose Isaac over Ishmael.

He chose Jacob over Esau.

When the human mind raises the inevitable objection, "Is there injustice on God's part?" (Romans 9:14), Paul does not soften his stance. Instead, he quotes God's words to Moses: "I will have mercy on whom I have mercy, and I will have compassion on whom I have compassion" (Romans 9:15). He asserts that God, as the potter, has absolute rights over the clay (Romans 9:21).

6. Resolving the Tension: The Pastoral Application

The biblical doctrine of election is never presented in the New Testament as an abstract intellectual puzzle designed to frustrate believers, nor is it a tool to encourage spiritual elitism. When the apostles address this doctrine, they do not leave the reader in a state of speculative paralysis or spiritual confusion. Instead, Scripture consistently introduces the electing purpose of God for three highly practical, deeply pastoral reasons

1. To Produce Deep Humility

When a believer understands that they did not choose God because they were better, wiser, or quicker to repent, but that God chose them out of sheer, unmerited grace while they were still dead in sin, it utterly breaks human pride. It forces the believer to live in a state of constant, humble gratitude.

2. To Provide Absolute Security

In the ancient world, as well as the modern one, believers faced persecution, suffering, and intense doubt. The doctrine of election reminds the Christian that their salvation is not anchored in their own shifting feelings or fragile strength. It is anchored in an eternal, unshakeable decree of God made before the universe was formed. If God purposed it from eternity, He will surely consummate it in glory (Romans 8:30).

3. To Motivate Bold Evangelism

Far from dampening evangelism, a balanced biblical view of election empowers it. It guarantees that our gospel preaching will not be in vain. Because God has a people whom He has chosen, we can proclaim the message of Christ with bold confidence, knowing that while we cannot change a human heart, the Holy Spirit will inevitably open the hearts of His people to respond to the call (Acts 18:9–10).

Synthesis: Comparing the Two Systems

Theological Dimension Conditional Election (Arminian) Unconditional Election (Calvinist)
Ground of Election Foreseen faith and repentance in the sinner. The sovereign good pleasure of God's will.
Role of Human Will Must freely cooperate with enabling grace. Is quickened by grace to willingly respond.
Primary Scriptures Romans 8:29; 1 Peter 1:1–2; John 3:16 Ephesians 1:4–5; Romans 9:11–16; John 6:44
Pastoral Focus Emphasizes universal human responsibility. Emphasizes radical divine grace and security.

Chapter 7 — Results of Salvation

The salvation initiated by the Father, accomplished by the Son, and applied by the Holy Spirit produces immediate, irreversible, and comprehensive results in the life of the believer. In systematic theology, these results are not merely subjective emotional experiences; they represent an objective, cosmic transformation of the believer’s legal standing, inner nature, and relational status before a holy God. This concluding chapter examines the multi-faceted "benefits of salvation" as they unfold from the moment of conversion into eternity.

1. Regeneration: The Impartation of New Life

The initial, supernatural result of salvation is Regeneration. To be regenerated is to be given new spiritual life—to be born from above by the sovereign operation of the Holy Spirit, transforming an individual from a state of spiritual death into a state of vibrant life.

The Theological Nature of the New Birth

Regeneration is a radical, instantaneous work of God. It is fundamentally monergistic; it is an act of God alone in which the dead human soul is the recipient of life, not a contributor to it. Just as an infant plays no part in its own physical conception or birth, a spiritually dead sinner cannot cooperate in their spiritual quickening.
