At the moment of regeneration, the believer is not merely morally reformed, given a second chance, or handed a self-improvement program. Rather, the Holy Spirit radically alters the believer's spiritual ontology. The individual receives an entirely new nature, shifting from being a rebel who is dead in trespasses to a living child of God who is actively alive to His presence, His Word, and His holiness.

Scriptural Proof and Exegesis

The Sovereign Work of God (John 1:12–13): True spiritual rebirth does not originate in human lineage, physical desire, or human willpower. Those who receive Christ and believe in His name are born "not of blood nor of the will of the flesh nor of the will of man, but of God."

The Absolute Necessity of Rebirth (John 3:3–8): In His midnight discourse with Nicodemus, Jesus establishes that external religiosity is utterly useless without internal quickening: "Unless one is born again he cannot see the kingdom of God." This birth is explicitly spiritual, driven by the Holy Spirit who blows sovereignly wherever He wishes.

The Washing of Renewal (Titus 3:4–7): Paul explicitly anchors our salvation outside of human works of righteousness, attributing it entirely to God’s mercy through the "washing of regeneration and renewal of the Holy Spirit," whom He poured out out rich through Jesus Christ.

The Reality of the New Creation (2 Corinthians 5:17): The legal and spiritual identity of the believer is fundamentally redefined: "If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away; behold, the new has come."

Alive to God (Romans 6:8–11): Because the believer has been sacramentally and spiritually united with Christ in His death, they are also united with Him in His resurrection life. The Christian is commanded to constantly "consider yourselves dead to sin and alive to God in Christ Jesus."

The Source of Living Hope (1 John 5:1 & 1 Peter 1:3): Regeneration is the divine catalyst that produces saving faith. It births the believer into an unshakeable, living hope anchored by the historical resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead.

2. Justification: The Legal Declaration

While regeneration describes the internal change of the believer's nature, Justification describes the objective change of the believer's legal standing before the tribunal of a holy God. It answers the supreme judicial dilemma of the ages: How can a perfectly just God clear a guilty sinner without compromising His own justice?

The Forensic Reality of Right Standing

Justification must be strictly understood as a forensic or legal term. It belongs to the vocabulary of the ancient law court, not the hospital. It stands in distinct contrast to sanctification: while sanctification is a progressive process that changes a believer's inner moral state, justification is an instantaneous legal declaration that alters the believer's legal standing.

When God justifies a sinner, He does not make them subjectively perfect in their daily behavior at that moment. Rather, acting as the Supreme Judge of the cosmos, He issues a formal legal decree that the believing sinner is officially not guilty and is now possessed of a valid, unstained right standing before Him.

The Ground of Justification: Imputation

This divine legal verdict is not a legal fiction, nor does it mean God simply "winks" at transgression. It rests entirely upon a dual transaction known as imputation (or legal crediting):

Because Christ fully satisfied the judicial wrath of God through His penal substitutionary death, His flawless, lifelong active righteousness is legally credited to the believer. When God looks upon the justified sinner, He sees the perfect moral record of Jesus Christ.

Scriptural Proof and Exegesis

Abraham as the Theological Model (Romans 4:1–5): Paul demonstrates that right standing has always been by grace through faith, apart from works: "Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness." To the one who does not work but trusts Him who justifies the ungodly, his faith is counted as righteousness.

Objective Peace with God (Romans 5:1): The immediate, unshakeable consequence of this legal declaration is the cessation of cosmic hostility: "Therefore, since we have been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ."

The Double Imputation (2 Corinthians 5:21): The core mechanism of the atonement and its result: "For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God."

The Shield Against All Accusations (Romans 8:33–34): Because God is the ultimate justification-issuing Authority, no external accusation—whether from Satan, the law, or conscience—can overturn the verdict: "Who shall bring any charge against God's elect? It is God who justifies."

The Exclusivity of Faith (Galatians 2:16–21): Paul vehemently warns against adding human merit to the gospel, asserting that "a person is not justified by works of the law but through faith in Jesus Christ." If righteousness could be achieved through human effort or the law, then Christ died for no purpose.

3. Forgiveness of Sins: The Cancellation of Debt

Justification provides the believer with a positive, perfect legal righteousness; Forgiveness of Sins provides the negative, absolute removal of our crushing moral liability. They are two sides of the same redemptive coin: justification grants us a position we did not earn, while forgiveness pardons us from the punishment we richly deserved.

The Absolute Cleanse and Pardon

To be biblically forgiven means that the legal and relational debt incurred by our active rebellion and inherited corruption has been completely and permanently canceled. In the ancient world, a pardon was a sovereign act that wiped away a criminal's record. In the divine economy, God promises to no longer hold our transgressions against us.

This forgiveness is absolute and comprehensive. Because Christ’s sacrifice was of infinite value, the believer has been definitively saved and delivered from the eternal penalty of their sins. The Christian no longer walks under the terrifying canopy of divine wrath, nor do they ever have to fear standing under final condemnation at the judgment seat of God.

Scriptural Proof and Exegesis

The Universal Call (Acts 10:43): Peter proclaims the universal scope of the gospel message to the household of Cornelius, testifying "that everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name."

Anchored in the Blood (Ephesians 1:7 & Colossians 1:13–14): Paul anchors the cancellation of our debt directly in the historical substitutionary death of the Savior: "In him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses, according to the riches of his grace."

The Threat Removed (John 3:36): The text presents a stark, binary reality regarding human destiny: "Whoever believes in the Son has eternal life; whoever does not obey the Son shall not see life, but the wrath of God remains on him." For the believer, that remaining wrath is totally removed.

The Ultimate Security (Romans 8:1): The triumphant, pastoral climax of the forensic argument: "There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus."

The Totality of Cleanse (1 John 1:9, Psalm 103:12): God promises to remove our sins as far as the east is from the west, ensuring that when we confess, He is perfectly "faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness."

4. Adoption: Placement into the Divine Family

Salvation moves beyond the legal courtroom and enters the intimate setting of the home through the grand benefit of Adoption.

From Outcasts to Legitimate Heirs

Adoption is the legal act of God whereby He receives those who were by nature "children of wrath," outcasts, and spiritual orphans into His family, granting them all the rights, privileges, and responsibilities of adult sons and daughters.

Through adoption, the believer’s relational identity is completely overhauled. We do not enter God's house merely as hired servants or pardoned criminals who must stay in the margins; we are brought to the family table as loved co-heirs with Jesus Christ.

Scriptural Proof and Exegesis

The Predestined Choice (Ephesians 1:5): Adoption was never an emergency response to the Fall; it was woven into God's pre-temporal plan: He "predestined us for adoption to himself as sons through Jesus Christ, according to the purpose of his will."

Redemed to Be Adopted (Galatians 4:4–7): In the fullness of time, Christ was born under the law specifically to redeem those condemned by the law, "so that we might receive adoption as sons." Because we are sons, we are no longer slaves, but heirs through God.

The Cry of Intimacy (Romans 8:15–17): Believers do not receive a spirit of slavery to fall back into fear, dread, and relational distance before a holy God. Instead, they receive the Spirit of adoption, by whom we cry out, "Abba! Father!" The Spirit Himself bears witness with our spirit that we are children of God, and if children, then "heirs of God and fellow heirs with Christ."

The Father's Marvelous Love (1 John 3:1–2): The Apostle John stands in awe of this relational reality, exclaiming: "See what kind of love the Father has given to us, that we should be called children of God; and so we are."

5. Sanctification: The Process of Holiness

While justification fixes our standing instantly, Sanctification is the dynamic, ongoing result of that new standing, working itself out within human time and history. It represents the process of internal transformation where the believer is progressively conformed into the moral image of Jesus Christ.

The Three Tenses of Sanctification

To teach this doctrine without causing confusion, an instructor must divide sanctification into its distinct biblical dimensions:

Definitive (Positional) Sanctification: This is an instantaneous act occurring at conversion. The believer is legally and positionally broken away from the dominion of sin, set apart for God's possession, and declared to be a "saint" or "holy one" based on their union with Christ (1 Corinthians 1:2; 6:11).

Progressive Sanctification: This is the lifelong, continuous process where the Holy Spirit actively subdues the remaining power of habitual sin in the believer’s daily life, increasing their desire and ability to walk in obedience to God (2 Corinthians 3:18).

Final Sanctification (Glorification): The ultimate culmination of the process when the believer enters glory, is completely separated from the very presence of sin, and receives a perfected, resurrected body (1 John 3:2).

The Synergistic Cooperation

Unlike justification—which is entirely monergistic (God alone declares the verdict without human cooperation)—progressive sanctification is synergistic. It requires the active, responsible cooperation of the regenerated believer working in perfect partnership with the indwelling Holy Spirit. Believers are not passive; they are commanded to aggressively fight sin, pursue righteousness, and use the means of grace (the Word, prayer, sacraments, and church community) to grow.

Scriptural Proof and Exegesis

Mortifying the Flesh (Romans 8:12–14): Believers are no longer under an obligation to live according to the old, fallen nature. Rather, through the enabling power of the Spirit, they are called to actively "put to death the deeds of the body" to live out their identity as sons of God.

The Active Pursuit (Philippians 2:12–13): Paul perfectly balances human responsibility and divine enablement: "work out your own salvation with fear and trembling, for it is God who works in you, both to will and to work for his good pleasure."

The Unyielding Mandate (1 Peter 1:14–16): As obedient children, Christians must not be shaped by the passions of their former spiritual ignorance. Instead, they must mirror their Creator: "As he who called you is holy, you also be holy in all your conduct, since it is written, 'You shall be holy, for I am holy.'"

The Refining Discipline of the Father (Hebrews 12:10–14): God actively and lovingly disciplines His children through trials and providential circumstances with a specific, holy design: "that we may share his holiness." The text starkly warns that believers must strive for the holiness "without which no one will see the Lord."

The Command to Cleanse (2 Corinthians 7:1): Holiness demands a clean break from moral defilement: "Since we have these promises, beloved, let us cleanse ourselves from every defilement of body and spirit, bringing holiness to completion in the fear of God."

6. Reconciliation: The Restoration of Peace

The structural consequence of human sin was not merely a subjective feeling of guilt; it created an objective state of enmity, alienation, and judicial hostility between a holy God and a rebellious human race. Through Reconciliation, that barrier is broken down, and objective harmony is established.

The Cessation of Cosmic Hostility

Reconciliation means that the relational rift has been perfectly mended. The cross has removed the cause of the alienation—namely, our sin and the righteous wrath of God against it. Consequently, the "war" is over. The believer is no longer an outlaw under the impending judgment of the King; they have been brought into a state of structural, permanent peace.

Scriptural Proof and Exegesis

The Status of Objective Peace (Romans 5:1): Because our legal standing has been corrected through justification, we possess actual, relational peace with God through the mediation of Jesus Christ.

Reconciled While Enemies (Romans 5:10–11): The magnitude of grace is shown in that God acted while we were still hostile rebels: "For if while we were enemies we were reconciled to God by the death of his Son, much more, now that we are reconciled, shall we be saved by his life."

The Ministry of Reconciliation (2 Corinthians 5:18–21): God is the author of this restored peace. Through Christ, He reconciled us to Himself and has entrusted to the Church the sacred message of reconciliation, namely, that "in Christ God was reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them."

Brought Near by Blood (Ephesians 2:13–16): Those who were once far off, alienated from the covenants of promise, have been radically brought near by the blood of Christ. He has broken down the dividing wall of hostility, making peace and reconciling us to God in one body through the cross, thereby killing the hostility.

Blameless in His Sight (Colossians 1:19–22): It pleased the Father to reconcile all things to Himself, "making peace by the blood of his cross." You, who were once alienated and hostile in mind, doing evil deeds, He has now reconciled in His body of flesh by His death, in order to present you holy, blameless, and above reproach before Him.

7. Eternal Life: The Ultimate Destination

The final, overarching, and all-encompassing result of salvation is the possession of Eternal Life. In Evangelical systematic theology, this concept must never be reduced to a mere endless duration of chronological time; it refers to a specific, transcendent quality of life—unbroken, perfect, intimate fellowship with the Triune God.

A Present Possession and Future Guarantee

Eternal life is both a present reality and an eschatological certainty. The moment a sinner exercises saving faith, they pass instantly from the realm of spiritual death into the realm of eternal life. This life is completely secure because it is anchored in the immutable promise and holding power of Jesus Christ.

Scriptural Proof and Exegesis

The Gift Countering the Wages (Romans 6:23): The ultimate contrast of systematic theology: "For the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord."

The Christological Definition (John 17:3): In His High Priestly Prayer, Jesus defines the very essence of this benefit: "And this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent." Eternal life is relational knowledge of God.

The Present Reality of the Gift (John 3:36 & John 5:24): The possession of this life is immediate: "Whoever believes in the Son has eternal life." Truly, truly, whoever hears the word and believes Him who sent Christ "has eternal life. He does not come into judgment, but has passed from death to life."

The Absolute Security of the Sheep (John 10:27–29): Jesus provides absolute pastoral comfort, guaranteeing that the life He gives cannot be lost or stolen: "I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand... and no one is able to snatch them out of the Father's hand."

The Certainty of Knowledge (1 John 5:11–13): John concludes his canonical witness so that believers do not live in crippling doubt: "And this is the testimony, that God gave us eternal life, and this life is in his Son... I write these things to you who believe in the name of the Son of God, that you may know that you have eternal life."

Conclusion: The Golden Chain of Salvation

As we bring this study of Soteriology to its ultimate close, the student must see that these individual results are not fragmented, disconnected blessings. They are parts of a flawless, divinely forged, unshakeable continuum of grace. The Apostle Paul traces this unbreakable sequence from eternity past to eternity future in what theologians call The Golden Chain of Salvation:

"And those whom he predestined he also called, and those whom he called he also justified, and those whom he justified he also glorified." > — Romans 8:30

Every single individual who is sovereignly elected and regenerated will inevitably be justified, pardoned, adopted, progressively sanctified, and ultimately glorified. Not a single link in this chain can be broken. The study of salvation must ultimately move from the analytical corners of the academic classroom into the deep sanctuary of our hearts, compelling both teacher and student to live a life of radical holiness, bold evangelism, and ceaseless worship “to the praise of his glorious grace.”

The Multi-Dimensional Benefits of Salvation

Benefit of Salvation Contextual Domain Core Functional Definition Definitive Structural Impact Primary Scriptural Anchors
Regeneration The Heart / Inner Nature Instantaneous, monergistic impartation of spiritual life to a dead soul. Breaks the penalty of sin; gives a new nature; makes us a new creation. John 3:3–8; 2 Cor 5:17; Titus 3:5
Justification The Courtroom / Legal Instantaneous, forensic declaration by God that the sinner is "not guilty." Imputes Christ's perfect active righteousness to our account. Romans 4:1–5; Romans 5:1; 2 Cor 5:21
Forgiveness The Judicial Ledger Sovereign cancellation of our total moral debt and legal liability. Permanently removes the believer from under divine wrath and condemnation. Ephesians 1:7; Acts 10:43; Romans 8:1
Adoption The Family Home / Relational Legal placement of former outcasts into the family of God as full children. Grants full inheritance rights; prompts the intimate cry of "Abba! Father!" Galatians 4:4–7; Romans 8:15–17; Eph 1:5
Sanctification The Daily Walk / Behavioral Progressive, synergistic conformity of character and conduct to Christ. Subdues the power of sin; cleanses from defilement of body and spirit. 1 Peter 1:14–16; Phil 2:12–13; Heb 12:14
Reconciliation The Objective Relationship Total dismantling of alienation and hostility, establishing harmony. Achieves structural, permanent peace with God through the cross. 2 Cor 5:18–21; Romans 5:10; Col 1:19–22
Eternal Life Ultimate Destiny / Quality Secure, unending relational fellowship with the Triune God. Guarantees that the believer has already passed from death to life and will never perish. John 17:3; Romans 6:23; John 10:27–29
Q

Question Bank

358 short questions with answers, covering all subjects

Tap a question to reveal its answer. Only revealed answers are printed — hide answers to print a question paper, show them to print an answer key. Combine with search + “Matching sections only” to print questions on a single topic.

Christology — 65 Questions

1.1What does the term 'Christology' literally mean?
The study of Christ — from the Greek Christos ('Anointed One' / 'Messiah') and logos ('study' or 'doctrine').
1.2What is Christology the study of?
The branch of systematic theology that studies the person, nature, offices, and work of Jesus Christ.
1.3What central question of Jesus does Christology seek to answer?
'Who do you say that I am?' (Matthew 16:15).
1.4Name three key Christological texts.
John 1:1–18; Philippians 2:5–11; Colossians 1:15–22 (also Ephesians 1:3–10; Colossians 2:9–15; Hebrews 1:1–9).
1.5What does the pre-existence of Christ mean?
Jesus existed before His birth at Bethlehem; He did not begin His life on earth but entered the world through incarnation.
1.6Which verse records Jesus saying 'Before Abraham was, I AM'?
John 8:58.
1.7What does the eternality of Christ mean?
He has no beginning and no end — He existed eternally before His incarnation and continues into eternity future.
1.8Which verse says 'Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever'?
Hebrews 13:8.
1.9Which verse declares that 'in Him dwells all the fullness of the Godhead bodily'?
Colossians 2:9.
1.10Define omnipresence and give one verse showing Jesus is omnipresent.
Being present at all places at the same time; e.g. Matthew 18:20 — 'Where two or three are gathered in My name, I am there.'
1.11Give one proof of Jesus' omniscience.
He knew the Samaritan woman's past (John 4:17–19), knew what was in man (John 2:24–25), and knew the scribes' thoughts (Mark 2:8).
1.12Give one demonstration of Jesus' omnipotence.
He calmed the storm (Matthew 8:26–27), raised Lazarus (John 11:43–44), and claimed all authority (Matthew 28:18).
1.13What is immutability?
Being unchanging in nature, character, and purpose — Jesus remains the same forever (Hebrews 13:8).
1.14What does the name 'Jesus' mean?
'The Lord saves' — from the Hebrew Yeshua, revealing His mission to save people from sin (Matthew 1:21).
1.15What does 'Immanuel' mean?
'God with us' — God came to live among humanity through Jesus (Isaiah 7:14; Matthew 1:23).
1.16What does the title 'Christ' mean?
'The Anointed One' — the Greek Christos, translating the Hebrew for Messiah.
1.17How does Jesus identify Himself with Yahweh?
He applies God's covenant name 'I AM' (Exodus 3:14) to Himself in John 8:58.
1.18What does the title 'Son of Man' emphasise?
Jesus' humanity and His role as the suffering Savior and coming Judge (Daniel 7:13–14; Mark 10:45).
1.19What does the title 'Son of David' confirm?
That Jesus is the promised Messianic King, heir to David's throne (2 Samuel 7:12–13; Matthew 1:1).
1.20What does the Greek word monogenes mean?
'One and only' / unique, one-of-a-kind Son — speaking of Jesus' unique relationship and nature, not origin in time (John 3:16).
1.21Does 'firstborn' (prototokos) mean Jesus was created?
No — it means supreme in rank, authority, and inheritance, not first created (Colossians 1:15–18; cf. David called firstborn in Psalm 89:27).
1.22What is a Christophany?
A pre-incarnate appearance of Christ in the Old Testament, especially as the Angel of the LORD.
1.23Give two reasons the Angel of the LORD is identified with Christ.
He is equated with God and worshiped as God (Exodus 3:1–5), and He never appears after Jesus is born.
1.24How does the Passover lamb prefigure Christ?
Its blood saved Israel's firstborn from death; Christ is our Passover Lamb (Exodus 12; 1 Corinthians 5:7; John 1:29).
1.25How does the bronze serpent prefigure Christ?
Those bitten lived by looking at it; the Son of Man was lifted up so all who believe may have eternal life (Numbers 21; John 3:14–15).
1.26In what way is Adam a type of Christ?
Paul calls Adam 'a type of the one to come' (Romans 5:14); Adam's disobedience brought death, Christ's obedience brings life — the second Adam.
1.27Which prophecy foretold the Messiah's birthplace, and where?
Micah 5:2 — Bethlehem (fulfilled in Matthew 2:1–6).
1.28Which prophecy foretold the virgin birth?
Isaiah 7:14 — fulfilled in Matthew 1:22–23.
1.29Which Psalm describes pierced hands and feet centuries before crucifixion?
Psalm 22:16 (fulfilled at the cross, John 19).
1.30Which Psalm foretells the resurrection ('will not see decay')?
Psalm 16:10 — applied to Jesus in Acts 2:24–31.
1.31How much was the Messiah betrayed for, and where was it prophesied?
Thirty pieces of silver — Zechariah 11:12–13, fulfilled in Matthew 26:14–15.
1.32What is the incarnation?
The act by which the eternal Son of God took upon Himself human flesh — 'The Word became flesh and dwelt among us' (John 1:14).
1.33Give two reasons it was necessary for Jesus to become man.
To reveal God to humanity, to identify with humanity, to fulfill the law perfectly, and to die as a substitute for sin.
1.34Why did God choose the virgin birth?
To unite full deity and full humanity, prevent the transmission of sin, fulfill Isaiah 7:14, show salvation is from God alone, and establish Jesus as the second Adam.
1.35Which two Gospels record the virgin birth, and from whose perspectives?
Matthew (from Joseph's perspective) and Luke (from Mary's perspective).
1.36Define the kenosis.
Christ's voluntary self-emptying (Philippians 2:6–8): He did not empty Himself of deity but laid aside the independent exercise of His divine privileges.
1.37Did the kenosis mean Jesus lost His deity?
No — the emptying happened by addition (taking human nature and a servant's role), not subtraction; He never ceased to be God (Colossians 2:9).
1.38What is the hypostatic union?
The union of Christ's divine and human natures in one person — without confusion, change, division, or separation.
1.39What is meant by 'one Person, two natures'?
Jesus Christ is one divine Person possessing two complete natures, fully divine and fully human.
1.40What error did Arius teach and which council answered it?
That Christ was a created being, not eternal God; answered at the Council of Nicea (AD 325).
1.41What did the Council of Nicea declare about Christ?
That Christ is of the same essence (homoousios) as the Father — eternal, uncreated, and true God.
1.42What error did Apollinarius teach and which council addressed it?
That Christ had a human body and soul but a divine spirit (not true man); addressed at Constantinople (AD 381).
1.43What error did Nestorius teach and which council addressed it?
He divided Christ, denying the true union of God and man ('god-bearer,' not true God-man); addressed at Ephesus (AD 431).
1.44What error did Eutyches teach and which council answered it?
That Christ's humanity was swallowed up in His divinity; answered at Chalcedon (AD 451), which affirmed the hypostatic union.
1.45Why was Jesus baptized though sinless?
To identify with sinful humanity and to fulfill all righteousness (Matthew 3:13–17); His baptism also revealed the Trinity.
1.46How did Jesus answer each of Satan's three temptations?
With Scripture — Deuteronomy 8:3; 6:16; and 6:13.
1.47What was the real temptation in 'turn stones to bread'?
To use divine power independently of the Father, not merely to eat.
1.48What do the temptations prove about Jesus?
He is fully human and understands our struggles, fully obedient and sinless, and qualified to be our sympathetic High Priest (Hebrews 4:15; 2:18).
1.49Which verses affirm Jesus' sinlessness?
2 Corinthians 5:21 ('knew no sin') and 1 Peter 2:22 ('committed no sin').
1.50Name the four great periods of biblical miracles.
The Exodus (Moses), the prophets Elijah and Elisha, the time of Christ, and the time of Acts and the Apostles.
1.51Give two specific purposes of Jesus' miracles.
They authenticated Jesus as Messiah (John 20:30–31) and proved He was God (John 9:30–33), glorifying the Father and producing faith.
1.52Name the four offices of Christ.
Prophet, Priest, Judge, and King.
1.53How is Christ the ultimate Prophet?
He reveals God perfectly and finally — He is not merely a bearer of the Word, He is the Word (Hebrews 1:1–2; John 1:1, 14).
1.54How does Christ's priesthood surpass the Levitical priesthood?
He offered Himself, once for all, and continually intercedes for believers (Hebrews 9:12; 10:10; 7:25).
1.55What does substitution mean in Christ's death?
One person dying in the place of another — Jesus took upon Himself what sinners deserved (Romans 6:23; 2 Corinthians 5:21).
1.56Give three characteristics of Christ's death.
It was voluntary, sacrificial, substitutionary, atoning, and sufficient for all.
1.57Give three evidences for the bodily resurrection of Christ.
The empty tomb, the linen wrappings left behind, the appearances to many witnesses, and the transformed boldness of the apostles.
1.58Name two false views of the resurrection.
That Christ only lost consciousness on the cross; that the disciples stole the body; that only His spirit was raised, not His body.
1.59What was Jesus' resurrection body like?
Physical and real yet glorified — recognizable, able to eat and speak, yet not limited by time, space, or material objects (Luke 24; John 20–21).
1.60After how many days of post-resurrection ministry did Jesus ascend?
Forty days (Acts 1:1–11).
1.61Give two reasons the ascension is significant.
It completes the resurrection, commences Christ's re-exaltation, activates His intercessory ministry, and allows the Holy Spirit to come (John 16:7).
1.62What is Christ doing now in heaven for believers?
Interceding for them (Romans 8:34; 1 John 2:1), securely protecting their salvation (Hebrews 7:25), and preparing a place for them (John 14:1–3).
1.63Name three aspects of Christ's ministry to the Church today.
Acting as head and guardian, being the chief cornerstone, cleansing His bride, and giving gifted men to build up the Church (Ephesians 4:7–16).
1.64What is the 'bema seat'?
Christ's future evaluation of believers in heaven (Romans 14:9–12; 2 Corinthians 5:9–10; 1 Corinthians 3:6–15).
1.65List three events in Christ's future ministry.
The rapture of His Church, resurrection/transformation of believers, tribulation judgment, the marriage of the Lamb, the second coming at Armageddon, and handing the kingdom to the Father.

Pneumatology — 35 Questions

2.1What is Pneumatology?
The study of the nature, person, and ministry of the Holy Spirit — the third Person of the Trinity.
2.2Why is equating the Holy Spirit simply with 'power' dangerous?
It denies His personhood and usually leads to erroneous practice in relation to doctrine.
2.3How does Matthew 28:19 show the Spirit's deity?
The 'name' of the Holy Spirit is placed on the same level as the Father and the Son in the baptismal formula.
2.4How does Acts 5:3–4 prove the Spirit is God?
Lying to the Holy Spirit is equated with lying to God.
2.5Name three divine attributes ascribed to the Holy Spirit with references.
Eternity (Hebrews 9:14), omniscience (1 Corinthians 2:10–11), omnipresence (Psalm 139:7–12); also life, love, and truth.
2.6Name four biblical symbols of the Holy Spirit.
Wind (John 3:8), oil (1 Samuel 16:13), living water (John 7:38–39), fire (Matthew 3:11–12), dove (John 1:32–34), seal, deposit, clothing, power.
2.7What does the symbol of the 'seal' signify?
Security — the believer is sealed by the Spirit (2 Corinthians 1:22; Ephesians 1:13–14).
2.8What does the Greek word endysēsthe ('clothed', Luke 24:49) imply?
Being fully wrapped or enveloped — divine empowerment, not self-generated strength.
2.9Give three titles of the Holy Spirit that denote His deity.
Spirit of Holiness (Romans 1:4), Spirit of the Lord (Luke 4:18), Spirit of Christ (Romans 8:9), Spirit of the Living God (2 Corinthians 3:3).
2.10What is meant by the procession of the Spirit?
The Holy Spirit eternally proceeds from the Father and the Son (John 15:26; 16:7).
2.11What does 'Paraclete' mean?
'One who comes alongside to aid or help' — Counselor, Comforter, Consoler, Advocate (John 14:16, 26; 16:7).
2.12Give three evidences that the Holy Spirit is a person, not a force.
He has emotions (Ephesians 4:30), a mind (Romans 8:27), and a will (1 Corinthians 12:11); He speaks, prays, guides, and teaches.
2.13In what ways can the Holy Spirit be sinned against?
He can be resisted (Acts 7:51), lied to (Acts 5:3), grieved (Ephesians 4:30), insulted (Hebrews 10:29), and quenched (1 Thessalonians 5:19).
2.14Why is the pronoun 'He' significant for the Spirit?
Scripture uses the masculine personal pronoun even though 'spirit' is neuter in Greek (John 16:13–14), affirming personhood.
2.15What does 'another Counselor' (John 14:16) mean?
'Another of the same kind' as Jesus — the Spirit is a divine person like Christ.
2.16How did the Spirit work in the Old Testament?
He came upon selected individuals temporarily, for special tasks or leadership, and could depart from a person.
2.17How does the Spirit's New Testament ministry differ from the Old?
He now dwells permanently in all believers for daily guidance, holiness, and power (John 14:17; 1 Corinthians 6:19).
2.18Give an Old Testament example of the Spirit departing from a person.
Saul — the Spirit came upon him but later departed (1 Samuel 10:10; 16:14).
2.19Give an example of the Spirit empowering for a task in the Old Testament.
Gideon (Judges 6:34) and Samson (Judges 15:14–15) — the Spirit came upon them for deliverance.
2.20What did Moses wish in Numbers 11:29?
'Oh, that all the Lord's people were prophets and that the Lord would put His Spirit upon them!'
2.21How was the Spirit involved in the life of Christ?
In His birth and resurrection (Romans 1:4; 8:11; 1 Peter 3:18); Jesus continually depended on the Spirit for ministry.
2.22Why might Acts be called 'The Acts of the Holy Spirit'?
Because the Spirit's work is so prominent — empowering the apostles' witness after Pentecost.
2.23What Old Testament prophecy did Pentecost fulfill?
Joel 2:28–29, together with Christ's promise (Luke 24:49; Acts 1:4–5).
2.24What were the 'tongues' in Acts 2?
Real foreign languages (glossa) unknown to the speakers but known to the hearers — a sign of authentication to unbelievers (1 Corinthians 14:22).
2.25What did tongues validate in Acts 10 and Acts 19?
In Acts 10, salvation coming to the Gentiles; in Acts 19, Paul's apostolic ministry.
2.26What is the primary purpose of the filling of the Spirit in Acts 1:8?
Empowerment for witnessing.
2.27What is the Spirit's present ministry to the lost world?
Convicting the world of sin, righteousness, and judgment (John 16:8–11) and restraining evil.
2.28What is the baptism of the Holy Spirit?
The Spirit's placing of every believer into the Body of Christ at salvation (1 Corinthians 12:13).
2.29What is the sealing of the Spirit?
God's mark of ownership and security guaranteeing the believer's inheritance (Ephesians 1:13–14).
2.30What is the permanent indwelling of the Spirit?
The Spirit lives in every believer permanently from salvation onward (John 14:17; 1 Corinthians 6:19).
2.31Distinguish the baptism and the filling of the Spirit.
Baptism is once-for-all at salvation, placing the believer into Christ's Body; filling is repeated, ongoing empowerment for life and service.
2.32What will the Spirit's role be during the Tribulation?
He continues to work — restraining evil until removed in His restraining role, and empowering witness and salvation during the period (cf. 2 Thessalonians 2).
2.33What will the Spirit's ministry be in the Millennial Kingdom?
Poured out abundantly — indwelling and empowering God's people in the kingdom age (Joel 2:28–29; Ezekiel 36:26–27).
2.34Why did Jesus, being God, depend on the Spirit?
As a man He demonstrated dependence on the Spirit, becoming the model for believers to live in the Spirit's power.
2.35Which verse calls the believer's body the temple of the Holy Spirit?
1 Corinthians 6:19.

Hamartiology — 45 Questions

3.1From which Greek word is Hamartiology derived, and what does it mean?
From hamartia, meaning sin — the branch of systematic theology that studies sin.
3.2What four major areas does Hamartiology study?
The origin of sin, the nature of sin, the consequences of sin, and the solution to sin.
3.3Why is the study of sin essential in theology?
It explains the human condition, reveals the need for salvation, magnifies God's grace, and gives clarity for Christian living.
3.4Name two modern false views of sin.
That sin is only a social construct; that sin is merely comparative (better/worse than others); that sin applies only to actions, not attitudes.
3.5How does the Bible affirm that sin is real?
Scripture affirms sin is a present and active reality (1 John 1:8; Genesis 4:7).
3.6What three kinds of death does sin produce?
Spiritual death, physical death, and eternal separation (Romans 6:23; James 1:15).
3.7Why is sin ultimately against God?
Even when it harms others, its ultimate offense is against God's holiness — 'Against You, You only, have I sinned' (Psalm 51:4).
3.8Did God create sin?
No — sin is not a created substance; it is a privation (absence of good) and a corruption of what God made good (James 1:13).
3.9What is meant by sin as 'privation'?
The absence or lack of righteousness where it ought to be — as darkness is the absence of light.
3.10Give two reasons God permitted sin.
To display His justice and mercy, to accomplish His sovereign plans (Genesis 50:20), and to reveal His glory through redemption.
3.11What two things lie at the root of sin's origin?
Pride (self-exaltation) and the misuse of free will (choice against God).
3.12Where did sin first appear?
In Satan (Ezekiel 28:15–16), and it entered humanity through Adam's disobedience (Romans 5:12).
3.13Give Grudem's definition of sin.
'Sin is any failure to conform to the moral law of God in act, attitude, or nature.'
3.14Give Ryrie's definition of sin.
'Sin is anything in the creature which does not express, or which is contrary to, the holy character of the Creator.'
3.15Give a one-line theological summary definition of sin.
'Any deviation from the character, will, or law of God in thought, word, or deed.'
3.16What does 'missing the mark' mean?
Failing to attain God's perfect standard of righteousness — falling short of His glory (Romans 3:23).
3.17Differentiate transgression and rebellion.
Transgression is deliberately crossing God's revealed boundaries (Romans 2:23); rebellion is active heart-level resistance against God's rule (Isaiah 59:13).
3.18What is lawlessness as a description of sin?
Living as though there is no divine law — disregarding God's moral order (1 John 3:4).
3.19What are sins of omission? Give an example.
Failing to do what is right (James 4:17) — e.g. not helping others, not praying, not acting in faith.
3.20What is the total scope of sin?
Sin includes thoughts, attitudes, actions, and omissions (Matthew 5:28; Proverbs 21:2; Galatians 5:19–21; James 4:17).
3.21How does sin deceive?
By hiding consequences, distorting truth, and promising false satisfaction (Hebrews 3:13; Genesis 3:1–5; Hebrews 11:25).
3.22Describe sin's enslaving progression.
Habit formation, then loss of control, then spiritual slavery — 'everyone who sins is a slave to sin' (John 8:34).
3.23What three things does sin ultimately destroy?
Life, relationships, and eternal destiny (Romans 6:23; Isaiah 59:2; Revelation 20:14–15).
3.24What does separation from God involve?
Alienation, spiritual distance, and enmity with God (Colossians 1:21; Isaiah 59:2; Romans 8:7).
3.25Define spiritual death.
The condition of being separated from God and devoid of spiritual life, unresponsive to spiritual truth (Ephesians 2:1; 1 Corinthians 2:14).
3.26What is condemnation?
The legal standing of guilt before God's righteous standard, under divine judgment (Romans 3:19–23; John 3:18).
3.27What does it mean to be 'in Adam'?
All humans inherit sin, share in death, and live under the curse because of Adam's fall (Romans 5:12; 1 Corinthians 15:22).
3.28What are the eternal consequences of unredeemed sin?
Eternal separation from God, judgment, and the lake of fire (2 Thessalonians 1:8–9; Revelation 20:14–15).
3.29What is the threefold problem of sin for the unbeliever?
Inherited sin nature, imputed sin, and personal sins.
3.30Define inherited sin nature.
The corrupted nature transmitted from Adam to all his descendants — we are 'born in sin' (Psalm 51:5).
3.31What does the 'poisoned well' illustrate?
Inherited sin: since the source (the heart) is corrupted, everything that flows from it is affected.
3.32What is imputed sin?
God's judicial act of crediting Adam's original sin to the account of all humanity, since Adam was our federal head (Romans 5:12).
3.33What does the 'joint bank account' illustrate?
Imputed sin — Adam's act of rebellion is legally reckoned to our account because he was our representative.
3.34What are personal sins, and what do they prove?
Actual sins of thought, word, and deed (commission and omission); they confirm the guilt and sinful nature already present.
3.35Are we sinners because we sin, or do we sin because we are sinners?
We commit acts of sin because we are already constituted sinners in Adam — by imputed guilt and inherited nature.
3.36How complete is the believer's forgiveness?
Past, present, and future sins were judicially dealt with at the cross (Colossians 2:13–14; Hebrews 10:10–14).
3.37Is the believer's sin nature removed at salvation?
No — it was condemned at the cross and its power broken, but it remains until glorification (Romans 8:1–3; Philippians 3:20–21).
3.38Distinguish fellowship and relationship regarding a believer's sin.
Sin breaks fellowship (communion), not relationship (sonship) — relationship is judicial and permanent; fellowship is experiential and variable (1 John 1:6–9).
3.39On what is the believer's victory over sin based?
Union with Christ, the indwelling Holy Spirit, and knowledge of truth — who the believer is in Christ, not merely what he does.
3.40Name three means of experiencing victory over sin.
Walking by the Spirit (Galatians 5:16), renewing the mind (Romans 12:2), and obedience to God's Word (James 1:22).
3.41What is 'put off / put on' in daily Christian living?
Rejecting the old sinful patterns (Colossians 3:5–9) and actively cultivating Christlike character (Ephesians 4:24).
3.42What are the steps for a believer's restoration after sinning?
Confession, repentance, and faith in Christ's finished work (1 John 1:9; Acts 3:19).
3.43Does restoration restore salvation or fellowship?
Fellowship — salvation was never lost; the believer's position in Christ remains secure.
3.44State the three dimensions of salvation over sin.
Penalty removed (justification), power broken (sanctification), presence removed (glorification).
3.45What four responses should the doctrine of sin produce?
Humility, dependence on God, gratitude for salvation, and pursuit of holiness.

Bibliology — 54 Questions

6.1What does 'Bibliology' literally mean?
'Study of the books' — from Biblia + ology; the study of what the Bible is, how it came to be, and why it is authoritative.
6.2Why is Bibliology called the epistemological foundation of theology?
Because we must first establish how we know what we know; if our understanding of Scripture is weak, every other doctrine is affected.
6.3Define revelation.
The act of God unveiling Himself — truths we could never discover on our own unless He chose to make them known.
6.4Is revelation man's search for God?
No — it is God's gracious initiative to make Himself known to man (Deuteronomy 29:29; John 1:18).
6.5What are the two main categories of revelation?
General revelation and special revelation.
6.6Name the three channels of general revelation.
Creation (Psalm 19:1–6), conscience (Romans 2:14–15), and providence (Acts 17:26–27).
6.7Give three characteristics of general revelation through creation.
It is continuous, wordless, and worldwide (Psalm 19:1–6).
6.8What invisible attributes of God does creation make evident?
His eternal power and divine nature (Romans 1:18–21).
6.9What is the key limitation of general revelation?
It cannot declare God's grace or explain the way of salvation — it reveals a Creator, but not how to be reconciled to Him.
6.10What is the 'painting' illustration of general revelation's limits?
Looking at a painting reveals there is a painter, but not his name, character, or how to personally know him.
6.11Define special revelation.
God revealing Himself to specific people, at specific times, through specific means — knowledge unattainable by reason or nature.
6.12What is progressive revelation?
God did not reveal all truth at once but gradually, in stages, as His redemptive plan advanced — each revelation building on the previous.
6.13Why is the Bible more than a record about revelation?
It is itself revelation — 'All Scripture is God-breathed' (2 Timothy 3:16); through it God continues to speak.
6.14Who is the living Word, and what does He reveal?
Jesus Christ (John 1:1, 14) — the perfect and final revelation of God, making His invisible nature visible (Hebrews 1:3).
6.15Contrast general and special revelation in one line each.
General: universal, reveals God's power and moral order, sufficient to condemn. Special: particular, reveals God's plan of redemption, sufficient to save.
6.16Define inspiration.
The supernatural influence of the Holy Spirit upon the human authors of Scripture, ensuring they wrote exactly what God intended, without error, in the original manuscripts.
6.17What does theopneustos mean?
'God-breathed' (2 Timothy 3:16) — Scripture is the very expression of God's mind and will.
6.18What three implications follow if all Scripture is God-breathed?
It carries divine authority, it is truthful and trustworthy, and it is sufficient and useful for teaching, rebuking, correcting, and training.
6.19What does 2 Peter 1:21 teach about how Scripture came?
Prophecy never had its origin in the human will; men spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit.
6.20What is the Intuition (Natural) theory of inspiration?
That the writers were merely religious geniuses with heightened insight — unusual men like Shakespeare, not supernaturally inspired.
6.21What is the Illumination theory of inspiration?
That the Spirit did not inspire the words but only heightened the writers' perception of truth.
6.22What is the Dynamic (Conceptual) theory?
That God inspired the ideas or concepts, and the authors used their own words to express them.
6.23What is the Dictation theory?
That God dictated the words directly and the authors acted merely as secretaries.
6.24Define Plenary Verbal Inspiration.
Plenary = all of Scripture is inspired; Verbal = the very words are inspired — Scripture is fully divine and fully human.
6.25Define inerrancy.
The Bible, in its original manuscripts, is without error in everything it affirms — doctrine, history, ethics, and matters of fact.
6.26What statement classically defines inerrancy, from 1978?
The Chicago Statement on Biblical Inerrancy.
6.27Give two things inerrancy does NOT mean.
That writers used scientific precision or modern terminology; that there are no manuscript variations; that every translation is perfect.
6.28Define infallibility.
Scripture cannot fail — it is completely trustworthy and unfailing in accomplishing God's purpose (rooted in the God 'who never lies,' Titus 1:2).
6.29Distinguish inerrancy and infallibility.
Inerrancy = truthfulness (the Bible does not err); infallibility = trustworthiness (the Bible cannot fail or mislead).
6.30Explain the Christ–Scripture analogy.
Both are divine and human united in one medium: two natures in one person (Christ); two natures in the same propositions (Scripture) — both without error.
6.31Complete the parallel: Christ is without sin; the Bible is without ___.
Error — the living incarnate Word and the living written Word.
6.32How did Jesus treat Old Testament narratives?
As straightforward records of fact — including Adam and Eve, Noah's flood, Sodom, and Jonah.
6.33What did Jesus say about Scripture's permanence?
It cannot pass away (Matthew 5:17) or be broken (John 10:35).
6.34Define illumination.
The Holy Spirit's supernatural work enabling believers to understand, embrace, and apply Scripture (1 Corinthians 2:14; Psalm 119:130).
6.35Why is illumination necessary?
Because of moral antipathy (sin rejects truth) and spiritual blindness (without the Spirit, truth is inaccessible).
6.36What is illumination NOT?
It does not produce new revelation outside Scripture, and it is not a one-time event but ongoing.
6.37How do interpretation and illumination relate?
Interpretation is human effort (language, culture, context); illumination is divine enabling — both work hand in hand; neither is sufficient alone.
6.38Define the authority of Scripture (Grudem).
All the words in Scripture are God's words in such a way that to disbelieve or disobey any word of Scripture is to disbelieve or disobey God.
6.39Above what does the Bible stand as final authority?
Above tradition, reason, and human opinion — for faith and practice.
6.40What is the canon?
A 'measuring rod' — the standard measuring whether a book is God's inspired truth; God's people discovering what God already inspired.
6.41How many books are in the OT and NT canons?
39 in the Old Testament, 27 in the New Testament.
6.42What are the three tests of OT canonicity?
Is the author a prophet or eyewitness? Does it match God's known truth? Was it accepted and used by God's people?
6.43What are the four tests of NT canonicity?
Apostolic authorship or close association; agreement with OT truth; orthodox teaching; wide acceptance and use by the churches.
6.44Where and when was the OT list settled?
At Jamnia, around 400 BC — no books after Malachi.
6.45How did Jesus confirm the OT canon?
In Luke 24:44 He affirmed 'the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms' — our exact 39 books — and quoted them as Scripture 78 times.
6.46What discovery confirmed the accuracy of the OT text?
The Dead Sea Scrolls (found 1947), matching our OT.
6.47Who first listed the exact 27 NT books, and when?
Athanasius, in his Easter letter of AD 367.
6.48Which councils confirmed the NT canon, and when?
Hippo and Carthage, AD 397 — agreeing on what churches already used, not inventing the list.
6.49How does the NT manuscript evidence compare to other ancient texts?
About 5,000 copies with 99+% accuracy and an earliest copy ~130 AD — far surpassing Homer (643 copies) or Caesar (10 copies).
6.50Name four apocryphal books.
Tobit, Judith, Wisdom, Sirach, Baruch, 1–2 Maccabees, Additions to Esther, Additions to Daniel.
6.51What did liberalism teach about the Bible?
That it is not divine revelation but the fallible product of human authors — a record of men's thoughts about God, judged by human reason (Kant, Schleiermacher).
6.52What did Karl Barth's neo-orthodoxy teach about the Bible?
The Bible may contain the Word of God but is not the Word of God; revelation comes only through personal encounter, so biblical truth is relative and subjective.
6.53What is the Roman Catholic view of Scripture and tradition?
Church tradition and interpretation carry the same authority as the Bible — the Bible teaches what the Church says it teaches.
6.54Summarize the conservative evangelical view of the Bible.
The Bible is God's divine supernatural revelation; its inerrant, inspired words are authoritative — God's objective, absolute truth, not subjective or relative.

Theology Proper — 20 Questions

7.1What is Theology Proper?
The doctrine of God — the study of God's existence, essence, and attributes as He has revealed Himself.
7.2Why is knowing about God not the same as knowing God?
Information alone is insufficient; Theology Proper aims at relational knowledge that produces worship, rooted in God's self-revelation.
7.3What is agnosticism, and what is its flaw?
'We cannot know God' — its flaw is that God has revealed Himself, so He can be truly (though not exhaustively) known.
7.4What is rationalism, and what is its flaw?
'Reason is the final authority' — its flaw is that finite, fallen reason cannot sit in judgment over infinite God; reason is a tool, not the source of truth.
7.5What is fideism, and what is its flaw?
'Just believe without evidence' — its flaw is that biblical faith is grounded in God's revelation and evidence, not blind leaps.
7.6What is experientialism, and what is its flaw?
'Truth is what I feel' — feelings fluctuate and can deceive; experience must be tested by Scripture.
7.7What is pragmatism as an approach to truth?
'Truth is what works' — but what seems to work is not necessarily true; Scripture, not results, defines truth.
7.8Define pantheism.
The view that everything is God and God is everything — identifying the creation with the Creator.
7.9Define deism.
The view that God created the world but does not intervene in it — a divine watchmaker who wound the world and left it.
7.10Define polytheism and theism.
Polytheism: belief in many gods. Theism: belief in one personal, transcendent Creator God who is involved with His creation.
7.11State the cosmological argument in three steps.
Everything that begins to exist has a cause; the universe began to exist; therefore the universe must have a cause — an eternal, powerful First Cause (Genesis 1:1).
7.12State the teleological argument.
Design and order always imply a designer; the universe displays incredible design; therefore the universe must have a Designer (Psalm 19:1).
7.13State the anthropological argument.
Human personality, moral awareness, and hunger for meaning and worship point to a personal, moral Creator (Romans 2:14–15).
7.14Give the Westminster Shorter Catechism's definition of God.
'God is a Spirit, infinite, eternal, and unchangeable, in His being, wisdom, power, holiness, justice, goodness, and truth.'
7.15List the five statements about God's essence in the notes.
God is Spirit, God is Life, God is Perfect, God is Unique, God is Eternal.
7.16What does 'God is Spirit' mean?
God is immaterial and invisible, not composed of matter, and must be worshiped in spirit and truth (John 4:24).
7.17What is the difference between God's incommunicable and communicable attributes?
Incommunicable attributes belong to God alone (e.g. omnipresence, eternality); communicable attributes are shared with humans in limited measure (e.g. love, holiness, justice).
7.18Name three moral/relational attributes of God.
Holiness, love, justice/righteousness, mercy, grace, faithfulness, and truth.
7.19Why does a wrong view of God matter practically?
Our understanding of God shapes worship, prayer, salvation, and relationships — a confused view of God corrupts all of them.
7.20What is the biblical corrective to all human approaches to truth?
God's self-revelation in Scripture — truth is what God has revealed, not what man reasons, feels, or finds useful.

The Trinity — 22 Questions

7.1Does the word 'Trinity' appear in the Bible?
No — but the reality it describes is revealed throughout Scripture.
7.2Define the Trinity.
There is one true God who eternally exists as three distinct persons — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — each fully God, sharing one divine nature.
7.3Why does the Trinity feel difficult?
God is infinite and we are finite; we think in created categories — Scripture gives true revelation, not exhaustive explanation.
7.4Is mystery the same as contradiction?
No — mystery does not mean contradiction, and limited understanding does not mean false doctrine.
7.5State the fourfold orthodox emphasis of the Trinity.
Oneness of essence, threeness of persons, functional order (distinct roles without inequality), and eternality of all three.
7.6Is God three beings, three wills, or three powers?
No — God is one being, with one will, one power, and one glory; the threeness is of persons, not essence.
7.7How is the Trinity seen in creation?
The Father initiates (Genesis 1:1), the Son creates life (John 1:3), the Spirit orders and sustains (Genesis 1:2) — one work of one God.
7.8How is the Trinity seen in redemption?
The Father plans, the Son accomplishes, the Spirit applies.
7.9Give one verse each for the deity of the Father, Son, and Spirit.
Father: John 6:27. Son: Hebrews 1:8. Spirit: Acts 5:3–4.
7.10Which two texts present the three persons together?
The baptismal formula (Matthew 28:19) and the apostolic blessing (2 Corinthians 13:14).
7.11Did God become triune at some point?
No — God did not become triune; God has always been triune. The persons never came into being and were never without one another.
7.12Why are Father, Son, and Spirit relationships and not roles?
A role can change; a relationship defines who someone is — the Father has always been Father, the Son always Son, the Spirit always Spirit.
7.13Does the Son's obedience imply inferiority?
No — obedience reflects relationship, not rank; all three persons are equal in essence, glory, and power.
7.14How does Genesis 1 hint at the Trinity?
Elohim is plural in form yet singular in action — 'Let us make mankind' (plural deliberation), 'So God created' (singular action).
7.15Name two other Old Testament anticipations of the Trinity.
Genesis 3:22 and 11:7; Isaiah 6:8; Daniel 7 (Ancient of Days and Son of Man); Psalm 110:1.
7.16Where does the New Testament reveal the Trinity most clearly?
Jesus' baptism (Matthew 3:16–17), the Great Commission (Matthew 28:19), John 1:1,14, and 2 Corinthians 13:14.
7.17What is Unitarianism?
The heresy that denies the Trinity, rejecting the full deity of the Son and the Spirit.
7.18What is Modalism?
The heresy that God is one person wearing three masks — denying real relationships within God.
7.19What is Tritheism?
The heresy of three separate gods — denying biblical monotheism.
7.20What three phrases in the Nicene Creed safeguard Christ's deity?
'God from God,' 'begotten, not made,' and 'of one being (homoousios) with the Father.'
7.21Why does the atonement depend on the Trinity?
Because atonement depends on Christ's full deity — salvation must be God's work, not a creature's.
7.22Give one practical implication of the Trinity.
God is personal and relational in Himself; unity and diversity find their source in God; church life reflects triune unity.

Angelology — 33 Questions

8.1What does Angelology study, and from what Greek word does it come?
The systematic biblical study of angels, both holy and fallen — from angelos, meaning 'messenger.'
8.2Why is a correct understanding of angels important?
It prevents angel worship (Colossians 2:18) and fear-driven superstition, and guards sound doctrine.
8.3Give two Old Testament evidences for angels.
They guarded Eden (Genesis 3:24) and ministered to Hagar (Genesis 16:7); they appear throughout Israel's history.
8.4Give two New Testament evidences for angels.
They announced the births of John and Jesus (Luke 1:11–38) and ministered to Christ after His temptation (Matthew 4:11).
8.5What did Jesus teach about angels?
They are real beings who worship God (Matthew 18:10) and do not marry (Matthew 22:30).
8.6Are angels eternal beings?
No — they are created beings, made by Christ and for Christ (Colossians 1:16; Psalm 148:2,5).
8.7When were angels created?
Before humanity — they were present at the foundation of the earth (Job 38:4–7).
8.8How many angels are there?
Innumerable (Daniel 7:10; Hebrews 12:22; Revelation 5:11).
8.9Were angels created good or evil?
All were created good (Genesis 1:31); the fall of some came from misuse of will, not a defect in creation.
8.10What are angels in essence?
Ministering spirits (Hebrews 1:14) — spirit beings who can nevertheless appear visibly.
8.11Give three evidences of angelic personality.
Intellect (1 Peter 1:12), emotions (Luke 15:10), and will (Jude 6).
8.12How powerful are angels? Give an example.
They excel in strength (Psalm 103:20) — one angel destroyed 185,000 Assyrians (2 Kings 19:35); yet they lack omnipotence.
8.13Do humans become angels after death?
No — angels do not die (Luke 20:36) and do not marry; humans and angels are distinct orders of creation.
8.14Who is the archangel?
Michael (Jude 9; 1 Thessalonians 4:16), a defender of God's people.
8.15What is the ministry of the cherubim?
Guarding God's holiness (Genesis 3:24; Ezekiel 10), closely connected with God's throne.
8.16What is the ministry of the seraphim?
Proclaiming God's holiness in worship — 'Holy, holy, holy' (Isaiah 6:1–7).
8.17Who are the 'living creatures'?
Beings around God's throne in ceaseless praise (Revelation 4:6–8).
8.18Who are the elect angels?
Holy angels who did not fall and remain faithful servants (1 Timothy 5:21).
8.19Name three ministries of holy angels to believers.
They minister to heirs of salvation (Hebrews 1:14), protect (Psalm 34:7), and deliver (Acts 12:7–11).
8.20How did angels minister to Christ?
They announced His birth (Luke 2:8–14), strengthened Him (Luke 22:43), and will accompany His return (Matthew 25:31).
8.21What role do angels play in judgment?
They execute divine judgment (Genesis 19:13; Revelation 8–9).
8.22What was Satan's original position?
A high-ranking created being, exalted before his fall (Ezekiel 28:12–15; Isaiah 14:12–15).
8.23What caused Satan's fall?
Pride and self-exaltation (1 Timothy 3:6) — he desired equality with God (Isaiah 14:13–14).
8.24What was the result of Satan's fall?
He was cast down (Luke 10:18; Revelation 12:7–9), and his authority became corrupt and adversarial.
8.25How does Satan work against unbelievers?
He blinds their minds (2 Corinthians 4:4) and deceives the nations (Revelation 20:3).
8.26How does Satan work against believers?
He accuses (Revelation 12:10), tempts (1 Thessalonians 3:5), and seeks to devour (1 Peter 5:8).
8.27Who are demons?
Fallen angels who followed Satan in rebellion (Revelation 12:4; Jude 6).
8.28What limits demonic activity?
They operate only within God's permission (Job 1:12), and Christ has triumphed over them (Colossians 2:15).
8.29List four pieces of the armor of God.
Truth, righteousness, faith, salvation, the Word of God, and prayer (Ephesians 6:10–18).
8.30What is the biblical formula for resisting the devil?
Submit to God and resist the devil, and he will flee (James 4:7; 1 Peter 5:9).
8.31How did Christ model victory over Satan?
Through Scripture — answering each temptation with 'It is written' (Matthew 4:4, 7, 10).
8.32What is Satan's final destiny?
Temporary binding (Revelation 20:1–3), then the lake of fire forever (Revelation 20:10); fallen angels are reserved for judgment (Matthew 25:41).
8.33What roles do angels play in eschatology?
They accompany Christ at His return (Matthew 25:31), gather the elect (Matthew 24:31), and separate the wicked (Matthew 13:39–41).

Anthropology — 35 Questions

9.1What is theological anthropology?
The doctrine of man — the study of humanity's origin, nature, purpose, problem, and destiny as revealed by God.
9.2Why can anthropology not be separated from cosmology?
Because our understanding of man depends on our understanding of the universe's origin — worldview about the cosmos shapes worldview about man.
9.3List the six fundamental questions of human existence.
Origin (where did we come from?), Nature (what are we?), Purpose (why were we created?), Problem (what went wrong?), Destiny (what is our future?), Meaning (where is fulfillment?).
9.4What are the two foundational worldviews on human origin?
The atheistic/naturalistic view and the theistic (biblical) view.
9.5What is abiogenesis?
The naturalistic claim that life arose from non-life — 'life from non-living matter.'
9.6What is the central problem of abiogenesis?
Explaining the origin of the information in life — matter and chance cannot account for coded genetic information.
9.7Name two challenges to evolution mentioned in the notes.
The origin of information (mutations alter but do not create information), the absence of transitional forms, and human consciousness.
9.8What is the foundation of the biblical view of man's origin?
Revelation — God speaks about man; man does not merely speculate upward (Genesis 1:1).
9.9Why does the doctrine of creation matter?
It is not merely about how the world began — it forms the foundation of all theology: God's ownership, man's identity, and purpose.
9.10Give three affirmations about creation from Genesis 1.
Creation has a beginning, creation is intentional (designed), and creation is dependent on God for its existence.
9.11How did God create according to Genesis 1?
By divine word — He spoke and it was so, demonstrating His power and authority.
9.12What makes the creation of man special?
Unlike the rest of creation, man is formed uniquely — personally fashioned by God and made in His image (Genesis 2:7; 1:26–27).
9.13Name the non-negotiable truths of the evangelical doctrine of creation.
Creation by divine act, creation by divine word, a historical Adam, death through sin (Romans 5:12), and Christ as the second Adam.
9.14Why is a historical Adam theologically necessary?
Because Paul's doctrine of sin and salvation rests on the parallel of Adam and Christ (Romans 5:12; 1 Corinthians 15).
9.15Can science alone explain life?
No — a living cell is more than chemicals; life must ultimately be understood through God's revelation as God-given.
9.16What is the Imago Dei?
The image of God — the central truth of biblical anthropology: man is created in God's image and likeness (Genesis 1:26–27).
9.17Why is the Imago Dei the foundation of human dignity?
Every human bears God's image, so every human life has worth regardless of ability, status, or condition.
9.18Was the image of God lost at the fall?
No — it was distorted and defaced, but not erased; fallen man still bears God's image.
9.19Who is the perfect image of God?
Jesus Christ — 'the image of the invisible God' (Colossians 1:15).
9.20How is the image restored in salvation?
Salvation is not only forgiveness — believers are progressively renewed and conformed to the image of Christ.
9.21What is NOT the image of God?
It is not a physical likeness of God's body — God is Spirit; the image concerns spiritual, moral, relational, and functional likeness.
9.22Is gender created by God or constructed by culture?
Created by God — 'male and female He created them' (Genesis 1:27); a creational reality, not a social construct.
9.23Are male and female equal?
Yes — equal in the image of God, equal in dignity and worth, while complementary in design.
9.24What is complementarity?
Male and female are created different, not deficient — designed differences that serve unity, marriage, and God's purposes.
9.25What institution expresses the male–female relational design?
Marriage — a divine institution of covenant and community, reflecting relational aspects of the triune God.
9.26What are the two essential aspects of man's constitution in Genesis 2:7?
The material (body, formed from dust) and the immaterial (spirit/soul, breathed by God).
9.27What is the dichotomy view of man?
That man consists of two parts — material (body) and immaterial (soul/spirit, used interchangeably).
9.28What is the trichotomy view of man?
That man consists of three parts — body, soul, and spirit (citing 1 Thessalonians 5:23; Hebrews 4:12).
9.29Why does the constitution of man matter?
Sin affects the whole person, and salvation redeems the whole person — guarding against errors that despise the body or ignore the soul.
9.30What is the primary purpose of man?
To glorify God and enjoy fellowship with Him.
9.31How did sin distort human purpose?
It shifted life from God-centered to self-centered — seeking meaning in self rather than the Creator.
9.32What are the noetic effects of sin?
Sin affects thinking — the mind is darkened and reasoning corrupted, so man suppresses and distorts truth.
9.33How does Christ restore man's purpose?
As the second Adam He restores what was lost — reconciling man to God and renewing him for God's glory.
9.34Name the three stages of restoration.
Justification (legal restoration), sanctification (progressive renewal), glorification (final perfection).
9.35In what three stages can human history be summarized?
Creation, fall, and redemption/restoration.

Soteriology — 49 Questions

10.1What is Soteriology?
The doctrine of salvation — the study of God's rescue of lost humanity through Jesus Christ.
10.2What is the foundational premise of salvation?
That humanity is fundamentally lost — condemned, alienated, and unable to save itself.
10.3What is 'the problem of righteousness'?
God requires perfect righteousness, and man has none — an insurmountable moral and legal crisis apart from Christ.
10.4What is meant by man's inability?
Because of total depravity, man has an absolute lack of spiritual capacity to save himself — the bondage of the will and spiritual death.
10.5Name the four human attempts at self-salvation.
By being good (relative morality), by doing good (legalism), because of who they are (heritage/ritual), and because of what they know (intellectualism).
10.6What is the divine verdict on self-righteousness?
Every human attempt to bridge the chasm between sinner and holy God ends in failure — righteousness cannot be earned.
10.7What is the ultimate purpose of salvation?
The glory of God — our rescue is an act of love, but its highest end is God's glory.
10.8Where does the initiative of salvation begin?
With God seeking man, not man seeking God — seen from the garden ('Where are you?') through the patriarchs and prophets.
10.9Define grace.
Unmerited favor — God's kindness given to those who deserve the opposite; the foundation-principle of salvation (charis).
10.10What is the motivation and foundation of salvation?
God's love (John 3:16) and grace — no other reason is given for a holy God seeking rebellious creatures.
10.11What is the basis of salvation?
The substitutionary atoning work of Jesus Christ — the legal and moral ground on which God saves.
10.12What is the means of salvation?
Faith and repentance — conversion; faith is the instrument (not the cause), the 'hand' that receives salvation.
10.13What is repentance (metanoia)?
A change of mind, heart, and direction — turning from sin toward God; the other side of the coin of faith.
10.14Why can no one boast about faith?
Because salvation, including faith itself, is a gift of God, not a work (Ephesians 2:8–9).
10.15What are the three dimensions of the Spirit's conviction (John 16)?
Concerning sin (v.9 — unbelief), righteousness (v.10 — God's standard in Christ), and judgment (v.11 — Satan's defeat and coming judgment).
10.16Define regeneration.
The divine, supernatural act of imparting new spiritual life — the new birth (palingenesia), wholly God's work (monergistic).
10.17What does John 1:12–13 teach about the new birth?
True spiritual rebirth does not originate in human lineage, desire, or decision — it is the sovereign work of God.
10.18What is the indwelling of the Spirit?
The Spirit's permanent presence in the believer — the body becomes the temple of the Holy Spirit.
10.19What is the sealing of the Spirit for?
It is God's guarantee and legal seal of salvation — securing the believer's inheritance.
10.20What tension does the atonement resolve in God's character?
How God can be both perfectly just (punishing sin) and lovingly merciful (forgiving sinners) — resolved at the cross.
10.21Define substitutionary (vicarious) atonement.
Christ, the God-man, acted in the sinner's place, bearing the penalty sinners deserved.
10.22What principle did the OT sacrificial system establish?
Life for life — the penalty of sin is death, and atonement requires a substitute's blood.
10.23What is Yom Kippur, and why does it matter for the atonement?
The Day of Atonement (Leviticus 16) — the apex of the OT picture of atonement, fulfilled in Christ's once-for-all sacrifice.
10.24Define propitiation.
The appeasement of divine wrath — temple imagery: God's righteous anger against sin is satisfied by Christ's sacrifice.
10.25Define expiation.
The wiping away, cleansing, and total removal of sin and its guilt.
10.26Define redemption.
Buying back, liberating, or rescuing from bondage — marketplace imagery: Christ paid the ransom to free slaves of sin.
10.27Define reconciliation.
The total restoration of a broken relationship — peace with God replacing enmity.
10.28What is the 'limited/definite atonement' view?
The Reformed/Calvinistic view that the atonement was designed specifically to secure the salvation of the elect.
10.29What is the 'unlimited/general atonement' view?
The Arminian/general evangelical view that Christ died provisionally for all people, applied to those who believe.
10.30On what do both views of the atonement's extent agree?
That Christ's death is sufficient for all and that salvation is applied only through faith in Him.
10.31What two parallel truths frame the doctrine of election?
God's absolute sovereignty in election, and man's genuine responsibility and free will — both taught in Scripture.
10.32Define election.
God's eternal, pre-temporal choice — His sovereign purpose to save, made before the foundation of the world, not a reaction to history.
10.33What does 'unconditional' mean in election?
God's choice is not based on foreseen human merit — it eliminates comparative righteousness and highlights mercy, not effort.
10.34What is the conditional (Arminian) view of election?
That God's choice is based on His foreknowledge of who would freely believe.
10.35What is the unconditional (Calvinistic) view of election?
That God's choice is independent of any foreseen human action, grounded solely in His sovereign good pleasure.
10.36Which two key passages anchor the doctrine of election?
Ephesians 1:3–11 ('chose us before the foundation of the world') and Romans 9 (God's sovereign mercy).
10.37Name three pastoral purposes of the doctrine of election.
To produce deep humility, to provide absolute security, and to motivate bold evangelism.
10.38List the main results of salvation covered in the notes.
Regeneration, justification, forgiveness of sins, adoption, sanctification, reconciliation, and eternal life.
10.39Define justification.
God's forensic (legal) declaration that the believing sinner is righteous — a change of standing, not of nature.
10.40What is imputation in justification?
Christ's righteousness is credited to the believer's account, just as our sin was credited to Christ (Romans 4; 2 Corinthians 5:21).
10.41Who is the theological model of justification by faith?
Abraham — 'he believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness' (Romans 4:1–5).
10.42What is forgiveness of sins?
The cancellation of the sinner's debt — the legal and relational debt of rebellion is fully pardoned (Acts 10:43).
10.43Define adoption.
God's legal act of receiving believers into His family as legitimate sons and heirs — from children of wrath to children of God.
10.44What are the three tenses of sanctification?
Positional (set apart at salvation), progressive (ongoing growth in holiness), and ultimate/final (perfected at glorification).
10.45How does justification differ from sanctification?
Justification is an instant legal declaration fixing our standing; sanctification is the ongoing process transforming our living.
10.46What does Romans 5:1 say reconciliation gives us?
Peace with God — objective peace grounded in our corrected legal standing through Christ.
10.47Is eternal life only future?
No — it is a present possession from the moment of faith and a guaranteed future reality (John 3:36; Romans 6:23).
10.48What is the 'golden chain' of salvation?
The unbreakable sequence of God's saving purpose — foreknown, predestined, called, justified, glorified (Romans 8:29–30).
10.49Which persons of the Trinity do what in salvation?
The Father initiates and plans, the Son accomplishes redemption, and the Holy Spirit applies it.
